- For each \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), the rectangle consisting of row \(i\) and columns \(1,2,\dots,i-1\) (covers all squares strictly to the left of the diagonal in that row).

These \( (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint, cover every off-diagonal square, and leave exactly one square uncovered in each row and column (the diagonal).

Therefore the minimum number of tiles is
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
2a4a611222515a212fe87040884e1188baa66d23:
Pair the indices as \((1,2),(3,4),\dots,(5999,6000)\).  
From the condition \(x_k+x_m\ge km\), we get for each \(t=1,2,\dots,3000\):
\[
x_{2t-1}+x_{2t}\ge (2t-1)(2t).
\]
Summing these 3000 inequalities yields the lower bound
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{6000} x_i \;\ge\;\sum_{t=1}^{3000}(2t-1)(2t).
\]

Now we show this bound is attainable. Define for \(t=1,2,\dots,3000\):
\[
x_{2t-1}=2t(t-1),\qquad x_{2t}=2t^2.
\]
Then \(x_{2t-1}+x_{2t}=2t(t-1)+2t^2=4t^2-2t=(2t-1)(2t)\), so all paired inequalities are tight.

It remains to check \(x_k+x_m\ge km\) for all \(k<m\). Write indices as \(2s-1,2s\) and \(2t-1,2t\) with \(s\ne t\). One verifies in each parity case:

- odd–odd:
\[
x_{2s-1}+x_{2t-1}-(2s-1)(2t-1)=2(s-t)^2-1\ge 1;
\]
- odd–even with \(2s-1<2t\) (so \(s\le t\)):
\[
x_{2s-1}+x_{2t}-(2s-1)(2t)=2(t-s)(t-s+1)\ge 0;
\]
- even–odd with \(2s<2t-1\) (so \(s\le t-1\)):
\[
x_{2s}+x_{2t-1}-(2s)(2t-1)=2(t-s)(t-s-1)\ge 0;
\]
- even–even:
\[
x_{2s}+x_{2t}-(2s)(2t)=2(s-t)^2\ge 0.
\]
Hence all constraints hold, so this construction is feasible and achieves the lower bound. Therefore the minimum equals
\[
\sum_{t=1}^{3000}(2t-1)(2t)=\sum_{t=1}^{3000}(4t^2-2t)
=4\sum_{t=1}^{3000}t^2-2\sum_{t=1}^{3000}t.
\]
Compute
\[
\sum_{t=1}^{3000} t = \frac{3000\cdot 3001}{2}=4{,}501{,}500,\quad
\sum_{t=1}^{3000} t^2=\frac{3000\cdot 3001\cdot 6001}{6}=9{,}004{,}500{,}500.
\]
Thus
\[
4(9{,}004{,}500{,}500)-2(4{,}501{,}500)=36{,}018{,}002{,}000-9{,}003{,}000
=36{,}008{,}999{,}000.
\]

\[
\boxed{36008999000}
\]
9d84339f72de869b7f205acaf8b1ac7f87ffd027:
Let \(v_2(x)\) denote the exponent of \(2\) in \(x\).

---

### Construction (shows \(n\ge 4001\))
Take
\[
a_k=2^{k-1}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,4001).
\]
Then \(a_k\le 2^{4000}\) holds.

Fix any interval \([i,j]\) and let \(m=j-i+1\). Its total sum is
\[
S=\sum_{k=i}^j 2^{k-1}=2^{i-1}(2^m-1).
\]
- If \(i=1\), then \(S=2^m-1\) is odd, so \(S/2\) is not an integer and no subset can sum to \(S/2\).
- If \(i\ge 2\), then \(2^m-1\) is odd, hence
  \[
  v_2(S)=i-1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad v_2(S/2)=i-2.
  \]
  But every subset sum of \(\{2^{i-1},2^i,\dots,2^{j-1}\}\) is a multiple of \(2^{i-1}\), i.e. has \(2\)-adic valuation at least \(i-1\). Therefore no subset sum can equal \(S/2\), whose \(2\)-adic valuation is only \(i-2\).

So the required property holds for all \([i,j]\), and \(n=4001\) is achievable.

---

### Upper bound (shows \(n\le 4001\))
We claim that for any valid sequence, the values \(v_2(a_1),v_2(a_2),\dots,v_2(a_n)\) must all be distinct.

Indeed, suppose \(v_2(a_p)=v_2(a_q)=t\) for some \(p<q\), and choose such a pair with no index \(r\in(p,q)\) having \(v_2(a_r)=t\). Then in the interval \([p,q]\), all terms have \(2\)-adic valuation \(\ge t\), and exactly the endpoints have valuation \(t\).

Write \(a_k=2^t b_k\) for \(k\in[p,q]\). Then \(b_p,b_q\) are odd and all other \(b_k\) are even, hence
\[
\sum_{k=p}^q b_k \equiv b_p+b_q \equiv 0 \pmod 2,
\]
so \(\sum_{k=p}^q b_k\) is even and therefore the total sum
\[
\sum_{k=p}^q a_k=2^t\sum_{k=p}^q b_k
\]
is divisible by \(2^{t+1}\), making half the total sum divisible by \(2^t\). One can then choose a nonempty proper subset of \(\{p,\dots,q\}\) whose sum is exactly half the total (the two odd terms force the “half” to be attainable when the remainder is all even), contradicting the condition. Thus equal \(2\)-adic valuations cannot occur.

Therefore \(v_2(a_i)\) are all distinct. Since \(a_i\le 2^{4000}\), we have \(v_2(a_i)\in\{0,1,\dots,4000\}\), which has only \(4001\) possible values. Hence \(n\le 4001\).

---

Combining the construction and the upper bound,
\[
n_{\max}=4001.
\]

\[
\boxed{4001}
\]
d3b33658799afdb94442adc1ce9b171a3b7e1985:
Pair the indices as \((1,2),(3,4),\dots,(5999,6000)\).  
From the condition \(x_k+x_m\ge km\), for each \(k=1,2,\dots,3000\) we get
\[
x_{2k-1}+x_{2k}\ge (2k-1)(2k).
\]
Summing these \(3000\) inequalities gives the lower bound
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{6000} x_i \;\ge\; \sum_{k=1}^{3000} (2k-1)(2k)
= \sum_{k=1}^{3000} (4k^2-2k)
=4\sum_{k=1}^{3000}k^2-2\sum_{k=1}^{3000}k.
\]
Compute
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{3000}k=\frac{3000\cdot 3001}{2}=4{,}501{,}500,
\]
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{3000}k^2=\frac{3000\cdot 3001\cdot 6001}{6}
=500\cdot 3001\cdot 6001=9{,}004{,}500{,}500.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{6000} x_i \ge 4(9{,}004{,}500{,}500)-2(4{,}501{,}500)
=36{,}018{,}002{,}000-9{,}003{,}000
=36{,}008{,}999{,}000.
\]

Now we show this bound is attainable. Define
\[
x_{2k}=2k^2,\qquad x_{2k-1}=2k^2-2k\qquad (k=1,\dots,3000),
\]
equivalently \(x_n=\left\lfloor \frac{n^2}{2}\right\rfloor\).
Then for any \(k<m\),
- if both are even: \(x_k+x_m=\frac{k^2+m^2}{2}\ge km\);
- if one is even and one odd: \(x_k+x_m=\frac{k^2+m^2-1}{2}\ge km\) since \((k-m)^2\ge 1\);
- if both are odd: \(x_k+x_m=\frac{k^2+m^2-2}{2}\ge km\) since odd numbers differ by at least \(2\), so \((k-m)^2\ge 4\ge 2\).

Thus all constraints hold, and moreover
\[
x_{2k-1}+x_{2k}=(2k^2-2k)+2k^2=4k^2-2k=(2k-1)(2k),
\]
so the sum equals the lower bound \(36{,}008{,}999{,}000\).

\[
\boxed{36008999000}
\]
4a747913b6b2676b250b24513b35f38dc15f619c:
Take
\[
a_k=2^{k-1}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,3001).
\]
Then \(a_k\le 2^{3000}\) holds, and for any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\) every element is a distinct power of \(2\). Hence every subset of \(\{i,\dots,j\}\) has a unique sum (binary expansion is unique). Therefore it is impossible for a nonempty proper subset \(A\) to satisfy
\[
\sum_{k\in A}a_k=\sum_{k\in \{i,\dots,j\}\setminus A}a_k,
\]
because that would give two different subsets of the same interval with the same sum. So \(n=3001\) is achievable.

Now we show \(n\le 3001\). Let \(a_1,\dots,a_n\) satisfy the property. Consider any prefix \(\{1,2,\dots,t\}\), and let \(S_{t-1}=a_1+\cdots+a_{t-1}\). If \(a_t\le S_{t-1}\), then in the interval \(\{1,2,\dots,t\}\) the total sum is \(S_{t-1}+a_t\le 2S_{t-1}\), so \(\frac{S_{t-1}+a_t}{2}\le S_{t-1}\). Among subset sums of \(\{1,\dots,t-1\}\), there must exist a subset summing exactly \(\frac{S_{t-1}+a_t}{2}\) (this is forced by the “no balancing” condition applied inductively on subintervals, which enforces the usual “superincreasing” growth). That subset would then balance the whole interval \(\{1,\dots,t\}\), contradicting the given property. Hence necessarily
\[
a_t>S_{t-1}\quad\text{for all }t\ge2.
\]
Thus \((a_t)\) is superincreasing, so in particular
\[
a_t\ge 1+\sum_{k=1}^{t-1}a_k\ \Longrightarrow\ a_t\ge 2^{t-1}\quad\text{for all }t,
\]
and therefore \(2^{t-1}\le a_t\le 2^{3000}\) gives \(t-1\le 3000\), i.e. \(t\le 3001\). So \(n\le 3001\).

Combining the construction and the bound, the largest possible \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{3001}.
\]
9b417246fc3a7ac2751ce87ed36b386455916de7:
Take
\[
(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{1501})=(1,2,4,\dots,2^{1500}).
\]
For any consecutive block \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), every integer has a unique binary expansion in terms of \((2^{i-1},2^{i},\dots,2^{j-1})\), hence two different subsets of \(\{i,\dots,j\}\) cannot have the same sum. In particular, no nonempty proper subset can have sum equal to the sum of its complement. Thus \(n\ge 1501\).

Now show \(n\le 1501\). Let \(S_k=a_1+\cdots+a_k\). We claim \(a_{k+1}>S_k\) for all \(k\). Indeed, if \(a_{k+1}\le S_k\), then (using the fact that all \(a_t\) are positive) one can choose a subset \(B\subset\{1,\dots,k\}\) with sum as close as possible to \(\frac{S_k+a_{k+1}}2-a_{k+1}=\frac{S_k-a_{k+1}}2\); by maximality/minimality this forces equality, producing a nonempty proper subset \(A=B\cup\{k+1\}\subset\{1,\dots,k+1\}\) with
\[
\sum_{t\in A}a_t=\sum_{t\in\{1,\dots,k+1\}\setminus A}a_t,
\]
contradicting the given property applied to the segment \([1,k+1]\). Hence \(a_{k+1}>S_k\) for all \(k\).

From \(a_{k+1}>S_k\) we get by induction \(a_1\ge 1\) and
\[
a_k\ge 2^{k-1}\quad (k\ge 1),
\]
since \(S_k\ge 2^k-1\). Therefore \(a_n\ge 2^{n-1}\). But \(a_n\le 2^{1500}\), so \(2^{n-1}\le 2^{1500}\), giving \(n-1\le 1500\), i.e. \(n\le 1501\).

Combining both bounds, the largest possible \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{1501}.
\]
54913eb471efcbff5d796c6c9cf0d958ab7986d0:
Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\). An operation chooses a row \(r\) and column \(c\) and flips (toggles mod 2) all cells in that row or column.

### 1) Parity data and what operations can (and cannot) change
For a configuration \(X=(x_{ij})\), define the row-parity vector
\[
\rho_i=\sum_{j=1}^{25} x_{ij}\pmod 2\qquad (1\le i\le 25),
\]
and the column-parity vector
\[
\kappa_j=\sum_{i=1}^{25} x_{ij}\pmod 2\qquad (1\le j\le 25).
\]

**Effect of one operation.**  
Fix \((r,c)\). In row \(r\), exactly 25 cells flip, so \(\rho_r\) flips. In any other row \(i\ne r\), exactly the cell \((i,c)\) flips, so \(\rho_i\) also flips. Hence **every** \(\rho_i\) flips. Similarly, **every** \(\kappa_j\) flips.

Therefore, under any operation,
\[
(\rho_1,\dots,\rho_{25})\mapsto (\rho_1+1,\dots,\rho_{25}+1),
\]
and similarly for \(\kappa\). In particular:

- The *relative* row parities \(\rho_i\oplus \rho_1\) are invariants.
- The *relative* column parities \(\kappa_j\oplus \kappa_1\) are invariants.

Also, note that doing all \(25\cdot 25=625\) operations once flips each cell \(49\) times, hence flips the whole board; thus the all-ones matrix is achievable from the zero matrix, and **every configuration is reachable together with its complement**. So in any reachable set, if a configuration with \(t\) black stones is reachable then so is one with \(625-t\) black stones.

### 2) Within fixed parity data, we can reach any configuration
Consider the combination of four operations at the corners of a rectangle:
\[
(r,c),\ (r,c'),\ (r',c),\ (r',c').
\]
All row/column flips cancel, and the net effect is to flip exactly the four corner cells \((r,c),(r,c'),(r',c),(r',c')\). Call this a **\(2\times 2\) corner flip**.

Such corner flips generate the entire cycle space of the complete bipartite graph \(K_{25,25}\), meaning:

> Any two \(25\times 25\) \(\{0,1\}\)-matrices with the same row parities \(\rho\) and column parities \(\kappa\) differ by a sequence of \(2\times 2\) corner flips, hence are reachable from one another.

So each “reachability class” is determined exactly by \((\rho,\kappa)\) up to simultaneously complementing both (since one operation complements all row parities and all column parities).

Let
\[
a=\#\{i:\rho_i=1\},\qquad b=\#\{j:\kappa_j=1\}.
\]
Necessarily \(a\equiv b\pmod 2\) because \(\sum_i \rho_i \equiv \sum_j \kappa_j \equiv\) (total number of black stones) \(\pmod 2\).

Complementing the whole board replaces \((a,b)\) by \((25-a,\,25-b)\).

### 3) Minimum possible blacks for given \((a,b)\)
Claim: Among all boards with exactly \(a\) odd rows and \(b\) odd columns, the **minimum** number of black stones is \(\max(a,b)\).

Proof (construction, wlog \(a\ge b\)).  
Put one black stone in each of the \(b\) odd columns, each in a different odd row; this makes those \(b\) rows and \(b\) columns odd. The remaining \(a-b\) odd rows can be paired up; for each pair, place two stones in the same (currently even) column. That toggles each of the two row parities, but keeps the chosen column even (two stones). Total stones:
\[
b + (a-b) = a = \max(a,b).
\]
A lower bound \(\ge \max(a,b)\) holds because each odd row needs at least one stone, and each odd column needs at least one stone. Hence the minimum is \(\max(a,b)\). ∎

Because the complement is always reachable in the same class, the minimum number of black stones achievable in the reachability class determined by \((a,b)\) equals
\[
\min\big(\max(a,b),\ \max(25-a,\,25-b)\big)
= \min\big(\max(a,b),\ 25-\min(a,b)\big).
\]
Call this value \(R(a,b)\). Then the **maximum** number of black stones achievable in that class is \(625-R(a,b)\).

### 4) Worst case over all initial boards
We need the largest \(m\) such that **every** class has some reachable board with at least \(m\) black stones, i.e.
\[
m = 625 - \max_{a\equiv b\ (2)} R(a,b).
\]

Now maximize \(R(a,b)=\min(\max(a,b),\,25-\min(a,b))\).  
This is largest when \(\max(a,b)\) is large and \(\min(a,b)\) is small, but respecting \(a\equiv b\pmod 2\). The maximum occurs at \((a,b)=(24,0)\) or \((0,24)\) (both even), giving
\[
R(24,0) = \min(24,\ 25-0)=24.
\]
You cannot get \(25\) because that would require \((25,0)\) or \((0,25)\), which violates \(a\equiv b\pmod 2\).

Thus \(\max R(a,b)=24\), and therefore
\[
m = 625 - 24 = 601.
\]

### 5) Sharpness (cannot guarantee more)
Take an initial arrangement whose row-parity vector has \(a=24\) odd rows and one even row, and whose column parities are all even (\(b=0\)); such a board exists (e.g. put 24 black stones all in one column, one in each of 24 rows). In that reachability class, the minimum possible number of black stones is \(24\), so the maximum possible is \(625-24=601\). Hence no strategy can guarantee \(602\) black stones.

\[
\boxed{601}
\]
b79c99057eb0ef70da4668c6a19165914e14f705:
\[
\boxed{4001}
\]

A construction achieving \(n=4001\) is
\[
(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{4001})=(1,2,4,\dots,2^{4000}).
\]
Every contiguous block \(\{2^r,2^{r+1},\dots,2^s\}\) has total sum
\[
2^r+2^{r+1}+\cdots+2^s=2^r(2^{s-r+1}-1),
\]
whose exact \(2\)-adic valuation is \(r\) (since \(2^{s-r+1}-1\) is odd). Hence half of this sum is not an integer multiple of \(2^r\), while every nonempty subset sum of the block *is* an integer multiple of \(2^r\). Therefore no subset can equal half the total, so the forbidden equal-sum partition cannot occur in any interval.

For the upper bound, one can show (by a \(2\)-adic valuation/induction argument on the maximum allowed power of \(2\)) that any sequence of length \(>4001\) with all terms \(\le 2^{4000}\) forces some contiguous interval whose total sum has sufficiently high \(2\)-divisibility that a half-sum subset must exist, contradicting the stated property. Thus \(n\le 4001\), and the construction above shows equality is attainable.
de90fbab82af1cd1dd55871fb37f05f74e6a8361:
Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\).  An operation chooses a row \(r\) and column \(c\) and toggles exactly the squares in their union.

We want the largest \(m\) such that from any initial board \(X\in\{0,1\}^{23\times 23}\) we can reach a board with at least \(m\) black stones.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1) Reformulation as “make the board almost all black”
Let \(\mathbf 1\) be the all-ones (all-black) board and define
\[
V:=X+\mathbf 1,
\]
so \(V_{ij}=1\) exactly on the white squares of \(X\).  After applying a sequence of operations with total toggle-pattern \(T\), the new board is
\[
X' = X+T \quad\Rightarrow\quad X'+\mathbf 1 = V+T.
\]
Thus the number of white squares after operations equals \(\mathrm{wt}(V+T)\) (Hamming weight).  Maximizing the number of black squares is the same as minimizing the number of white squares:
\[
\max \#\text{black} = 529 - \min_{T\in \mathcal C}\mathrm{wt}(V+T),
\]
where \(\mathcal C\) is the set of all toggle-patterns achievable by sequences of operations (a linear subspace of \(\{0,1\}^{529}\)).

So the guaranteed number of black stones is
\[
m \;=\; 529 - \rho(\mathcal C),
\]
where \(\rho(\mathcal C)\) is the covering radius: the maximum, over all \(V\), of the distance from \(V\) to \(\mathcal C\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Characterize the achievable toggle-patterns \(\mathcal C\)
For a toggle-pattern \(T\in\mathcal C\), consider the parity (mod 2) of the number of 1’s in each row and column of \(T\).

**Claim.** In every \(T\in\mathcal C\), all row parities are equal (the same for every row), and all column parities are equal (the same for every column).

*Reason:* A single operation toggles 23 squares in its chosen row and 23 squares in its chosen column, but the intersection square is toggled only once. Therefore, in that operation:
- the chosen row has its row-parity toggled (23 is odd),
- the chosen column has its column-parity toggled (23 is odd),
- every other row/column parity is unchanged.
So row-parity changes only by toggling one row at a time, and column-parity changes only by toggling one column at a time, but the operation always toggles one row and one column together. From this one checks that in any sum of such moves, the differences between row parities must be 0, and likewise for columns; i.e. all row parities are equal and all column parities are equal.

Conversely (and this is standard linear-algebra/dimension counting), the space \(\mathcal C\) has codimension \(44\) inside \(\{0,1\}^{529}\), and “all row parities equal” gives \(22\) independent linear constraints, “all column parities equal” gives another \(22\), total \(44\). Hence \(\mathcal C\) is **exactly** the set of \(23\times 23\) binary matrices whose row parities are all equal and whose column parities are all equal.

So to make \(V+T\) have small weight, we are allowed to replace \(V\) by any matrix in the coset \(V+\mathcal C\), i.e. we can change \(V\) to a matrix whose row parities are constant and whose column parities are constant.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3) Distance to \(\mathcal C\) depends only on which rows/columns have odd parity
For a given \(V\), let
- \(a\) = number of rows of \(V\) with odd parity,
- \(b\) = number of columns of \(V\) with odd parity.

Necessarily \(a\equiv b\pmod 2\) (both equal the parity of the total number of 1’s).

If we want to change \(V\) into some matrix in \(\mathcal C\), we must make **all** row parities equal to some \(p\in\{0,1\}\) and all column parities equal to the same \(p\) (since \(23\) is odd, constant row parity forces the total parity to be \(p\), and constant column parity forces the same total parity).

Changing a single entry \((i,j)\) flips the parity of row \(i\) and column \(j\) and affects no other row/column parity. So we need to flip entries so that exactly the “wrong-parity” rows and columns get toggled.

- If target parity is \(p=0\): we must fix the \(a\) odd rows and the \(b\) odd columns.
- If target parity is \(p=1\): we must fix the \(23-a\) even rows and the \(23-b\) even columns.

Each flipped entry fixes (toggles) one row and one column. Therefore at least \(\max(a,b)\) flips are needed for \(p=0\), and at least \(\max(23-a,\,23-b)\) flips are needed for \(p=1\). In the complete \(23\times 23\) grid, these lower bounds are achievable by explicitly pairing rows with columns (one can assign one flip in each “bad” row, distributing them among “bad” columns as needed). Hence the exact distance is
\[
d(V,\mathcal C)=\min\Big(\max(a,b),\ \max(23-a,\,23-b)\Big).
\]

So the covering radius is
\[
\rho(\mathcal C)=\max_{a,b\in\{0,\dots,23\},\ a\equiv b\!\!\!\pmod 2}\ \min\Big(\max(a,b),\ \max(23-a,\,23-b)\Big).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4) Compute the maximum possible distance
The expression is symmetric under \((a,b)\mapsto(23-a,23-b)\), and it can never reach \(23\) because if \(\max(a,b)=23\) then \(\max(23-a,23-b)\le 22\), and vice versa.

We can attain \(22\): take \((a,b)=(23,1)\) (allowed since both are odd). Then
\[
\min(\max(23,1),\max(0,22))=\min(23,22)=22.
\]
Thus \(\rho(\mathcal C)\ge 22\). By the previous paragraph, \(\rho(\mathcal C)\le 22\). Hence
\[
\rho(\mathcal C)=22.
\]

Therefore the guaranteed number of black stones is
\[
m = 529 - 22 = 507.
\]

It remains only to note tightness: the pattern \(V\) consisting of 1’s in a single column and 0’s elsewhere indeed has \(a=23\), \(b=1\), so it stays at distance \(22\) from \(\mathcal C\). Equivalently, starting from the board that is all black except that one entire column is white (23 whites), no sequence of operations can reduce the number of whites below \(22\), so one cannot guarantee more than \(507\) blacks.

\[
\boxed{507}
\]
1bbc6f053152b9b617c08ab9e907babb8ddee0cb:
Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\), and work mod \(2\).  
An operation chooses a row \(i\) and column \(j\) and flips exactly the \(41\) squares in their union.

### 1) What is invariant under the operations?
Consider the parity (mod \(2\)) of the number of black stones in each row and each column.

In one operation \((i,j)\):

- Row \(i\): all \(21\) squares in that row flip \(\Rightarrow\) row \(i\)’s parity flips (since \(21\) is odd).
- Any other row \(r\neq i\): exactly the square \((r,j)\) flips \(\Rightarrow\) that row’s parity also flips.
So **every row parity flips**.

Similarly, **every column parity flips**.

Therefore, for rows \(r,s\), the XOR “row \(r\) parity \(\oplus\) row \(s\) parity” is unchanged (since both bits flip together). Same for columns. Hence the following are invariants:

- the vector of row parities, up to flipping all \(21\) bits at once;
- the vector of column parities, up to flipping all \(21\) bits at once.

Equivalently: the sets
\[
R=\{\text{rows with odd \# of blacks}\},\quad C=\{\text{columns with odd \# of blacks}\}
\]
are determined **up to simultaneously replacing \((R,C)\) by \((R^c,C^c)\)** (because flipping all \(441\) squares flips every row/column parity, and flipping all squares is achievable since \(21\) is odd and the all-ones board lies in the operation span).

So, within any reachable class, you may choose whichever of \((R,C)\) or \((R^c,C^c)\) is more favorable.

Also, for any configuration, \(|R|\equiv |C|\pmod 2\) because both equal the parity of the total number of black stones.

### 2) Minimum possible number of black stones given \((R,C)\)
Fix a parity pattern \((R,C)\). Let \(r=|R|\), \(c=|C|\).

**Lower bound.**  
Each odd row must contain at least one black, so \(\#\text{blacks}\ge r\).  
Each odd column must contain at least one black, so \(\#\text{blacks}\ge c\).  
Thus
\[
\#\text{blacks}\ge \max(r,c).
\]

**Construction achieving \(\max(r,c)\).**  
Assume \(r\ge c\). Since \(r\equiv c\pmod 2\), \(r-c\) is even.

- Pick \(c\) distinct odd rows in \(R\) and match them to the \(c\) odd columns in \(C\), placing one black in each matched \((\text{odd row},\text{odd col})\). Now all columns in \(C\) have odd parity, and those \(c\) rows are handled.
- There remain \(r-c\) odd rows. Pair them up. For each pair, place two blacks in a single fixed column from \(C\) (or any column if \(C=\varnothing\) and \(c=0\); the parity conditions still work because then \(r\) is even). Two blacks in the same column do not change that column’s parity, and they flip the two row parities as needed.

This uses exactly \(c+(r-c)=r=\max(r,c)\) blacks and produces precisely the desired odd rows/columns. Therefore:
\[
\min(\#\text{blacks} \text{ with pattern }(R,C))=\max(r,c).
\]

### 3) Worst case over a whole reachable class
A reachable class contains both \((R,C)\) and \((R^c,C^c)\). Their sizes are \((r,c)\) and \((21-r,21-c)\). So the minimum number of blacks achievable within that class is
\[
\min\big(\max(r,c),\max(21-r,\,21-c)\big)
= \min\big(\max(r,c),\,21-\min(r,c)\big).
\]
Let \(r\ge c\). Then this becomes \(\min(r,\,21-c)\). Under the constraint \(r\equiv c\pmod 2\), the maximum possible value of \(\min(r,21-c)\) occurs at \((r,c)=(20,0)\) or \((21,1)\), giving value \(20\). Hence in the worst reachable class, you cannot force fewer than \(20\) blacks (and likewise cannot force fewer than \(20\) whites).

So the **maximum** number of blacks you can always guarantee is
\[
441-20=421.
\]

\[
\boxed{421}
\]
8ab8a58519d7bbddf8443fc6ec62217531b53ba7:
Pair the indices as \((1,2),(3,4),\dots,(3999,4000)\).  
From the given condition, for each \(k=1,2,\dots,2000\),
\[
x_{2k-1}+x_{2k}\ge (2k-1)(2k).
\]
Summing these \(2000\) inequalities gives the universal lower bound
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{4000} x_i \;\ge\; \sum_{k=1}^{2000} (2k-1)(2k).
\]

Now define (allowing \(x_1=0\) for the moment)
\[
x_i=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{i^2}{2}, & i \text{ even},\\[6pt]
\dfrac{i^2-1}{2}, & i \text{ odd}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then for any \(k<m\),
\[
x_k+x_m=\frac{k^2+m^2-\delta(k)-\delta(m)}{2},
\]
where \(\delta(i)=1\) if \(i\) is odd and \(0\) if \(i\) is even. Hence
\[
x_k+x_m-km=\frac{(m-k)^2-\bigl(\delta(k)+\delta(m)\bigr)}{2}\ge 0,
\]
because:
- if at least one of \(k,m\) is even, then \(\delta(k)+\delta(m)\le 1\) and \((m-k)^2\ge 1\);
- if both are odd, then \(m-k\) is even \(\ge 2\), so \((m-k)^2\ge 4\ge 2=\delta(k)+\delta(m)\).

So all constraints hold, and moreover each paired inequality \((2k-1,2k)\) is tight:
\[
x_{2k-1}+x_{2k}=\frac{(2k-1)^2-1}{2}+\frac{(2k)^2}{2}=(2k-1)(2k).
\]
Thus \(\sum_{i=1}^{4000}x_i=\sum_{k=1}^{2000}(2k-1)(2k)\), achieving the lower bound (and with the “positive” requirement one can take \(x_1\) arbitrarily small to approach the same minimum value).

Compute:
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{2000}(2k-1)(2k)=\sum_{k=1}^{2000}(4k^2-2k)
=4\sum_{k=1}^{2000}k^2-2\sum_{k=1}^{2000}k.
\]
Using \(\sum_{k=1}^n k^2=\frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}\) and \(\sum_{k=1}^n k=\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\) with \(n=2000\):
\[
4\cdot \frac{2000\cdot 2001\cdot 4001}{6}-2\cdot \frac{2000\cdot 2001}{2}
=10{,}670{,}666{,}000.
\]

\[
\boxed{10670666000}
\]
ea78e960eff43519aed231f3b6ddccfef384e1c5:
For every \(t=1,2,\dots,3000\), applying the condition to the pair \((2t-1,\,2t)\) gives
\[
x_{2t-1}+x_{2t}\ge (2t-1)(2t).
\]
Summing these \(3000\) inequalities yields
\[
x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_{6000}
=\sum_{t=1}^{3000}(x_{2t-1}+x_{2t})
\ge \sum_{t=1}^{3000}(2t-1)(2t).
\]
Compute
\[
\sum_{t=1}^{3000}(2t-1)(2t)=\sum_{t=1}^{3000}(4t^2-2t)
=4\sum_{t=1}^{3000}t^2-2\sum_{t=1}^{3000}t.
\]
Now
\[
\sum_{t=1}^{3000}t=\frac{3000\cdot 3001}{2}=4{,}501{,}500,
\]
\[
\sum_{t=1}^{3000}t^2=\frac{3000\cdot 3001\cdot 6001}{6}
=500\cdot 3001\cdot 6001=9{,}004{,}500{,}500.
\]
So the lower bound equals
\[
4(9{,}004{,}500{,}500)-2(4{,}501{,}500)
=36{,}018{,}002{,}000-9{,}003{,}000
=36{,}008{,}999{,}000.
\]

It remains to show this bound is achievable with all \(x_k>0\). Fix \(\varepsilon=\tfrac12\) and define, for \(k=1,2,\dots,6000\),
\[
x_k=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{k^2}{2}, & k\ \text{odd},\\[6pt]
\dfrac{k^2}{2}-\dfrac12, & k\ \text{even}.
\end{cases}
\]
All \(x_k>0\). Also for consecutive integers \(k,k+1\), one checks directly that
\[
x_k+x_{k+1}=k(k+1),
\]
so in particular \(x_{2t-1}+x_{2t}=(2t-1)(2t)\), making the total sum equal to the lower bound.

Finally, for any \(k<m\):  
- If \(|m-k|\ge 2\), then
\[
x_k+x_m \ge \frac{k^2+m^2}{2}-1
=km+\frac{(m-k)^2}{2}-1 \ge km.
\]
- If \(m=k+1\), we already have equality \(x_k+x_{k+1}=k(k+1)\).

Thus all constraints hold, and the minimum sum is
\[
\boxed{36008999000}.
\]
9fd06733104a35efdf5cf8e707c1fe22f985cc9a:
Let the blocks be the vertices of the \(8\times 8\) grid graph, with edges between orthogonally adjacent blocks. The condition “every block has an adjacent block with a booth” is exactly the condition that the set of booth-blocks is a **total dominating set**.

### Construction with 24 booths (upper bound)
Split the grid into four disjoint \(2\times 8\) horizontal strips: rows \((1,2),(3,4),(5,6),(7,8)\).

In each \(2\times 8\) strip, place booths in **both rows** of columns \(2,5,7\).  
That is, in each strip you place 6 booths:
\[
(\text{row }a,\text{col }2),(\text{row }a,\text{col }5),(\text{row }a,\text{col }7),
(\text{row }a+1,\text{col }2),(\text{row }a+1,\text{col }5),(\text{row }a+1,\text{col }7).
\]
This pattern totally dominates that entire \(2\times 8\) strip: every non-booth block is adjacent (horizontally or vertically) to one of these booths, and every booth is adjacent (vertically) to its paired booth in the other row.

So the whole \(8\times 8\) grid is totally dominated using
\[
4\text{ strips}\times 6 = 24
\]
booths.

### Why fewer than 24 is impossible (lower bound)
A standard result for the \(2\times n\) grid (the “ladder” graph) is that its total domination number is
\[
\gamma_t(2\times n)=\left\lceil \frac{2n}{3}\right\rceil.
\]
In particular, for \(n=8\),
\[
\gamma_t(2\times 8)=\left\lceil \frac{16}{3}\right\rceil=6.
\]
Now observe that the \(8\times 8\) grid contains four **vertex-disjoint induced** \(2\times 8\) ladder subgraphs (the four row-pairs \((1,2),(3,4),(5,6),(7,8)\)). Any total dominating set for the whole grid restricts to a total dominating set on each of these induced ladders, so it must have at least 6 vertices (booths) in each ladder. Hence at least
\[
4\times 6=24
\]
booths are necessary.

Combining with the construction above, the minimum is \(24\).

\[
\boxed{24}
\]
ff092c86d71991fe94865d78b19b429404aea103:
Let the sequence satisfy the stated “no equal-sum split” condition for every interval \([i,j]\).

### 1) A key lemma: the sequence must be superincreasing
Claim: For every \(k\ge 2\),
\[
a_k>\sum_{t=1}^{k-1} a_t.
\]
Proof by contradiction. Let \(k\) be the smallest index with
\[
a_k\le S:=\sum_{t=1}^{k-1} a_t.
\]
By minimality of \(k\), we have \(a_m>\sum_{t=1}^{m-1}a_t\) for all \(m<k\). In particular, the initial segment \((a_1,\dots,a_{k-1})\) is **superincreasing**, and a standard greedy argument implies:

> Every integer \(x\) with \(0\le x\le S\) can be written as a sum of some subset of \(\{a_1,\dots,a_{k-1}\}\).

Now consider parity.

- **Case 1: \(S\equiv a_k\pmod 2\).** Then \(S+a_k\) is even, so \(\frac{S+a_k}{2}\) is an integer \(\le S\) (since \(a_k\le S\)). Hence there is a subset \(A\subset\{1,\dots,k-1\}\) with
  \[
  \sum_{t\in A} a_t=\frac{S+a_k}{2}.
  \]
  In the interval \([1,k]\), this subset \(A\) is a nonempty proper subset (because it does not contain \(k\)), and its complement has sum
  \[
  (S+a_k)-\frac{S+a_k}{2}=\frac{S+a_k}{2},
  \]
  giving a forbidden equal partition of \(\{1,\dots,k\}\).

- **Case 2: \(S\not\equiv a_k\pmod 2\).** Since \(a_1\) is odd and \(a_m>\sum_{t<m}a_t\) for \(m\ge2\), all \(a_2,a_3,\dots,a_{k-1}\) are even, hence \(S\) is odd. Thus \(a_k\) is even, and the interval \([2,k]\) has even total sum
  \[
  T:=\sum_{t=2}^k a_t=(S-a_1)+a_k,
  \]
  which is even. Also \((a_2,\dots,a_{k-1})\) is superincreasing and even, so dividing by \(2\) shows its subset sums hit every integer from \(0\) to \(\frac{S-a_1}{2}\). Since \(a_k\le S\), we get
  \[
  \frac{T}{2}=\frac{(S-a_1)+a_k}{2}\le \frac{S-a_1}{2},
  \]
  so there exists \(A\subset\{2,\dots,k-1\}\) with \(\sum_{t\in A}a_t=\frac{T}{2}\). Then \(A\) is a nonempty proper subset of \(\{2,\dots,k\}\) whose sum equals the complement’s sum, contradicting the condition on interval \([2,k]\).

Both cases contradict the hypothesis, so the claim holds:
\[
a_k>\sum_{t=1}^{k-1}a_t\quad \forall k\ge2.
\]

### 2) Consequence: exponential growth, hence \(n\le 1501\)
From \(a_k>\sum_{t<k}a_t\), we get inductively \(a_k\ge 2^{k-1}a_1\ge 2^{k-1}\). In particular,
\[
a_n\ge 2^{n-1}.
\]
Given \(a_n\le 2^{1500}\), it follows that \(2^{n-1}\le 2^{1500}\), hence \(n-1\le 1500\) and
\[
n\le 1501.
\]

### 3) Construction achieving \(n=1501\)
Take
\[
(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{1501})=(1,2,4,\dots,2^{1500}).
\]
Then every interval consists of distinct powers of \(2\), so all subset sums are distinct; in particular no subset can have the same sum as its complement. Also \(a_i\le 2^{1500}\) holds.

Thus the maximum possible \(n\) is \(1501\).

\[
\boxed{1501}
\]
596a2051bf0ebb81aa34789c08b0bde5b9192a7b:
Let \(n=1936\). We are free to choose which \(n\) unit squares (one in each row and column) remain uncovered, so we may choose the simplest pattern: leave the main diagonal squares \((i,i)\) uncovered for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\).

---

## Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles (upper bound)

Cover all squares above the diagonal by the \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles
\[
R_i=\{(i,j): i<j\le n\}\qquad (i=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
each of size \(1\times (n-i)\).

Cover all squares below the diagonal by the \(n-1\) vertical rectangles
\[
C_i=\{(j,i): i<j\le n\}\qquad (i=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
each of size \((n-i)\times 1\).

These \(2(n-1)\) rectangles are pairwise disjoint and cover every square except the diagonal ones, leaving exactly one uncovered square in each row and column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

---

## Lower bound: \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary

Still assuming the diagonal squares are the uncovered ones, consider the \(n-1\) squares just above the diagonal:
\[
(i,i+1)\qquad (i=1,2,\dots,n-1).
\]
Each of these must be covered.

**Claim:** For each \(i\), any rectangle tile covering \((i,i+1)\) must have left edge on column \(i+1\) and bottom edge on row \(i\).  
Reason: If it extended left to column \(i\), it would also contain \((i,i)\), which is uncovered (forbidden). If it extended down to row \(i+1\), then since it already contains column \(i+1\), it would also contain \((i+1,i+1)\), also uncovered. Thus the tile covering \((i,i+1)\) is forced to have left edge exactly at column \(i+1\) and bottom edge exactly at row \(i\).

Therefore, two different squares \((i,i+1)\) and \((k,k+1)\) (\(i\neq k\)) cannot be covered by the same rectangle (they would force different left edges and bottom edges). Hence at least \(n-1\) distinct rectangles are needed to cover these \(n-1\) squares.

Similarly, consider the \(n-1\) squares just below the diagonal:
\[
(i+1,i)\qquad (i=1,2,\dots,n-1).
\]
By the same forced-edge argument (now forbidding inclusion of \((i,i)\) and \((i+1,i+1)\)), each requires its own rectangle, and these rectangles cannot coincide with those forced by the \((i,i+1)\) squares. Thus at least another \(n-1\) rectangles are necessary.

So any such tiling needs at least
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2
\]
rectangles.

Combined with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\).

---

For \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
e9edd9c4582742a97643dcb19f2d96add8919d76:
\[
\boxed{3001}
\]

**Construction (shows \(n\ge 3001\)):**  
Take
\[
a_k=2^{k-1}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,3001).
\]
Then \(a_k\le 2^{3000}\) holds.

Now fix any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\). All numbers in this interval are **distinct powers of 2**, hence every subset of \(\{a_i,\dots,a_j\}\) has a **unique** sum (binary representation is unique). Therefore, if for some nonempty proper subset \(A\subset\{i,\dots,j\}\) we had
\[
\sum_{k\in A}a_k=\sum_{k\in\{i,\dots,j\}\setminus A}a_k,
\]
then two different subsets (\(A\) and its complement) would have the same sum, contradicting uniqueness. So the required property holds.

Hence \(n\ge 3001\).

---

**Upper bound (shows \(n\le 3001\)):**  
Assume there is a valid sequence of length \(n\). Consider the last term \(a_n\). If
\[
a_n \le \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} a_k,
\]
then within the interval \(\{1,2,\dots,n\}\), the set of subset sums of \(\{a_1,\dots,a_{n-1}\}\) contains two values whose difference is exactly \(a_n\) (by the standard “interval coverage” argument for subset sums when the next element does not exceed the sum of the previous ones). That produces two disjoint subsets \(B,C\subset\{1,\dots,n-1\}\) with
\[
\sum_{k\in B}a_k=\sum_{k\in C}a_k+a_n,
\]
which rearranges to an equal-sum partition of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\), contradicting the condition. Therefore we must have the strict inequality
\[
a_n > \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} a_k.
\]
Applying the same argument inductively to prefixes gives
\[
a_k > \sum_{t=1}^{k-1} a_t\quad\text{for each }k\ge 2.
\]
In particular, the minimal possible growth under these inequalities is
\[
a_1\ge 1,\quad a_2\ge 2,\quad a_3\ge 4,\ \dots,\ a_k\ge 2^{k-1}.
\]
Hence
\[
a_n \ge 2^{n-1}.
\]
But we are given \(a_n \le 2^{3000}\), so
\[
2^{n-1}\le 2^{3000}\implies n-1\le 3000\implies n\le 3001.
\]

Combining both parts yields \(n=3001\).
9039950066183e1dedfd00b05140aa250c7bf6ec:
Let \(n=4000\). From the condition \(x_k+x_m\ge km\) for all \(1\le k<m\le n\), in particular for the \(2000\) disjoint pairs
\[
(1,2),(3,4),\dots,(3999,4000),
\]
we have
\[
x_{2i-1}+x_{2i}\ \ge\ (2i-1)(2i)\qquad (i=1,2,\dots,2000).
\]
Summing these \(2000\) inequalities gives the lower bound
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{4000} x_k
=\sum_{i=1}^{2000}(x_{2i-1}+x_{2i})
\ge \sum_{i=1}^{2000}(2i-1)(2i).
\]
Compute
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{2000}(2i-1)(2i)=\sum_{i=1}^{2000}(4i^2-2i)
=4\sum_{i=1}^{2000}i^2-2\sum_{i=1}^{2000}i.
\]
Using \(\sum_{i=1}^{m} i^2=\frac{m(m+1)(2m+1)}6\) and \(\sum_{i=1}^m i=\frac{m(m+1)}2\) with \(m=2000\),
\[
4\cdot \frac{2000\cdot 2001\cdot 4001}{6}-2\cdot \frac{2000\cdot 2001}{2}
=10{,}670{,}666{,}000.
\]
So
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{4000} x_k \ge 10{,}670{,}666{,}000.
\]

Now we show this bound is attainable. Define
\[
x_k=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{k^2}{2}, & k\ \text{odd},\\[4pt]
\dfrac{k^2-1}{2}, & k\ \text{even}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then for any \(k<m\),
\[
x_k+x_m-\!km=\frac{k^2+m^2}{2}-\frac{\varepsilon_k+\varepsilon_m}{2}-km
=\frac{(k-m)^2}{2}-\frac{\varepsilon_k+\varepsilon_m}{2},
\]
where \(\varepsilon_t=0\) if \(t\) is odd and \(\varepsilon_t=1\) if \(t\) is even. Thus \(\varepsilon_k+\varepsilon_m\in\{0,1,2\}\). Check cases:

- If both are odd: RHS \(=\frac{(k-m)^2}{2}\ge0\).
- If one is even, one odd: RHS \(=\frac{(k-m)^2}{2}-\frac12\ge0\) since \(|k-m|\ge1\).
- If both are even: RHS \(=\frac{(k-m)^2}{2}-1\ge0\) since \(|k-m|\ge2\).

Hence all constraints \(x_k+x_m\ge km\) hold. Also,
\[
x_{2i-1}+x_{2i}=\frac{(2i-1)^2}{2}+\frac{(2i)^2-1}{2}=(2i-1)(2i),
\]
so
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{4000} x_k=\sum_{i=1}^{2000}(2i-1)(2i)=10{,}670{,}666{,}000.
\]

Therefore the minimum possible value is
\[
\boxed{10{,}670{,}666{,}000}.
\]
a22f1a6122e9414cd677a09064a0b84c0505e73e:
Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\) (working mod 2). An operation chooses a row \(i\) and column \(j\) and flips exactly the \(41\) stones in their union (the intersection \((i,j)\) is flipped once).

### 1) The key invariants: row- and column-parity differences
Let
\[
r_i=\sum_{k=1}^{21} a_{ik}\pmod 2,\qquad c_j=\sum_{k=1}^{21} a_{kj}\pmod 2
\]
be the parities of row \(i\) and column \(j\).

In one operation, every row has an odd number of flipped cells (row \(i\) has 21 flips; each other row has 1 flip), hence **every** \(r_i\) toggles. So all row parities flip simultaneously:
\[
(r_1,\dots,r_{21})\mapsto (r_1+1,\dots,r_{21}+1).
\]
Similarly all column parities flip simultaneously. Therefore:

- The pairwise differences \(r_i+r_k\) are invariant (same for columns).
- From any position, the possible row-parity vectors are exactly \(r\) and \(r+\mathbf 1\); similarly columns are \(c\) and \(c+\mathbf 1\).

Also, summing all \(441\) possible operations flips each cell \(41\equiv 1\pmod 2\) times, so it flips **every** cell once; thus each orbit contains a configuration and its complement.

A standard fact (and easy to verify here using “\(2\times2\) toggles” obtained by combining four operations) is: **once the row and column parities are fixed, we can reach any board with exactly those parities.** Hence, for optimization we only need to consider what maximum number of black stones is possible given the row/column parity pattern (up to simultaneous complement).

### 2) Maximizing blacks for a fixed parity pattern
Let \(a\) be the number of rows of even parity, and \(b\) the number of columns of even parity.

We claim: for fixed \((a,b)\), the **minimum** possible number of white stones is \(\max(a,b)\), hence the **maximum** possible number of black stones is
\[
441-\max(a,b).
\]

- **Lower bound:** Every even-parity row must contain at least one white (otherwise it would have 21 blacks, odd parity). So \(\#\text{whites}\ge a\). Similarly \(\#\text{whites}\ge b\). Thus \(\#\text{whites}\ge \max(a,b)\).

- **Construction achieving \(\max(a,b)\):** Assume \(a\ge b\). Put one white in each even column, all in distinct even rows (this uses \(b\) whites and makes those \(b\) even rows have 1 white so far). The remaining \(a-b\) even rows must also have an odd number of whites; pair them up and for each pair place two whites in the same (odd-parity) column, one in each row. This adds \(a-b\) whites, keeps that column even (degree 2), and fixes both rows. Total whites \(=b+(a-b)=a=\max(a,b)\). (The parity consistency condition holds automatically because the number of odd rows equals the number of odd columns mod 2.)

So with parities \((a,b)\), best possible blacks \(=441-\max(a,b)\).

### 3) We may choose between \((a,b)\) and \((21-a,\,21-b)\)
Because an operation flips all row parities and all column parities, from a given orbit we may choose either parity pattern \((a,b)\) or its complemented pattern \((21-a,21-b)\). Thus the best achievable number of blacks in that orbit is
\[
441-\min\bigl(\max(a,b),\ \max(21-a,\,21-b)\bigr).
\]

To guarantee at least \(m\) blacks for **every** initial arrangement, we must take the worst orbit, i.e. maximize
\[
\min\bigl(\max(a,b),\ \max(21-a,\,21-b)\bigr).
\]
Writing \(u=\min(a,b)\), \(v=\max(a,b)\), note \(\max(21-a,21-b)=21-u\). So the quantity becomes \(\min(v,\,21-u)\). Its maximum (over integers \(0\le u\le v\le 21\) with the parity condition, which always holds here) is \(20\) (e.g. \((a,b)=(0,20)\) gives \(\min(20,21)=20\); value \(21\) is impossible because it would require \((a,b)=(0,21)\) or \((21,0)\), which violates parity consistency).

Therefore the worst-case minimum number of whites is \(20\), hence the guaranteed number of blacks is
\[
m=441-20=421.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (cannot guarantee \(422\))
Take the board that is all black except in the first row, columns \(1\) through \(20\), place whites. Then:
- every row has odd parity \(\Rightarrow a=0\),
- columns \(1\)–\(20\) have 20 blacks (even parity) and column \(21\) has 21 blacks (odd parity) \(\Rightarrow b=20\).

In its orbit, parities are either \((0,20)\) or \((21,1)\), and in either case any configuration must have at least \(\max(a,b)=20\) whites, i.e. at most \(421\) blacks. So \(422\) cannot be guaranteed.

\[
\boxed{421}
\]
5bcae6e53f9bf299b96bffa37d9670b56918e3ff:
Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\), and work over \(\mathbb F_2\).  
An operation chooses \((r,c)\) and flips the \(45\) entries in row \(r\) and column \(c\) (with \((r,c)\) flipped once).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1) The reachable set is a coset of a linear code

Represent a board as a \(23\times 23\) binary matrix \(X=(x_{ij})\).  
Let \(G\subseteq \mathbb F_2^{23\times 23}\) be the \(\mathbb F_2\)-span of all operation-matrices. From a starting board \(A\), the set of reachable boards is exactly the coset \(A+G\).

For any board \(X\), define row parities
\[
\rho_i(X)=\sum_{j=1}^{23} x_{ij}\pmod 2,
\]
and column parities
\[
\kappa_j(X)=\sum_{i=1}^{23} x_{ij}\pmod 2.
\]

**Effect of one operation.** In the operation \((r,c)\), every row gets flipped an odd number of times (row \(r\) gets \(23\) flips, every other row gets \(1\)), hence **every row parity toggles**. Similarly **every column parity toggles**. Therefore, for all \(i,i',j,j'\),
\[
\rho_i(X)+\rho_{i'}(X)\quad\text{and}\quad \kappa_j(X)+\kappa_{j'}(X)
\]
are invariants under operations (they do not change, since both terms toggle).

Equivalently, in any difference \(D\in G\), all row parities are equal (all \(0\) or all \(1\)), and all column parities are equal (same value). Let
\[
H=\{D:\ \rho_1(D)=\cdots=\rho_{23}(D)\ \text{and}\ \kappa_1(D)=\cdots=\kappa_{23}(D)\}.
\]
Then \(G\subseteq H\). One checks \(\dim(H)=529-44=485\), and the above \(44\) independent invariants show \(\dim(G)\le 485\), hence \(\dim(G)=485\) and
\[
G=H.
\]

So, **two boards are in the same coset \(A+G\) iff they have the same row-parity differences and the same column-parity differences.**

Also, summing the \(23\) operations \((r,c)\) over all \(c\) gives the all-ones matrix \(J\), so \(J\in G\). Thus every coset is closed under complementing all colors; hence within a coset, if a board with \(w\) blacks is reachable then so is one with \(529-w\) blacks.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Worst-case maximum blacks = \(529-\rho\), where \(\rho\) is a covering radius

For a coset \(A+G\), let
\[
d(A)=\min_{X\in A+G} \operatorname{wt}(X)
\]
(minimum number of black stones achievable in that coset). Since \(J\in G\),
\[
\max_{X\in A+G}\operatorname{wt}(X)=529-d(A).
\]
Therefore the guaranteed number of blacks is
\[
m= \min_A \max_{X\in A+G}\operatorname{wt}(X)=529-\max_A d(A)
=529-\rho,
\]
where \(\rho\) is the maximum, over all cosets, of the minimum weight in that coset.

So it remains to compute \(\rho\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3) Computing \(\rho\) via a minimum-edge parity graph

Fix a board \(X\). Let
\[
a=\#\{i:\rho_i(X)=1\},\qquad b=\#\{j:\kappa_j(X)=1\}.
\]
Because the total parity equals both the sum of row parities and the sum of column parities,
\[
a\equiv b\pmod 2.
\]

To change \(X\) into some codeword \(D\in G\) (i.e. make all row parities equal and all column parities equal), we may flip a set of individual cells; flipping one cell toggles exactly one row parity and one column parity.

This is equivalent to choosing edges in the complete bipartite graph \(K_{23,23}\) (rows on the left, columns on the right), where an edge \((i,j)\) means “flip cell \((i,j)\)”. The vertices that must end up with odd degree are precisely the rows/columns whose parity needs toggling.

There are two choices for the target constant parity \(t\in\{0,1\}\):

- If we target \(t=0\): we must toggle exactly the \(a\) odd rows and the \(b\) odd columns.
- If we target \(t=1\): we must toggle exactly the \(23-a\) even rows and the \(23-b\) even columns.

In \(K_{23,23}\), the minimum number of edges whose odd-degree vertices are a prescribed set \(R\) of rows and \(C\) of columns is
\[
\max(|R|,|C|),
\]
because \(|E|\ge |R|\) (each odd row needs degree \(\ge1\)) and \(|E|\ge |C|\), and this bound is achievable by a direct construction.

Hence the distance from \(X\) to \(G\) is exactly
\[
d(X)=\min\bigl(\max(a,b),\ \max(23-a,23-b)\bigr).
\]

Now maximize this over all \(a,b\in\{0,\dots,23\}\) with \(a\equiv b\pmod 2\).  
WLOG assume \(a\ge b\). Then
\[
d(X)=\min(a,\ 23-b).
\]
This is at most \(22\), because achieving \(23\) would require \(a=23\) and \(b=0\), which violates \(a\equiv b\pmod 2\). Thus \(\rho\le 22\).

Moreover \(\rho=22\) is achieved: take a board with blacks exactly in cells \((1,1),(2,1),\dots,(22,1)\). Then \(a=22\) (22 odd rows) and \(b=0\) (all columns even), giving
\[
d(X)=\min(\max(22,0),\max(1,23))=\min(22,23)=22.
\]
So \(\rho=22\).

Therefore
\[
m=529-\rho=529-22=507.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4) Conclusion

No matter the initial arrangement, one can always reach a board with at least \(507\) black stones, and there exist initial arrangements for which one cannot force more than \(507\).

\[
\boxed{507}
\]
