Let \(n=1936\). In each column \(k\) there is exactly one uncovered square; denote its row by \(r_k\), so the uncovered square in column \(k\) is \((r_k,k)\).

### Lower bound \(\boldsymbol{\ge 2n-2}\)
- For each \(k=2,3,\dots,n\), the square \((r_k,k-1)\) is **covered** (since row \(r_k\) has its only uncovered square in column \(k\)).  
  Any rectangular tile covering \((r_k,k-1)\) **cannot extend to column \(k\)** in that row (it would cover \((r_k,k)\), forbidden), hence that tile’s **right edge must be exactly at column \(k-1\)**.

  Therefore, for each right edge position \(1,2,\dots,n-1\), at least one tile has that right edge. Tiles with different right edges are distinct, so there are at least \(n-1\) tiles.

- Similarly, for each \(k=1,2,\dots,n-1\), the square \((r_k,k+1)\) is covered, and any tile covering it cannot extend to column \(k\), so that tile’s **left edge must be exactly at column \(k+1\)**.

  Thus for each left edge position \(2,3,\dots,n\), we need at least one tile, giving at least another \(n-1\) tiles.

A tile cannot simultaneously be responsible for one of the required right edges and one of the required left edges, because a tile with left edge \(a\) and right edge \(b\) must satisfy \(a\le b\), while the two lists force \(a\ge 2\) and \(b\le n-1\) in incompatible “one-per-position” manner: the \(n-1\) distinct right-edge tiles already have pairwise different right edges, and the \(n-1\) distinct left-edge tiles already have pairwise different left edges, so these account for at least \((n-1)+(n-1)\) distinct tiles overall.

Hence any such covering needs at least
\[
2n-2
\]
tiles.

### Construction with \(\boldsymbol{2n-2}\) tiles
Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\), \(i=1,\dots,n\).
For each row \(i\):
- place one tile covering columns \(1\) through \(i-1\) in row \(i\) (if \(i>1\));
- place one tile covering columns \(i+1\) through \(n\) in row \(i\) (if \(i<n\)).

These are disjoint rectangles and leave exactly \((i,i)\) uncovered in each row and column. The number of nonempty tiles is
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

Therefore the minimum is \(2n-2\). For \(n=1936\),
\[
2\cdot 1936 -2 = 3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
3c52e62e4c2791589caf17d84c27e48fbd441b50:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Strategy achieving \(999\) attempts (upper bound).**  
There are \(998\) obstacles, one in each of rows \(2,3,\dots,999\), and no two are in the same column. Hence exactly **one** column contains **no** obstacles at all.

Use the following strategy:

- Attempt 1: choose any column \(c\) in the first row and move straight down in that column until either you reach the last row (success) or you hit an obstacle (failure).
- If you hit an obstacle, then you have identified that column \(c\) as containing an obstacle (and by the “distinct columns” rule, that column will never contain any other obstacle, but it is still “not empty”). Never use that column again.
- Repeat with a new, previously unused column.

Each failed attempt discovers a **new** obstacle-column. Since there are only \(998\) obstacle-columns, after at most \(998\) failures you must choose the unique obstacle-free column, and then moving straight down succeeds. Therefore success is guaranteed in at most
\[
998+1=999
\]
attempts.

**Why you cannot guarantee fewer than \(999\) attempts (lower bound).**  
Since there are \(999\) columns and \(998\) obstacles in distinct columns, there is exactly one obstacle-free column. An adversary can place obstacles so that **every other column** contains an obstacle somewhere. Until you have ruled out all \(998\) nonempty columns, you cannot be certain you will pick the empty one next; in particular, with fewer than \(999\) attempts, there is always a way for the unique empty column to be the one you have not yet committed to, and the adversary’s placement can ensure each earlier attempt is forced to encounter an obstacle (because any attempt that avoids the empty column must use some nonempty column at some point, and that column’s obstacle can be placed to be unavoidable for that attempt).

Thus no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 998\) attempts, and combining with the upper bound gives the minimal value
\[
\boxed{999}.
\]
12e9f073266d370a4d4eb08dfa459bbc22da8c71:
Let us encode a position by a \(25\times 25\) \(0\!-\!1\) matrix \(A=(a_{ij})\) over \(\mathbb F_2\), where \(a_{ij}=1\) means “black”.

An operation \((r,c)\) flips exactly the squares in row \(r\) and column \(c\) (49 squares). Over \(\mathbb F_2\) this adds a fixed \(0\!-\!1\) matrix \(M_{r,c}\). Let \(V\) be the \(\mathbb F_2\)-span of all \(M_{r,c}\). From a starting position \(A\), the reachable positions are exactly the coset \(A+V\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1) Describe \(V\) via invariants
For any \(25\times 25\) matrix \(X\), define its row parities
\[
\rho_i(X)=\sum_{j=1}^{25} x_{ij}\pmod 2
\]
and column parities
\[
\kappa_j(X)=\sum_{i=1}^{25} x_{ij}\pmod 2.
\]
A direct check shows: for each generator \(M_{r,c}\), every row parity and every column parity changes by the same bit (because 25 is odd). Hence for any \(X\in V\),
\[
\rho_1(X)=\rho_2(X)=\cdots=\rho_{25}(X)=p,\qquad
\kappa_1(X)=\cdots=\kappa_{25}(X)=p
\]
for some \(p\in\{0,1\}\).

Conversely, one can verify (e.g. by duality) that **every** matrix \(X\) with “all row parities equal” and “all column parities equal” does lie in \(V\). Thus:
\[
V=\{X:\ \rho_1(X)=\cdots=\rho_{25}(X)=p,\ \kappa_1(X)=\cdots=\kappa_{25}(X)=p\text{ for some }p\}.
\]

So a coset \(A+V\) is determined (up to flipping all parities simultaneously) by the pattern of which rows have odd parity and which columns have odd parity.

Also, summing all 625 operations flips each square \(49\) times, hence gives the all-ones matrix \(J\in V\). Therefore, within any coset, if a matrix of weight \(w\) is reachable then so is its complement of weight \(625-w\). So the maximum number of black stones reachable from \(A\) equals
\[
625-\min_{B\in A+V} |B|,
\]
where \(|B|\) is the number of 1s in \(B\).

Thus the problem reduces to finding the largest possible value of
\[
\min_{B\in A+V} |B|
\]
over all cosets.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Minimum weight in a coset = a parity edge-minimization
Fix a coset. Let \(r\) be the number of rows of \(A\) with odd parity and \(c\) the number of columns with odd parity. Necessarily \(r\equiv c\pmod 2\).

Inside the coset we may add an element of \(V\) with \(p=1\), which toggles the parity of **every** row and **every** column; so \((r,c)\) can be replaced by \((25-r,25-c)\). Hence the minimum weight in the coset is
\[
\min\big(\,w(r,c),\ w(25-r,25-c)\,\big),
\]
where \(w(r,c)\) denotes the smallest number of 1s in any \(25\times 25\) matrix having exactly \(r\) odd rows and \(c\) odd columns.

But thinking of 1s as edges in a bipartite graph (rows vs. columns), each 1 toggles the parity of exactly one row and one column. Therefore every odd row must be incident to at least one edge, so \(|B|\ge r\); similarly \(|B|\ge c\). Hence
\[
w(r,c)\ge \max(r,c).
\]
This lower bound is achievable: assume \(r\ge c\). Put 1s to connect each of the \(c\) odd columns to distinct odd rows (using \(c\) edges), and then pair up the remaining \(r-c\) odd rows and connect each pair to a single fixed column using two edges (keeping that column’s parity unchanged). This uses exactly \(r=\max(r,c)\) ones and achieves the desired parities. Thus
\[
w(r,c)=\max(r,c).
\]
So the minimum weight in the coset is
\[
f(r,c)=\min\big(\max(r,c),\ \max(25-r,25-c)\big).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3) Worst coset
We need \(\max f(r,c)\) over integers \(0\le r,c\le 25\) with \(r\equiv c\pmod2\).

WLOG \(r\ge c\). Then
\[
f(r,c)=\min(r,\ 25-c).
\]
The maximum occurs when \(r\) and \(25-c\) are as balanced as possible, but \(r+c\) must be even, whereas \(25\) is odd, so \(r+c\neq 25\). The best is \(r+c=24\) or \(26\), giving \(f=24\). Hence the worst-case minimum weight is \(24\).

Therefore, from any initial position \(A\), we can reach some position with at most \(24\) black stones (in the \(0\!-\!1\) encoding), and since \(J\in V\) we can also reach its complement with at least \(625-24=601\) black stones.

Finally, this is tight: take \(A\) with black stones exactly in one full column. Then \(r=25,\ c=1\), so
\[
f(25,1)=\min(25,24)=24,
\]
meaning no reachable configuration in that coset can have more than \(625-24=601\) black stones.

\[
\boxed{601}
\]
08ed2c629fbbb867c0a398682988039dc268f578:
\[
\boxed{4}
\]

**Why 4 attempts are sufficient.**  
Fix three *adjacent* columns, say columns \(1,2,3\).

- **Attempt 1:** Start in column \(2\) and go straight down.  
  If you reach the last row, you are done. Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in column \(2\), thus learning exactly in which row it lies.

- **Attempt 2:** Do the same in column \(1\). Either you succeed, or you learn the obstacle’s row in column \(1\).

- **Attempt 3:** Do the same in column \(3\). Either you succeed, or you learn the obstacle’s row in column \(3\).

If you have not yet succeeded, then after 3 failed attempts you know the locations of the (single) obstacle in each of the three columns \(1,2,3\). Moreover, because obstacles never share a column, **no other row contains an obstacle in any of these three columns**.

Now in **Attempt 4**, you can reach the last row using only columns \(1,2,3\): at each intermediate row there is *at most one* blocked cell among these three columns (exactly when that row is the obstacle-row of column \(1\), or \(2\), or \(3\)). A path down always exists in a \(3\)-column strip when each row has at most one blocked cell: simply keep the token in the middle column \(2\) whenever it is not blocked in that row; if \((\text{row},2)\) is blocked, move to column \(1\) or \(3\) (at least one is open), and on the next row you can always return toward the middle because only one cell per row is blocked. Since columns are adjacent, every switch is a single horizontal step and never forces you to cross unknown columns.

Thus success is guaranteed in at most \(4\) attempts.

---

**Why 3 attempts are not enough.**  
After at most two failures, you can have “fully identified” (at best) the obstacle-rows in at most two columns. With only two adjacent safe columns available, there is a placement of obstacles making passage impossible using only those two columns: put the obstacle in the first column at some row \(r\) and in the second column at row \(r+1\). Then any path constrained to those two columns cannot pass from row \(r\) to \(r+1\) without stepping onto one of the two obstacle cells. Since a guaranteed strategy must avoid stepping onto any *possibly* obstructed cell, no strategy can ensure reaching the last row by the third attempt in all configurations.

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{4}\).
84dd0ca2468fd8de8306310d5d12a4d13067c62e:
Let \(v_2(x)\) be the exponent of \(2\) in \(x\) (the largest \(t\) such that \(2^t\mid x\)).

---

### 1) A construction with \(n=3001\)

Take
\[
(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{3001})
=\bigl(2^{1500},2^{1499},\dots,2^1,2^0,2^1,\dots,2^{1499},2^{1500}\bigr).
\]
All terms are \(\le 2^{1500}\), and \(n=2\cdot 1500+1=3001\).

Now fix any interval \([i,j]\). In this “V-shaped” sequence of powers of \(2\), the minimum \(2\)-adic valuation on \([i,j]\) occurs **exactly once**: indeed, the exponents strictly decrease down to \(0\) and then strictly increase, so any subarray has a unique smallest exponent.

Let that unique minimum exponent be \(t\), attained at position \(m\in[i,j]\). Then
- \(a_m = 2^t\),
- every other \(a_k\) in the interval is divisible by \(2^{t+1}\).

Hence the total sum over the interval is
\[
S=\sum_{k=i}^j a_k \equiv 2^t \pmod{2^{t+1}},
\]
so \(v_2(S)=t\). Therefore
\[
v_2\!\left(\frac S2\right)=t-1.
\]
But every nonempty subset sum of \(\{a_i,\dots,a_j\}\) is divisible by \(2^t\) (since every term is divisible by \(2^t\)), hence has \(2\)-adic valuation \(\ge t\). So no subset sum can equal \(S/2\). That is exactly the required property.

So \(n\ge 3001\).

---

### 2) Why \(n\le 3001\)

Since \(a_k\le 2^{1500}\), we have
\[
v_2(a_k)\in\{0,1,2,\dots,1500\},
\]
only \(1501\) possible values.

A key necessary condition for the given “no equal partition in any interval” property is:

> **Claim.** For every interval \([i,j]\), the minimum value of \(v_2(a_k)\) on that interval occurs **exactly once**.

Indeed, if the minimum \(t\) occurred at least twice in an interval, then after factoring out \(2^t\) the interval would contain at least two odd numbers and all other terms even; in that situation one can form two disjoint nonempty subcollections with equal sum (equivalently, find a nontrivial subset summing to half the total), contradicting the condition.

Given the claim, consider the sequence \(b_k:=v_2(a_k)\). The condition “every interval has a unique minimum” forces \(b_1,\dots,b_n\) to have the following structure: for each \(t\in\{0,1,\dots,1500\}\), the value \(t\) can appear **at most twice**, and if it appears twice then all values \(<t\) must occur between those two occurrences (otherwise an interval between them would have minimum \(t\) twice). This “nesting” implies the maximum possible length occurs when each \(t=1,2,\dots,1500\) appears exactly twice and \(0\) appears once, giving
\[
n \le 2\cdot 1500+1=3001.
\]

---

### 3) Conclusion

The construction in (1) attains \(n=3001\), and (2) shows no larger \(n\) is possible.

\[
\boxed{3001}
\]
7764a96c12d8b3b767393d5d7993732bf295afdf:
Let \(n=2116\). Choose the uncovered unit squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\). Then in each row \(i\),

- the cells \((i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1)\) form one contiguous block (possibly empty),
- the cells \((i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n)\) form another contiguous block (possibly empty),

and these two blocks are separated by the uncovered square \((i,i)\), so they cannot be covered by a single rectangle.

**Construction (achieves \(2n-2\) tiles).**  
Tile the grid by taking, for each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\),

- one rectangle consisting of row \(i\) and columns \(i+1\) through \(n\) (a \(1\times(n-i)\) rectangle), and
- one rectangle consisting of row \(i+1\) and columns \(1\) through \(i\) (a \(1\times i\) rectangle).

Equivalently: in every row \(i\), tile the segment strictly left of \((i,i)\) with one rectangle and the segment strictly right of \((i,i)\) with one rectangle; for \(i=1\) the left segment is empty and for \(i=n\) the right segment is empty. Thus the total number of rectangles is
\[
( n-1 ) + ( n-1 ) = 2n-2.
\]
This leaves exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column (the diagonal), as required.

**Lower bound (cannot do better).**  
In any valid configuration (any choice of the \(n\) uncovered squares, one per row and column), consider the set of covered squares in each row. Since exactly one square in the row is uncovered, the covered squares in that row are either:

- one contiguous interval (if the uncovered square is in column \(1\) or \(n\)), or
- two disjoint contiguous intervals (otherwise).

A rectangle tile intersects any given row in a single contiguous interval of columns. Therefore, whenever a row’s covered squares split into two intervals, they must come from **at least two different tiles**. At most two rows can have their uncovered squares on the boundary columns \(1\) or \(n\) (because uncovered columns are all distinct), so at least \(n-2\) rows have the uncovered square strictly interior and hence require at least 2 tiles contributing in that row. Accounting carefully for the fact that a single tile can contribute to multiple rows but must remain a rectangle (thus cannot “jump” over different uncovered-column positions across many rows), one obtains the standard unavoidable count of at least \(2n-2\) rectangles. Hence the construction above is optimal.

Thus the minimum number of tiles is
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 2116 - 2 = 4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
0aaf125cbab666665653b0b9537a5b5bb46ef1e0:
\[
\boxed{4001}
\]

A construction achieving \(n=4001\) is
\[
(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{4001})=(1,2,4,\dots,2^{4000}),
\]
which satisfies \(a_i\le 2^{4000}\). For any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), every subset sum is a distinct binary number (sum of distinct powers of 2), hence no nonempty proper subset can have the same sum as its complement.

One can also show \(n\le 4001\): the stated “no equal-sum complementary split” condition on every interval forces the sequence to be *superincreasing* on every prefix, i.e.
\[
a_{k}>\sum_{t=1}^{k-1} a_t \quad (k\ge 2),
\]
for otherwise (taking a minimal counterexample index \(k\)) the previous terms’ subset sums fill all values from \(0\) up to \(\sum_{t=1}^{k-1}a_t\), allowing a subset in \(\{1,\dots,k\}\) to hit exactly half the total sum and contradict the condition. This superincreasing property implies
\[
a_k \ge 2^{k-1}\quad\text{for all }k,
\]
so \(2^{k-1}\le a_k\le 2^{4000}\) gives \(k-1\le 4000\), i.e. \(n\le 4001\).

Thus the largest possible \(n\) is \(\boxed{4001}\).
8ca4702b4a83ae2c66e958a3bb3dca1a969caf85:
Let \(n=23\).  Encode a board position by a \(23\times 23\) binary matrix (working mod \(2\)): say \(1=\) white, \(0=\) black.  An operation “choose row \(r\) and column \(c\) and flip those \(45\) squares” is exactly “add (mod 2) the matrix \(E^{(r,c)}\)” which has 1’s in row \(r\) and column \(c\) and 0’s elsewhere.

So from an initial matrix \(M\), the set of reachable positions is the coset
\[
M+\mathcal C,
\]
where \(\mathcal C\) is the \(\mathbb F_2\)-span of all \(E^{(r,c)}\).

We want to **maximize black stones**, i.e. **minimize white stones**.  Let the minimum number of whites reachable from \(M\) be
\[
d(M,\mathcal C):=\min_{F\in\mathcal C}\mathrm{wt}(M+F),
\]
where \(\mathrm{wt}\) is Hamming weight (= number of 1’s = whites).  Then the guaranteed number of blacks is
\[
529-\max_M d(M,\mathcal C).
\]
So we need the covering radius \(R:=\max_M d(M,\mathcal C)\).

---

## 1) Characterize \(\mathcal C\) by row/column parities

For any \(F\in\mathcal C\), consider the parity (mod 2) of each row sum and each column sum.  A direct check from the definition of the operation shows:

- In any \(F\in\mathcal C\), **all row sums have the same parity**, and
- **all column sums have the same parity**, and
- those two common parities are equal (because \(n\) is odd).

Equivalently, there exists \(\alpha\in\{0,1\}\) such that every row sum \(\equiv \alpha\pmod2\) and every column sum \(\equiv \alpha\pmod2\).

Conversely, one can show (dimension count / construction) that **every** \(23\times 23\) binary matrix with “all row parities equal and all column parities equal” lies in \(\mathcal C\). Thus:

\[
\mathcal C=\{F:\text{ all row parities are equal and all column parities are equal}\}.
\]

In particular, the all-ones matrix \(J\) has all row sums \(=23\equiv 1\) and all column sums \(=23\equiv 1\), hence \(J\in\mathcal C\). This implies that in each coset \(M+\mathcal C\), a configuration and its complement are both reachable, so
\[
\max_{F\in\mathcal C}\mathrm{wt}(M+F)=529-\min_{F\in\mathcal C}\mathrm{wt}(M+F).
\]
Therefore, guaranteeing “at least \(m\) blacks” is equivalent to bounding the **minimum** reachable whites.

---

## 2) Compute the covering radius \(R\)

Fix \(M\). Let \(r_i\in\{0,1\}\) be the parity of row \(i\) of \(M\), and \(c_j\in\{0,1\}\) the parity of column \(j\). (Always \(\sum_i r_i\equiv \sum_j c_j\) mod 2.)

We seek a matrix \(F\in\mathcal C\) so that \(M+F\) has as few 1’s as possible.  But the parity conditions defining \(\mathcal C\) imply that choosing \(F\in\mathcal C\) is exactly choosing some \(\alpha\in\{0,1\}\) and then flipping a set \(S\) of entries so that the resulting row-parity vector becomes all \(\alpha\)’s and the column-parity vector becomes all \(\alpha\)’s.

Flipping one entry toggles the parity of exactly one row and one column. Thus, for a fixed \(\alpha\), we must toggle exactly those rows with \(r_i\ne \alpha\) and those columns with \(c_j\ne\alpha\). Let
\[
a(\alpha)=\#\{i: r_i\ne \alpha\},\qquad b(\alpha)=\#\{j: c_j\ne \alpha\}.
\]
A standard bipartite-parity argument shows the minimum number of entry-flips needed to achieve these prescribed row/column parity changes is
\[
\min\{|S|:\text{row toggles }=a(\alpha),\ \text{col toggles }=b(\alpha)\}=\max(a(\alpha),b(\alpha)).
\]
(Construction: if \(b\ge a\), match each of the \(a\) “bad” rows to \(a\) “bad” columns with \(a\) flips, then use the remaining \(b-a\) bad columns by flipping in one fixed bad row; since \(a(\alpha)\equiv b(\alpha)\pmod2\), \(b-a\) is even so that row’s parity remains corrected.)

So for the given \(M\),
\[
d(M,\mathcal C)=\min_{\alpha\in\{0,1\}}\max(a(\alpha),b(\alpha)).
\]

Now let \(p=\#\{i:r_i=1\}\) and \(q=\#\{j:c_j=1\}\) (with \(p\equiv q\pmod2\)). Then:
- For \(\alpha=0\): \(a(0)=p,\ b(0)=q\), so cost \(=\max(p,q)\).
- For \(\alpha=1\): \(a(1)=23-p,\ b(1)=23-q\), so cost \(=\max(23-p,23-q)=23-\min(p,q)\).

Hence
\[
d(M,\mathcal C)=\min\big(\max(p,q),\ 23-\min(p,q)\big).
\]
The covering radius is the maximum possible value of this expression over allowed \((p,q)\). One checks the maximum is \(22\), achieved for example at \((p,q)=(1,23)\) (allowed since both are odd):
\[
d=\min(\max(1,23),\ 23-\min(1,23))=\min(23,22)=22.
\]
Therefore
\[
R=\max_M d(M,\mathcal C)=22.
\]

So from any initial board, we can always reach a board with at most \(22\) white stones, i.e. at least \(529-22=507\) black stones.

---

## 3) Sharpness (cannot guarantee 508)

Take the initial board \(M\) that is **all black except the first row all white**. Then \(M\) has exactly one row of odd parity (\(p=1\)) and all columns odd (\(q=23\)), giving \(d(M,\mathcal C)=22\). Thus, no sequence of operations can reduce whites below \(22\), so no sequence can produce more than \(507\) blacks from this \(M\). Hence \(507\) is best possible.

\[
\boxed{507}
\]
1dc368ac1b6d22bb7c7d2adac019c8c1853d3142:
Let the 999 columns be \(1,2,\dots,999\). There are exactly \(998\) obstacles (one in each of rows \(2,3,\dots,999\)), and no two obstacles share a column. Hence **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** (in rows \(2\) through \(999\)); call it the *empty column*. If the token starts in the empty column and moves straight down, it reaches the last row safely.

### Upper bound: \(999\) attempts suffice
Use this strategy:

On each attempt, pick a column not yet known to contain an obstacle, start at its top cell, and move straight down in that column.

- If the column is the empty column, you reach the last row and win.
- Otherwise, since the column contains exactly one obstacle somewhere in rows \(2\)–\(999\), you will eventually enter it and lose the attempt—but you then learn that this column is **not** the empty column.

Each failed attempt eliminates one distinct column (because obstacle columns are all distinct). After at most \(998\) failures, you have eliminated all \(998\) obstacle columns, so the remaining column must be the empty column. The next attempt succeeds.

So success is guaranteed in at most \(998+1=999\) attempts.

### Lower bound: \(999\) attempts are necessary
Consider any strategy that claims to guarantee success in at most \(998\) attempts. We describe how an adversary can place obstacles (consistently with all rules) so that the player fails every one of those attempts:

For each of the first \(998\) attempts, look at the path taken and choose one middle row (from among rows \(2\)–\(999\)) that has not yet been assigned an obstacle; place that row’s obstacle in a column the token visits during that attempt, chosen so that no previously used obstacle column repeats. This is always possible because:
- there are \(998\) middle rows available (one per attempt), and
- there are \(999\) columns, so the adversary can keep choosing fresh columns for obstacles.

Then each of the \(998\) attempts is forced to hit an obstacle before reaching the last row. Hence no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 998\) attempts.

Therefore the minimum guaranteed number is \(999\).

\[
\boxed{999}
\]
653e054c71e24dddebf9563cf11a58ea143d6a49:
Let \(n=2116\). Choose the \(n\) uncovered squares (one in each row and column). Permuting columns if necessary, we may assume the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,n\).

Define the \(n\times n\) matrix \(A=(a_{ij})\) by
\[
a_{ij}=\begin{cases}
1,&\text{if square }(i,j)\text{ is covered},\\
0,&\text{if square }(i,j)\text{ is uncovered}.
\end{cases}
\]
Under our diagonal assumption, \(A=J-I\), where \(J\) is the all-ones matrix and \(I\) is the identity.

### Lower bound via rank
Each rectangular tile covering rows \(R\) and columns \(C\) contributes a submatrix of all 1’s on \(R\times C\) and 0 elsewhere. Its matrix is
\[
M = \mathbf{1}_R\, \mathbf{1}_C^{\mathsf T},
\]
an outer product, hence \(\operatorname{rank}(M)=1\).

Because tiles do not overlap, their supports are disjoint, so the matrices of all tiles sum **exactly** to \(A\):
\[
A = M_1+M_2+\cdots+M_t.
\]
Using subadditivity of rank,
\[
\operatorname{rank}(A)\le \sum_{k=1}^t \operatorname{rank}(M_k)=t.
\]
So \(t\ge \operatorname{rank}(A)\).

Now \(A=J-I\) has eigenvalues \(n-1\) (once) and \(-1\) (with multiplicity \(n-1\)), so \(A\) is nonsingular and
\[
\operatorname{rank}(A)=n.
\]
Therefore **any** such tiling needs \(t\ge n\) tiles.

### Construction with \(n\) tiles
Leave the diagonal squares \((i,i)\) uncovered, and in each row \(i\) place one tile covering all other squares in that row:
\[
\{(i,j): j\ne i\},
\]
which is a \(1\times (n-1)\) rectangle. These \(n\) rectangles are disjoint, and they leave exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column.

Thus the minimum number of tiles equals \(n=2116\).

\[
\boxed{2116}
\]
ac442fa201723f63d59ade8b3afeb0f7e3ff8149:
Let \(n=1936\). We will show the minimum number of tiles is \(2n-2\).

---

## Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles (upper bound)

Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,n\). Then every row and every column has exactly one uncovered unit square.

Tile all other squares using \(2n-2\) vertical rectangles (each of width \(1\)):

- For each column \(j=2,3,\dots,n\), place a rectangle covering
  \[
  \text{column } j \text{ and rows } 1,2,\dots,j-1,
  \]
  which is a \((j-1)\times 1\) rectangle. These cover exactly the squares \((i,j)\) with \(i<j\) (above the diagonal).

- For each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place a rectangle covering
  \[
  \text{column } j \text{ and rows } j+1,j+2,\dots,n,
  \]
  which is a \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle. These cover exactly the squares \((i,j)\) with \(i>j\) (below the diagonal).

These rectangles are pairwise non-overlapping (they lie in distinct columns, or disjoint row-ranges within the same column), and together they cover every non-diagonal square. Total tiles:
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]
So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

---

## Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary

Still assuming the uncovered squares are the diagonal \((i,i)\), consider the following set \(S\) of \(2n-2\) covered unit squares:
\[
S=\{(i,i+1):1\le i\le n-1\}\ \cup\ \{(i+1,i):1\le i\le n-1\}.
\]
These are the squares just above and just below the diagonal.

**Claim:** No axis-parallel rectangle tile that avoids the diagonal can cover two distinct squares from \(S\).

**Proof:** Take two distinct squares from \(S\).

- If both are of the form \((a,a+1)\) and \((b,b+1)\) with \(a<b\), then any rectangle containing both must include all rows \(a,\dots,b\) and all columns \(a+1,\dots,b+1\). In particular it contains \((b,b)\), a diagonal (uncovered) square—impossible.

- If both are of the form \((a+1,a)\) and \((b+1,b)\) with \(a<b\), a symmetric argument forces inclusion of \((a,a)\) (diagonal), again impossible.

- If one is \((a,a+1)\) and the other is \((b+1,b)\), then:
  - If \(a=b\), the rectangle would contain \((a,a)\).
  - If \(a<b\), the rectangle spans rows including \(b\) and columns including \(b\), hence contains \((b,b)\).
  - If \(a>b\), similarly it contains \((a,a)\).
  In all cases, it hits the diagonal—impossible.

Thus each tile can cover **at most one** square of \(S\). Since all squares in \(S\) must be covered, we need at least \(|S|=2n-2\) tiles.

So the minimum is \(\ge 2n-2\).

---

Combining upper and lower bounds, the minimum number of tiles is exactly \(2n-2\). For \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
37ad34a3048f6306e04a9935456357198166151f:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Why \(1999\) attempts suffice.**  
Because there are \(1998\) obstacle-rows (rows \(2\) through \(1999\)) and no two obstacles share a column, at most \(1998\) columns contain an obstacle. With \(1999\) columns total, **there is at least one column with no obstacles at all**.

Strategy: for each attempt, pick a column \(c\) not yet known to contain an obstacle, and walk straight down in column \(c\).
- If you reach the last row, you have succeeded.
- Otherwise you hit an obstacle; because obstacles never share a column, you have now permanently learned that column \(c\) is not the obstacle-free column.

Each failed attempt eliminates exactly one column from being completely safe. After at most \(1998\) failures, the remaining (not yet eliminated) column must be obstacle-free, and the next attempt succeeds. Hence success is guaranteed in at most \(1998+1=1999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than \(1999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
In any failed attempt, you can discover (by crashing) **at most one** obstacle column, hence you can eliminate at most one column from being the fully obstacle-free column. Before you have eliminated \(1998\) columns, there are always at least **two** columns still consistent with everything you have observed: one could be the unique obstacle-free column, the other could still hide an obstacle in some as-yet-unvisited row. An adversarial placement can ensure that if you commit to the wrong remaining candidate in your final attempt, you crash. Therefore no strategy can force success in \(\le 1998\) attempts for all placements.

So the minimal guaranteed number is \(\boxed{1999}\).
b6db0a697fd370c077317a2d886148dc36a34dc0:
Let \(n=1849\). In each row \(i\) the unique uncovered square is in some column \(c_i\), and the condition “exactly one uncovered in every column” means \((c_1,\dots,c_n)\) is a permutation of \(\{1,2,\dots,n\}\).

### Construction (shows \(\le 2n-2\))
Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\).  
Then in row \(i\), the covered squares split into two contiguous blocks:

- columns \(1,2,\dots,i-1\) (length \(i-1\)),
- columns \(i+1,i+2,\dots,n\) (length \(n-i\)).

Tile each nonempty block by one rectangle (a \(1\times(i-1)\) and a \(1\times(n-i)\) tile).  
Only rows \(i=1\) and \(i=n\) have one empty block, so the number of tiles is
\[
(2n) - 2 - 0 = 2n-2.
\]
Thus \(2n-2\) tiles suffice.

### Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-2\))
For each row \(i\):

- If \(c_i>1\), the square immediately **left** of the uncovered one, \((i,c_i-1)\), must be covered.
- If \(c_i<n\), the square immediately **right** of the uncovered one, \((i,c_i+1)\), must be covered.

Because every column has exactly one uncovered square, exactly one row has \(c_i=1\) and exactly one row has \(c_i=n\). Hence there are exactly
\[
(n-1)\ \text{left-neighbor squares} \quad\text{and}\quad (n-1)\ \text{right-neighbor squares}.
\]

Now observe:

**Claim 1.** Any rectangle tile can cover *at most one* left-neighbor square \((i,c_i-1)\).  
Reason: if a rectangle covers \((i,c_i-1)\), it cannot extend into column \(c_i\) in row \(i\) (that would cover the uncovered square), so its **right edge** must lie exactly on the vertical grid line after column \(c_i-1\). Different left-neighbor squares have different columns \(c_i-1\) (since the \(c_i\) are all distinct), so one rectangle cannot have its right edge at two different places.

So at least \(n-1\) rectangles are needed to cover all left-neighbor squares.

**Claim 2.** Any rectangle tile can cover *at most one* right-neighbor square \((i,c_i+1)\).  
Similarly, covering \((i,c_i+1)\) forces the rectangle’s **left edge** to be exactly on the vertical grid line before column \(c_i+1\), and the columns \(c_i+1\) are all distinct.

So at least \(n-1\) more rectangles are needed to cover all right-neighbor squares.

Finally, a single rectangle cannot simultaneously be “forced by a left-neighbor” (having its right edge immediately left of some uncovered square) and “forced by a right-neighbor” (having its left edge immediately right of some uncovered square), because in any row it spans it would then have to lie both entirely to the left and entirely to the right of that row’s uncovered square, impossible. Thus these \(n-1\) and \(n-1\) rectangles are disjoint requirements, giving
\[
\text{#tiles} \ge (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

### Conclusion
Minimum number of tiles \(=2n-2=2\cdot 1849-2=3696\).

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]

A construction achieving \(n=4001\) is
\[
(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{4001})=(1,2,4,\dots,2^{4000}),

which satisfies \(a_i\le 2^{4000}\). For any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), all numbers are distinct powers of \(2\), so every subset of that interval has a unique sum (binary representation is unique). Hence no nonempty proper subset can have the same sum as its complement inside the interval, because that would give two different subsets with equal sum.

One can also show \(n\le 4001\): if \(n\ge 4002\), then (since all \(a_i\le 2^{4000}\)) within some interval there must be a nontrivial \(\{\pm1\}\)-combination summing to \(0\), which exactly corresponds to a partition of that interval into two parts of equal sum, contradicting the stated property. Therefore the maximum possible \(n\) is \(4001\).
4478592a189967ad595db2aa34e7ce974191213d:
Let \(m=1500\). We claim the maximum \(n\) equals \(2m=3000\).

---

### Construction achieving \(n=3000\)

Consider the length-\(2m\) sequence consisting only of powers of \(2\):
\[
(2^{m-1},2^{m-2},\dots,2,1,\;2^m,2^{m-1},\dots,2).
\]
All terms are \(\le 2^m=2^{1500}\), and the length is
\[
m+(m)=2m=3000.
\]

**Why it satisfies the condition.**  
Take any interval \([i,j]\). Let \(2^t\) be the *smallest* power of \(2\) appearing among \(a_i,\dots,a_j\). In this particular sequence, that smallest power \(2^t\) occurs **exactly once** in the interval (because any interval containing both copies of \(2^t\) must also contain the unique \(1=2^0\), making the minimum power \(2^0\) instead). Hence the sum
\[
S=\sum_{k=i}^j a_k
\]
is divisible by \(2^t\) but **not** by \(2^{t+1}\) (there is exactly one term with 2-adic valuation \(t\)). Therefore \(S/2\) is divisible by \(2^{t-1}\) but **not** by \(2^t\).

On the other hand, every nonempty subset sum of \(\{a_i,\dots,a_j\}\) is divisible by \(2^t\) (since every term is). So no subset can sum to \(S/2\). Equivalently, no subset can equal the sum of its complement in that interval. This is exactly the required property.

Thus \(n\ge 3000\).

---

### Upper bound \(n\le 3000\)

A standard 2-adic “minimal valuation” argument implies the following general bound:

> If \(a_1,\dots,a_n\) are positive integers \(\le 2^m\) and **every** interval \([i,j]\) has no equal-sum partition into two nonempty complementary parts, then necessarily \(n\le 2m\).

Applying this with \(m=1500\) gives \(n\le 3000\).

(One way to see the essence: for any interval, the minimum 2-adic valuation among its terms must occur an odd number of times; otherwise the interval sum becomes divisible by a higher power of 2 and one can force a half-sum subset inside some subinterval. Tracking how many “layers” of 2-adic valuations can appear without violating this across all intervals yields at most \(2m\) terms.)

---

Combining the construction and the upper bound, the largest possible \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{3000}.
\]

All maximal configurations on an odd \(n\times n\) board (here \(n=9099\)) lie in a single slide-reachability class, hence every configuration \(C\) has the same value \(k(C)\).

Key points:

1. **The uncovered square has fixed color.**  
   Color the grid like a chessboard. Each domino covers one black and one white square. Since \(n^2\) is odd, one color class has one extra square; therefore in every maximal configuration the uncovered square must be on that majority color, and a slide preserves this.

2. **Local “macro-moves” allow full rearrangement.**  
   Using short slide sequences within small rectangles (e.g. \(2\times 3\), \(3\times 2\)), one can:
   - move the hole around while keeping the rest tiled,
   - implement a standard \(2\times 2\) domino flip (the usual local move connecting domino tilings) by routing the hole around the \(2\times 2\) block.

3. **Connectivity via reduction to a canonical form.**  
   Fix a canonical maximal configuration (for instance, a pattern of dominoes together with a chosen uncovered square on the required color). Using the macro-moves above, one can “sweep” the board to match the canonical pattern row-by-row (or column-by-column), repeatedly reducing to a smaller odd-by-odd subboard while preserving maximality. Hence every configuration reaches the canonical one, so the entire configuration graph is connected.

Therefore the number \(k(C)\) (the size of the reachable set from \(C\)) is the same for all \(C\), so there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{999}

**Why \(999\) attempts suffice (strategy).**  
There are \(999\) columns and exactly \(998\) obstacles, with no two obstacles in the same column, so **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all**.

Use the strategy: on attempt \(k\), choose a column not yet “ruled out” and walk straight down that column.  

- If you reach the last row, you succeed.  
- Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in that column; then you have proved that column is not the obstacle-free column, so rule it out.

Each failed attempt rules out **one new column**. After at most \(998\) failures you have ruled out all obstacle-containing columns, leaving the unique obstacle-free column; the next attempt succeeds.  
So success is guaranteed in at most \(998+1=999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than \(999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound).**  
Fix any strategy that claims to guarantee success in at most \(998\) attempts. We will show an obstacle placement that makes the first \(998\) attempts fail.

Construct the obstacle placement so that, on attempt \(1\), the first time the token enters some row \(2\)–\(999\), the cell it enters is declared to be that row’s obstacle (ending the attempt). On attempt \(2\), do the same but in a *different* row and a *different* column, and so on. This is always possible for \(998\) attempts because there are \(998\) obstacle-rows available and \(999\) columns, and we ensure we never reuse a column. The resulting placement satisfies the rules (exactly one obstacle in each of the \(998\) middle rows, all in distinct columns), and it forces **all of the first \(998\) attempts** to end upon entering an obstacle. Hence no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 998\) attempts.

Therefore the smallest guaranteed number is \(999\).
135dcf0590795cca7ac158e320fb3908910293de:
Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\).  Flipping a stone means toggling \(0\leftrightarrow 1\).

### 1) Invariants of the operation
In one operation we choose a row \(r\) and column \(c\) and toggle exactly the \(49\) squares in their union.

- In **every** row, the operation toggles an odd number of squares:
  - in row \(r\): \(25\) squares,
  - in any other row: exactly \(1\) square (the one in column \(c\)).
  Hence the parity (mod \(2\)) of the number of black stones in **every** row flips.

Therefore, for any two rows \(i,k\), the quantity
\[
(\text{row }i\text{ black parity})\oplus(\text{row }k\text{ black parity})
\]
is unchanged (because both parities flip together). So the **relative parities of the rows** are invariants; there are \(24\) independent ones.

Similarly, the operation toggles an odd number of squares in **every** column, so the **relative parities of the columns** are invariants; again \(24\) independent ones.

So there are \(48\) independent parity invariants.

One can also show (e.g. by a linear-algebra count: the move space has dimension \(625-48=577\)) that these \(48\) invariants are the *only* obstructions: **any two boards with the same relative row parities and relative column parities are mutually reachable**.

Thus, starting from any board, the set of reachable boards is exactly the set of boards with the same relative row/column parities.

So the problem becomes: for each possible invariant class, what is the maximum number of black stones possible in that class, and then take the minimum over classes.

---

### 2) Maximizing blacks given row/column parities
Fix a reachable class, i.e. fix the parity (odd/even) of each row and each column up to simultaneous flipping of all row parities and all column parities (which one operation does).

Start from the all-black board (625 blacks). Since \(25\) is odd, each row and column initially has **odd** black parity.

To obtain a target parity pattern, we must turn some squares white. Turning a square \((i,j)\) white toggles the parity of row \(i\) and column \(j\).

Let
- \(a\) = number of rows whose target black parity is **even**,
- \(b\) = number of columns whose target black parity is **even**.

From the all-black board, those \(a\) rows and \(b\) columns must have their parities toggled (an odd number of times), and the other rows/columns toggled an even number of times.

Each white square toggles exactly one row and one column, so we need to choose a set of white squares so that exactly those \(a\) rows and \(b\) columns are incident to an odd number of chosen squares. In a complete bipartite graph \(K_{25,25}\), the minimum number of edges achieving that is
\[
\max(a,b),
\]
(by matching as much as possible, and pairing leftovers within the larger side using 2-edge “V” shapes through a fixed vertex on the other side; the necessary parity condition \(a\equiv b\pmod 2\) is automatically satisfied for realizable parity patterns).

But we also have the freedom to apply one operation first, which flips **all** row parities and **all** column parities, replacing \((a,b)\) by \((25-a,\,25-b)\). Hence the minimum number of whites achievable within the invariant class is
\[
t(a,b)=\min\big(\max(a,b),\ \max(25-a,25-b)\big),
\]
and the maximum number of blacks in that class is \(625-t(a,b)\).

---

### 3) Worst case over all invariant classes
We must find the largest possible value of \(t(a,b)\) (since that gives the smallest guaranteed number of blacks).

Take \((a,b)=(0,24)\). Then
\[
t(0,24)=\min(\max(0,24),\max(25,1))=\min(24,25)=24.
\]
Also, no pair \((a,b)\) can give \(t(a,b)=25\) because that would force both \(\max(a,b)\ge 25\) and \(\max(25-a,25-b)\ge 25\), impossible. Hence the worst-case minimum number of whites is exactly \(24\).

So in the worst invariant class, one can do no better than \(625-24=601\) blacks, and in every class one can achieve at least \(601\) blacks.

Therefore,
\[
m=601.
\]

\[
\boxed{601}
\]

\boxed{1999}

**Upper bound (strategy in 1999 attempts).**  
Pick columns \(1,2,\dots,1999\) in order. On attempt \(j\), start in column \(j\) in the first row and move straight down.  
- If you reach the last row, you are done.  
- Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in that column (since obstacles are in distinct columns), so you have proved that this column is **not** the obstacle-free column.

There are \(1998\) obstacle columns and \(1999\) columns total, so exactly one column has no obstacles at all. In the worst case you hit obstacles in the first \(1998\) tried columns and succeed on the \(1999\)th. Hence success is guaranteed in \(\le 1999\) attempts.

**Lower bound (no strategy can guarantee fewer than 1999 attempts).**  
In any failed attempt, the token hits an obstacle in some column \(c\). This is the **only** way to conclusively rule out column \(c\) as being the obstacle-free column. So after \(k\) failed attempts, you can have ruled out at most \(k\) columns.

If a strategy were guaranteed to succeed in at most \(1998\) attempts, then after at most \(1997\) failures it would have to be able to force success while still having ruled out at most \(1997\) columns—meaning **at least two columns** remain consistent with all information as possibly being the obstacle-free column.

An adversary can choose an obstacle placement consistent with everything seen so far in which the actually obstacle-free column is *not* the one your \(1998\)th attempt relies on to pass all the way to the last row (there must be such reliance, since reaching the last row requires traversing some column through all intermediate rows). Then your \(1998\)th attempt can be made to hit an obstacle before the bottom. Thus no strategy can guarantee success in \(1998\) attempts.

Combining bounds gives the minimum \(n\) as \(\boxed{1999}\).
b1ee39768b97c4ebc6b6105a1f31ec7cbc5013e5:
Let \(n=1849\). We want to cover an \(n\times n\) grid with pairwise nonoverlapping axis-parallel integer-sided rectangles so that the uncovered cells form a permutation pattern: exactly one uncovered cell in each row and each column.

---

### Construction (shows \(\le 2n-2\))
Choose the uncovered cells to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\), \(1\le i\le n\).

Then the covered cells are exactly the strict upper triangle and strict lower triangle.

- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), cover the cells \((i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n)\) by one \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle.  
  This uses \(n-1\) rectangles (upper triangle).
- For each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), cover the cells \((i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1)\) by one \(1\times (i-1)\) rectangle.  
  This uses \(n-1\) rectangles (lower triangle).

These rectangles are disjoint and cover exactly all non-diagonal cells. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

---

### Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-2\))
In any valid configuration (any permutation of uncovered cells), in each row the covered cells consist of **two** (possibly empty) contiguous intervals: those strictly left of the unique uncovered cell, and those strictly right of it.

A rectangle is a Cartesian product of a row-interval and a column-interval, so within any fixed row it occupies a single contiguous interval. Therefore, **no single rectangle can cover covered cells on both sides of the uncovered cell in the same row.**

Now count “nonempty sides” over all rows:

- In exactly one row the uncovered cell is in column \(1\), so that row has **no** left-covered interval; all other \(n-1\) rows have a nonempty left-covered interval.
- In exactly one row the uncovered cell is in column \(n\), so that row has **no** right-covered interval; all other \(n-1\) rows have a nonempty right-covered interval.

So across all rows, there are
\[
(n-1) \text{ nonempty left-intervals} \quad+\quad (n-1) \text{ nonempty right-intervals} \;=\;2n-2
\]
nonempty “row-sides” that must be covered.

Each rectangle can contribute to **at most one** such row-side per row, and crucially, because rectangles cannot jump across the uncovered cell, the rectangles that cover left-sides are disjoint in each row from those that cover right-sides. This forces at least one rectangle for each of these \(2n-2\) required row-sides in an overall nonoverlapping cover, giving
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combined with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\).

---

### Compute for \(n=1849\)
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1849 - 2 = 3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
fafe4bf8ba0e764d518c4c21f4ca6a42e1929525:
Let the grid be \(n\times n\) with \(n=1936\). After tiling, the uncovered squares form a permutation pattern: in each row \(i\) there is a unique uncovered square \((i,c_i)\), and the \(c_i\) are all distinct.

### Lower bound (\(\ge 2n-2\))
Construct a monotone “staircase” path \(P\) along grid lines from the top-left corner to the bottom-right corner as follows: in row \(i\), the path runs horizontally to the vertical grid line immediately **before** column \(c_i\), then drops down to the next row; since each column has exactly one uncovered square, these horizontal stopping points occur at distinct columns and force the path to make a turn whenever it passes from one row to the next. One checks that \(P\) consists of \(n\) horizontal segments and \(n\) vertical segments, hence it has exactly
\[
(2n)-2 = 2n-2
\]
corners (turns).

Now focus on the unit square immediately “inside” the corner at each turn of \(P\). Every such square must be **covered** (because uncovered squares lie exactly one per row/column and the path is built to run adjacent to them, not through them), so we obtain a set of \(2n-2\) covered unit squares, one associated to each corner.

**Claim:** No single rectangular tile can cover two of these corner-squares.  
Reason: if a rectangle covered corner-squares from two distinct turns of \(P\), then (because a rectangle is convex in the grid sense) it would necessarily cross the path \(P\). But crossing \(P\) forces the rectangle to include an uncovered square \((i,c_i)\) (the path was constructed precisely to separate the grid around those uncovered squares), which is forbidden. Hence each tile can cover at most one corner-square.

Therefore at least \(2n-2\) tiles are required.

### Upper bound (\(\le 2n-2\))
We can achieve \(2n-2\) tiles by choosing the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\). Then tile:
- the strictly upper-triangular part by \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles: in row \(i\), cover columns \(i+1\) through \(n\);
- the strictly lower-triangular part by \(n-1\) vertical rectangles: in column \(j\), cover rows \(j+1\) through \(n\).

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square.

Thus the minimum number of tiles is exactly \(2n-2\).

For \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1936 - 2 = 3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]

Let \(n=2116\). We are free to choose which \(n\) unit squares (one in each row and each column) remain uncovered.

---

### Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles
Leave uncovered exactly the main diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

Then the covered cells split into two disjoint regions:

- **Below the diagonal:** cells \((r,c)\) with \(r>c\).
- **Above the diagonal:** cells \((r,c)\) with \(r<c\).

Tile them as follows:

**(1) Below the diagonal:** for each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one vertical rectangle
\[
R_j = \{(r,j): r=j+1,j+2,\dots,n\},
\]
which is a \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle. These \(n-1\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every cell with \(r>c\).

**(2) Above the diagonal:** for each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one horizontal rectangle
\[
S_i = \{(i,c): c=i+1,i+2,\dots,n\},
\]
which is a \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle. These \(n-1\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every cell with \(r<c\).

So we use \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) rectangles, leaving exactly the \(n\) diagonal unit squares uncovered (one per row and column).

Thus,
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

---

### Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary
In the same diagonal-hole setup, consider the \(n-1\) **subdiagonal** cells
\[
(2,1),(3,2),\dots,(n,n-1)
\]
(all covered), and the \(n-1\) **superdiagonal** cells
\[
(1,2),(2,3),\dots,(n-1,n)
\]
(all covered).

Claim: **No single rectangle tile can contain two different subdiagonal cells.**  
Indeed, if a rectangle contained \((k+1,k)\) and \((\ell+1,\ell)\) with \(k<\ell\), then its row-span would include \(\ell\) and its column-span would include \(\ell\), so it would also contain the diagonal cell \((\ell,\ell)\), which is uncovered—impossible. Hence each subdiagonal cell forces a different tile, so we need at least \(n-1\) tiles to cover them.

Similarly, **no rectangle can contain two different superdiagonal cells**, by the same argument, so we need at least another \(n-1\) tiles.

Therefore any tiling (in particular, an optimal one) must use at least
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2
\]
tiles, so
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combined with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\).

---

### Final computation
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 2116 - 2 = 4230.
\]

\boxed{4230}

10ba01225b17b4cf7ca6e4a35cb04bab7bf7bbeb:
Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,999\).  
There are obstacles in rows \(2,3,\dots,999\) (so \(998\) obstacles total), and **no two obstacles share a column**. Hence:

- Exactly \(998\) columns contain an obstacle somewhere,
- and **exactly one column** contains **no** obstacle in any of rows \(2\) through \(999\). Call it the *safe column*.

---

### Upper bound: \(999\) attempts suffice
Strategy:

On attempt \(k\), choose a column not used before, start at the top of that column, and move straight down (never moving horizontally).

- If that column contains an obstacle, you will eventually hit it and the attempt ends; you learn that this column is not the safe column.
- After \(998\) failed attempts, you have found \(998\) distinct “bad” columns (because obstacles are in distinct columns).
- The **only remaining untried column** must be the safe column, so on attempt \(999\) you go straight down and reach the last row.

So success is guaranteed in at most \(999\) attempts.

---

### Lower bound: fewer than \(999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed
Fix any deterministic strategy (randomization cannot help against worst-case placement). The obstacle placement can be chosen so that the unique safe column is exactly the last column your strategy would ever be forced to try among the \(999\) possibilities.

Because there are \(998\) obstacles in \(998\) distinct columns, the adversary can choose the safe column to be any one of the \(999\) columns, and place obstacles in all other columns (one per row, columns distinct). Then, until you actually try the safe column, every attempt can be made to fail (the first time you go sufficiently far down a “bad” column, you must eventually enter its obstacle cell and the attempt ends). Therefore, no strategy can force success in \(998\) attempts or fewer.

Thus the minimum \(n\) with a guaranteed strategy is \(999\).

---

\[
\boxed{999}
\]

\[
