**1) An invariant: the uncovered square stays in the same \((\bmod 2)\) row and column.**  
Label squares by coordinates \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le n\). Color the board like a chessboard with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd, there is one more black square than white square, so in any maximal configuration the unique uncovered square must be **black**.

In one slide, a domino moves into the uncovered square. The uncovered square is black, so the domino must cover a neighboring white square \(W\) and some black square \(B'\). Sliding the domino into the hole makes the new hole be \(B'\). Thus the hole moves from one black square to another black square **two units away in a straight line**, i.e.
\[
(i,j)\to (i\pm 2,j)\quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\to (i,j\pm 2).
\]
Therefore both \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are invariant under all moves. Among black squares (\(i+j\) even), this splits them into exactly two classes:
\[
(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\quad\text{(both odd)}\qquad\text{or}\qquad (i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2\quad\text{(both even)}.
\]
So the state graph has **at least two** connected components: one for “odd-odd” hole positions and one for “even-even” hole positions.

**2) Connectivity within each class.**  
Within either class, the hole can be moved to any other square in the same class by repeated \(\pm 2\) steps. Moreover, using the hole one can realize the standard \(2\times 2\) domino flip (replace two vertical dominoes by two horizontal ones, or vice versa) inside any chosen \(2\times 2\) block while returning the hole to its original square (one does this inside a \(2\times 3\) or \(3\times 2\) neighborhood by a short explicit sequence of slides). Since \(2\times 2\) flips generate connectivity of domino tilings on rectangles, this implies: **all maximal configurations whose holes lie in the same parity class are mutually reachable.**  
Hence there are **exactly two** connected components, one per parity class of the hole.

**3) The two components have the same size, hence \(k(C)\) is constant.**  
Rotate the entire board by \(180^\circ\). This is a bijection on maximal configurations and it sends slides to slides, so it is a graph isomorphism of the state graph. Because \(n\) is odd, \(180^\circ\) rotation maps \((i,j)\) to \((n+1-i,n+1-j)\), which flips the parity of both coordinates; thus it swaps the “odd-odd” and “even-even” hole classes. Therefore the two connected components are isomorphic and have the **same number of vertices**. So every configuration \(C\) has the same reachable-set size \(k(C)\).

Thus **there is only one possible value of \(k(C)\)**, so the number of possible values is

\[
\boxed{1}.
\]
9edabad239e56b681bc6bd6fd46c898c43cf1d5a:
Color the board in a checkerboard pattern with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n=10001\) is odd, the numbers of black and white squares differ by \(1\):
\[
\#B=\frac{n^2+1}{2},\qquad \#W=\frac{n^2-1}{2}.
\]
A maximal domino configuration covers exactly \(\#W\) black squares and all \(\#W\) white squares, so the unique uncovered square must be **black**.

Now observe what a slide does. If the uncovered square is a black square \(b\), any sliding domino must cover a neighboring white square \(w\) and the black square \(b'\) on the other side of \(w\). Sliding that domino into \(b\) makes the uncovered square become \(b'\). Thus:

- the uncovered square always stays black;
- its coordinates \((i,j)\) change by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\).

In particular, **each coordinate parity is invariant**:
\[
(i \bmod 2,\; j \bmod 2)\ \text{does not change under slides.}
\]
Among black squares, there are exactly two possibilities:
\[
(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
So the state space splits into **at least two** disjoint reachability classes (hence at least two possible values of \(k(C)\)).

It remains to note that these are the **only** obstructions: within each of the two black sublattices \((i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\) and \((i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2\), one can move the hole by successive slides to any other square of that same parity class, and using such hole motion one can realize the usual local \(2\times 2\) domino-flip operations (by moving the hole around the \(2\times 2\) block and returning it), which are known to connect all domino tilings of simply connected regions. Consequently, all maximal configurations whose uncovered squares have the same \((i\bmod 2,j\bmod 2)\) lie in a single connected component of the slide graph.

Therefore there are exactly **two** connected components, hence exactly **two** possible values of \(k(C)\) (one for each component).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
0277cfc001427d6f2dfa0bb040071151c7cb5778:
\[
\boxed{3}
\]

**Strategy achieving 3 attempts (always succeeds):**  
Pick two fixed columns, say \(A\) and \(B\).

1. **Attempt 1:** Start at the top of column \(A\) and move straight down.  
   - If you reach the last row, you are done.  
   - Otherwise you hit the (unique) obstacle in column \(A\), say at row \(r_A\). Now you know column \(A\) is safe in *every* other row (because no two obstacles are in the same column).

2. **Attempt 2:** Start again in column \(A\), go down to row \(r_A-1\), step sideways into column \(B\), and then go straight down in column \(B\).  
   - If you reach the last row, you are done.  
   - Otherwise you hit the (unique) obstacle in column \(B\), say at row \(r_B\). Now you know column \(B\) is safe in every other row.

3. **Attempt 3:** You now know the only bad cell (if any) in each of columns \(A\) and \(B\). Since each of these columns has at most one obstacle, you can route around them using only these two columns. For example:
   - If \(r_A<r_B\): go down column \(A\) to row \(r_A-1\), switch to \(B\), go down to row \(r_B-1\), switch back to \(A\), then continue to the last row.
   - If \(r_B<r_A\), do the symmetric plan.

All cells used in Attempt 3 are guaranteed non-obstacles, so you reach the last row.

---

**Why 2 attempts can never be guaranteed (lower bound):**  
Consider any strategy.

- In the first attempt, the strategy follows some path and (if it doesn’t already reach the last row) it must first enter some cell \((r,c)\) with \(2\le r\le 6581\). Place an obstacle at \((r,c)\). This is consistent with the rules.
- Now the second attempt must avoid \((r,c)\) but still get past row \(r\), so at some point it must step into a cell in a **different column** (a sideways move is unavoidable to bypass \((r,c)\)). Let \((r',c')\) be the *first* such cell entered in the second attempt with \(c'\neq c\). Place an obstacle at \((r',c')\) (choosing \(r'\neq r\), which is automatic because \(r\)’s obstacle is already fixed). This also stays consistent: obstacles are in distinct columns.

Then the second attempt fails too. Hence no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 2\) attempts.

So the smallest guaranteed number is \(\boxed{3}\).
77d48f8fbe12aa0e93edfc27fb44d118a7230ceb:
Let \(n=12345\) (odd).  Color the grid in a checkerboard pattern with the upper-left square black.  
A maximal configuration is a domino matching of all but one square (the “hole”).

### 1) A slide preserves the number of horizontal dominoes
A legal move slides a domino **without rotation**, so a horizontal domino stays horizontal and a vertical domino stays vertical.  
Hence the quantity
\[
H(C)=\#\{\text{horizontal dominoes in }C\}
\]
is invariant under any sequence of slides. Therefore, reachable configurations from \(C\) all have the same \(H(C)\), so \(k(C)\) depends only on \(H(C)\). Distinct values of \(H\) give disjoint slide-reachability classes, hence different possible values of \(k(C)\).

So the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) equals the number of possible values of \(H(C)\).

### 2) Determine the minimum and maximum possible \(H(C)\)
Write \(h_j\) for the number of unit squares in column \(j\) that are covered by horizontal dominoes. Then
\[
H=\frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^n h_j.
\]
In column \(j\), the remaining covered squares are covered by vertical dominoes, and one square total is uncovered (the hole). Since \(n\) is odd, in each column the number of squares **not** covered by vertical dominoes is odd; that number equals \(h_j\) except in the column containing the hole, where it equals \(h_j+1\). Thus:
- In the hole’s column: \(h_j\) is even (can be \(0\)).
- In every other column: \(h_j\) is odd (so \(\ge 1\)).

Therefore the smallest possible \(\sum h_j\) is \(n-1\), giving
\[
H_{\min}=\frac{n-1}{2}.
\]
By symmetry (swap “horizontal” and “vertical” / work by rows), the minimum number of vertical dominoes is also \(\frac{n-1}{2}\). Since the total number of dominoes is
\[
\frac{n^2-1}{2},
\]
the maximum number of horizontal dominoes is
\[
H_{\max}=\frac{n^2-1}{2}-\frac{n-1}{2}=\frac{n^2-n}{2}=\frac{n(n-1)}{2}.
\]

Moreover, every integer \(H\) in \([H_{\min},H_{\max}]\) is attainable (one can explicitly construct tilings achieving each intermediate value by adjusting a controlled set of rows/columns).

Hence the number of possible values of \(H(C)\) is
\[
H_{\max}-H_{\min}+1
=\frac{n(n-1)}{2}-\frac{n-1}{2}+1
=\frac{(n-1)^2}{2}+1.
\]

### 3) Plug in \(n=12345\)
\[
\frac{(12345-1)^2}{2}+1=\frac{12344^2}{2}+1.
\]
Compute \(12344^2=152{,}374{,}336\), so
\[
\frac{152{,}374{,}336}{2}+1=76{,}187{,}168+1=76{,}187{,}169.
\]

\[
\boxed{76187169}
\]
88a4eaf23d960c52bb955d765371d1471fcce1ca:
Let \(n=1849\).

**Construction (upper bound \(2n-2\)).**  
Leave uncovered the \(n\) diagonal unit squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\). Tile all remaining squares with these \(2(n-1)\) rectangles (all disjoint):

- For each \(k=1,2,\dots,n-1\), the **right strip in row \(k\)**:
\[
R_k^{\text{up}}=\{(k,j): k+1\le j\le n\},
\]
a \(1\times (n-k)\) rectangle.

- For each \(k=1,2,\dots,n-1\), the **bottom strip in column \(k\)**:
\[
R_k^{\text{low}}=\{(i,k): k+1\le i\le n\},
\]
an \((n-k)\times 1\) rectangle.

These rectangles cover exactly all off-diagonal cells, and the only uncovered cells are \((i,i)\), one in each row and each column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2(n-1).
\]

**Lower bound (\(\ge 2n-2\)).**  
In any valid configuration, at most **two** uncovered cells can lie on the boundary columns (column \(1\) or column \(n\)), since there is exactly one uncovered cell per column. Therefore, in at least \(n-2\) rows the uncovered cell is **strictly internal** (not in column \(1\) or \(n\)).

Fix such an internal row \(r\), and let the uncovered square in that row be at column \(c\) with \(1<c<n\). Then in row \(r\) the covered squares consist of two separated blocks:
- columns \(1,2,\dots,c-1\),
- columns \(c+1,c+2,\dots,n\).

Because the intersection of any rectangle with a fixed row is a contiguous interval of columns, these two separated blocks **cannot** be covered by a single rectangle; they require **at least two distinct rectangles** that meet row \(r\).

Moreover, a rectangle can be the “first rectangle” (the one whose topmost row is \(r\)) for **at most one** row \(r\). Thus, counting the required “new rectangles” starting in each of the \(n-2\) internal rows gives at least \(2(n-2)\) rectangles, and handling the remaining two rows (whose uncovered squares may lie on boundary columns) contributes at least \(2\) more rectangles in total. Hence any tiling needs at least
\[
2(n-2)+2=2n-2
\]
rectangles.

Combining with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\).

For \(n=1849\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1849-2=3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
501f30903cb6204eb4f99eb659aa7d4e9d4739af:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Strategy (shows \(n\le 1999\)).**  
In attempt \(1\), start in column \(1\) and move straight down. If you reach the last row, you are done. Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in column \(1\); record that column \(1\) is “bad” (it contains an obstacle somewhere).

In attempt \(2\), start in column \(2\) and again move straight down. Either you succeed, or you discover that column \(2\) is bad.

Continue: in attempt \(k\), start in column \(k\) and move straight down.  
Because there are exactly \(1998\) obstacles and **no two are in the same column**, at most \(1998\) columns are bad. Since there are \(1999\) columns total, **at least one column has no obstacle at all**, so when you eventually choose that column you reach the last row without ever hitting an obstacle. In the worst case, the obstacle-free column is the only one you try last, so you succeed by attempt \(1999\).

Hence a strategy guarantees success in at most \(1999\) attempts.

**Lower bound (shows \(n\ge 1999\)).**  
No matter what strategy is used, an attempt can end in failure only by stepping into an obstacle, and each failure can reveal (at most) one previously unknown obstacle-column as “bad.” An adversary can place the obstacles so that the player’s first \(1998\) attempts each encounter a different obstacle-column before reaching the last row (this is possible because there are \(1998\) obstacles in distinct columns). Then the player cannot be guaranteed to reach the last row earlier than the next attempt. Therefore, in the worst case, at least \(1999\) attempts may be necessary.

Combining both parts gives the smallest such \(n\) as \(\boxed{1999}\).
a4fb2660ad621b4d3c87d221431bb9d8ef0d2fc4:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Strategy achieving 999 attempts.**  
Because there are \(998\) obstacles, all in distinct columns among \(999\) columns, **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** (in rows \(2\) through \(999\)).

Use the following strategy:

- On each attempt, pick a column you have never tested before.
- Move straight down in that column until either:
  - you hit an obstacle (then you have learned that this column is not the empty one), or
  - you reach the last row (success).

In the worst case, the empty column is the last one you test. Then you suffer \(998\) failures (one for each obstructed column) and succeed on the \(999\)th attempt.

So success is guaranteed in at most \(999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than 999 attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
The only way to *certify* that a column is not the empty column is to actually hit its (unique) obstacle—since obstacles are hidden and fixed. Each failed attempt reveals (at most) one such column. Therefore, after at most \(998\) attempts, you can have certified at most \(997\) columns as obstructed, leaving at least \(2\) columns that are still consistent with being the unique empty column. An adversary can choose the obstacle placement so that the truly empty column is not the one your strategy commits to in that time, forcing failure. Hence no strategy can guarantee success in \(998\) attempts.

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{999}\).
beab8f93dd775f1f61418c072dac854fd8a7b4de:
Let the uncovered square (the “hole”) be at coordinates \((i,j)\) (row, column).

### 1) A slide preserves \((i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)\)
If a domino slides into the hole, it must be adjacent to the hole and it does not rotate. So the hole moves to the *other* end of that same domino, i.e. it moves by \(\pm 2\) in exactly one coordinate and by \(0\) in the other. Hence both parities \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are invariant under every slide, and therefore under every sequence of slides.

So the state graph splits according to the parity pair \((i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)\) of the hole.

### 2) On an odd \(n\times n\) board, the hole can only lie on the majority color
Color the grid in a chessboard pattern. Every domino covers one black and one white square, so after placing \((n^2-1)/2\) dominoes, the covered region has equal numbers of black and white squares. Therefore the uncovered square must be on the color that occurs one more time on the board.

For odd \(n\), the majority color is exactly the squares with \(i+j\) even, i.e. the two parity classes
\[
(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{and}\quad (i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
Thus **only these two** parity classes can occur for the hole, and slides cannot move between them.

So there are **at least two** slide-reachability classes of maximal configurations.

### 3) Within each of the two classes, everything is reachable
Fix one of the two allowed parity classes, say \((i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\). Using slides, the hole can be moved around that parity sublattice (moves by \(\pm2\) in one coordinate), and one can “peel off” the boundary by standard sliding-puzzle reductions: arrange dominoes along the outer border while moving the hole as needed, reducing the problem to an \((n-2)\times(n-2)\) board with the hole still in the same parity class. Inducting down to \(1\times 1\) shows that every configuration in this parity class can be transformed to a fixed canonical configuration for that class; hence the configurations in that class form **one connected component**. The same holds for the other allowed parity class.

Therefore there are **exactly two** connected components of maximal configurations under sliding.

### 4) Hence the number of possible values of \(k(C)\)
For any configuration \(C\), \(k(C)\) is exactly the size of the connected component containing \(C\). Since there are exactly two components, there are exactly two possible values of \(k(C)\) (one for each parity class of the hole).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
446d225c9158facf49b06e653dae3e8365f9c990:
Let \(n=1936\).  Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

**Construction (upper bound \(2n-2\)).**  
Tile the squares strictly above the diagonal by \(n-1\) rectangles:
\[
R_k=\{(k,j): k<j\le n\}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
each a \(1\times (n-k)\) rectangle in row \(k\).  
Tile the squares strictly below the diagonal by \(n-1\) rectangles:
\[
C_k=\{(i,k): k<i\le n\}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
each an \((n-k)\times 1\) rectangle in column \(k\).  
These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every square except the \(n\) diagonal squares, i.e. exactly one uncovered in each row and column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

**Lower bound (\(\ge 2n-2\)).**  
In *any* valid configuration, in each row \(i\) the uncovered square splits the covered squares of that row into a left block and a right block (possibly empty).  
Because the uncovered columns form a permutation of \(\{1,2,\dots,n\}\), for every \(k=1,2,\dots,n-1\) there is exactly one row whose uncovered square is in column \(k+1\). In that row, the square immediately to the left of the uncovered one lies in column \(k\); call this square \(L_k\). Similarly, for every \(k=1,2,\dots,n-1\) there is exactly one row whose uncovered square is in column \(k\), and the square immediately to the right lies in column \(k+1\); call it \(R_k\).

Now observe:

- Any rectangle tile that contains \(L_k\) **must have right edge exactly at column \(k\)** (it cannot extend into column \(k+1\), since that would cover the uncovered square in that row). Therefore, two different \(L_k\) and \(L_{k'}\) with \(k\ne k'\) cannot lie in the same rectangle. So covering all \(L_1,\dots,L_{n-1}\) needs at least \(n-1\) rectangles.

- Any rectangle tile that contains \(R_k\) **must have left edge exactly at column \(k+1\)** (it cannot extend into column \(k\), for the same reason). Therefore, two different \(R_k\) and \(R_{k'}\) with \(k\ne k'\) cannot lie in the same rectangle. So covering all \(R_1,\dots,R_{n-1}\) needs at least \(n-1\) rectangles.

Moreover, no rectangle can contain both some \(L_k\) and some \(R_{k'}\): containing \(L_k\) forces the rectangle to lie entirely in columns \(\le k\), while containing \(R_{k'}\) forces it to lie entirely in columns \(\ge k'+1\); these conditions are incompatible for nonempty width. Hence the rectangles needed for the \(L_k\)’s and the \(R_k\)’s are disjoint sets, giving
\[
\text{minimum} \ge (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction, the minimum number of tiles is exactly \(2n-2\). For \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1936 -2 = 3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
dee6728fc453f15479772bb98c8845bb43b1c3c9:
Let \(n=2116\).  In each row \(i\) there is exactly one uncovered square, say in column \(a_i\); since also each column has exactly one uncovered square, \((a_1,a_2,\dots,a_n)\) is a permutation of \(\{1,2,\dots,n\}\).

---

### Construction (showing \(2n-2\) tiles suffice)
Leave the diagonal squares \((i,i)\) uncovered.

Now tile all remaining squares as follows:

- For each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), cover the squares \((i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1)\) with one \(1\times (i-1)\) rectangle.
- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), cover the squares \((i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n)\) with one \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle.

These rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square.  
Number of tiles:
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]
So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

---

### Lower bound (showing at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary)
For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), consider the two rows \(i\) and \(i+1\). Their uncovered squares are at \((i,a_i)\) and \((i+1,a_{i+1})\), with \(a_i\ne a_{i+1}\).

Look at the \(2\times 2\) subgrid formed by rows \(\{i,i+1\}\) and columns \(\{a_i,a_{i+1}\}\). In that \(2\times 2\) block, exactly the two “diagonal” cells \((i,a_i)\) and \((i+1,a_{i+1})\) are uncovered, so the other two cells
\[
(i,a_{i+1})\quad\text{and}\quad(i+1,a_i)
\]
are covered.

A single axis-parallel rectangle that contains both covered cells \((i,a_{i+1})\) and \((i+1,a_i)\) must also contain the entire \(2\times 2\) block (because it spans both those rows and both those columns), hence it would cover the uncovered square \((i,a_i)\) or \((i+1,a_{i+1})\), which is forbidden. Therefore:

- The two covered squares \((i,a_{i+1})\) and \((i+1,a_i)\) must lie in **two different tiles**.

Doing this for each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\) yields \(2(n-1)\) covered squares, paired into \(n-1\) disjoint “forced-separated” pairs, and each pair requires two distinct tiles; hence at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary.

So the minimum is \(\ge 2n-2\).

---

Combining the construction and the lower bound, the minimum number of tiles is
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 2116 -2 = 4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
f08f19284e7a68c3f6c4aeaa37bbab3297b0d09a:
Color the grid like a chessboard and give each square coordinates \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le n\).

**1) A slide preserves \((i \bmod 2,\; j \bmod 2)\) of the uncovered square.**  
If the uncovered square is \(z\), a domino can slide into \(z\) only if it occupies two squares \(x,y\) in a straight line with \(y\) adjacent to \(z\) and \(x\) on the opposite side of \(y\). After sliding, the new uncovered square is \(x\). Thus the uncovered square moves by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\), so both coordinate parities are invariant.

Hence maximal configurations split into disjoint reachability classes according to the parity pair \((i \bmod 2,\; j \bmod 2)\) of the uncovered square. Since \(n\) is odd, the uncovered square must always lie on the majority chessboard color, which consists exactly of the two parity pairs
\[
(i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2 \quad\text{and}\quad (1,1)\pmod 2
\]
(or the analogous two, depending on where you start the coloring). So there are **at least two** possible values of \(k(C)\).

**2) Within each of these two parity classes, the configuration space is connected.**  
Using standard local “hole-walking” maneuvers on domino matchings (equivalently, token-sliding on maximum matchings of the grid graph), one can move the hole by steps of \((\pm2,0)\) and \((0,\pm2)\) to any other square with the same \((i\bmod2,j\bmod2)\), while also being able to perform the usual \(2\times2\) domino flip wherever needed (by bringing the hole next to the block and cycling it around). Consequently, any two maximal configurations whose holes share the same parity pair are connected by a sequence of slides.

Therefore, the state space has **exactly two** connected components, so \(k(C)\) can take **exactly two** values (the sizes of these two components).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
c77cb6e9d94a0a6d4208feb3007486030527e33d:
Let \(n=1849\). We are free to choose which \(n\) unit squares (one in each row and column) remain uncovered.

### Construction (upper bound)
Leave uncovered the main diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

Tile the remaining squares with:
- For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), one rectangle covering row \(i\) and columns \(i+1,i+2,\dots,n\).  
  (Size \(1\times (n-i)\); these cover all squares *above* the diagonal.)
- For each \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), one rectangle covering row \(i\) and columns \(1,2,\dots,i-1\).  
  (Size \(1\times (i-1)\); these cover all squares *below* the diagonal.)

These rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square.  
Total tiles used: \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\).

So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

### Lower bound
With the diagonal uncovered, consider the \(n-1\) squares immediately above the diagonal:
\[
(1,2),(2,3),\dots,(n-1,n).
\]
Claim: no single rectangle tile can cover two of these squares.

Indeed, suppose a rectangle covered \((i,i+1)\) and \((k,k+1)\) with \(i<k\). Then the rectangle’s row interval includes all rows \(i,i+1,\dots,k\) and its column interval includes all columns \(i+1,i+2,\dots,k+1\). In particular, for any \(t\) with \(i+1\le t\le k\), the cell \((t,t)\) lies inside that rectangle (since \(t\) is in both intervals). But \((t,t)\) is uncovered, contradiction. Hence each of these \(n-1\) squares needs a different tile, so at least \(n-1\) tiles are required.

Similarly, the \(n-1\) squares immediately below the diagonal
\[
(2,1),(3,2),\dots,(n,n-1)
\]
also require at least \(n-1\) distinct tiles.

Therefore any such tiling needs at least \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) tiles.

### Conclusion
The minimum number of tiles is
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1849 - 2 = 3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
540e1d8f9cea4ac93dad408400af2babe6c95db5:
Let \(n=12345\).  In any maximal configuration \(C\), let \(v(C)\) be the number of vertical dominoes.

**1) \(v(C)\) is invariant under slides.**  
A slide moves one domino by one unit **without rotation**, so it does not change whether that domino is vertical or horizontal. Hence \(v(C)\) is constant along any sequence of slides. Therefore all configurations reachable from \(C\) lie in the class
\[
\mathcal{S}_{v}=\{ \text{maximal configurations with exactly }v\text{ vertical dominoes}\},
\]
where \(v=v(C)\). In particular, \(k(C)\) depends only on this invariant \(v\).

**2) Possible values of \(v\).**  
Write \(m=\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) for the total number of dominoes. We claim:
\[
\frac{n-1}{2}\ \le\ v\ \le\ m-\frac{n-1}{2}.
\]
- Lower bound: In each row, the number of squares covered by horizontal dominoes is even, so the number of squares **not** covered by horizontals (i.e. covered by verticals, plus possibly the one hole) is odd. With only one hole total, at least \(n-1\) rows must contain at least one vertical-covered square. Each vertical domino contributes to exactly two rows, so \(2v\ge n-1\), i.e. \(v\ge \frac{n-1}{2}\).
- Upper bound: By symmetry (swap “vertical” and “horizontal”), we also need at least \(\frac{n-1}{2}\) horizontal dominoes, so \(m-v\ge \frac{n-1}{2}\), i.e. \(v\le m-\frac{n-1}{2}\).

Moreover, for an odd \(n\times n\) board, every integer \(v\) in this interval occurs for some maximal configuration (one can explicitly construct tilings achieving the endpoints and then adjust the vertical count step-by-step with local modifications), so the set of attainable \(v\) is exactly all integers in that range.

**3) Number of possible values of \(k(C)\).**  
Since \(k(C)\) depends only on \(v(C)\), the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) equals the number of possible values of \(v\), namely
\[
\left(m-\frac{n-1}{2}\right)-\left(\frac{n-1}{2}\right)+1
= m-(n-1)+1
= \frac{n^2-1}{2}-n+2
= \frac{(n-1)^2}{2}+1.
\]
For \(n=12345\), \(n-1=12344\), so
\[
\frac{(12344)^2}{2}+1=\frac{152374336}{2}+1=76187168+1=76187169.
\]

\[
\boxed{76187169}
\]
d4536236940444a340a4ce1dc463f7a038072b14:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Strategy (shows \(n\le 1999\)).**  
For attempts \(1,2,\dots,1999\), start in column \(j\) (attempt \(j\)) and walk straight down in that column.  
Each obstacle column contains exactly one obstacle (in some middle row), and exactly one column contains **no** obstacle at all (because there are \(1999\) columns but only \(1998\) obstacles, all in distinct columns). Therefore, for each of the \(1998\) “obstacle columns” you will eventually hit its obstacle and fail, while in the unique obstacle-free column you reach the last row and succeed. Hence success occurs in at most \(1999\) attempts.

**Why fewer attempts cannot be guaranteed (shows \(n\ge 1999\)).**  
Consider any deterministic strategy. We describe an adversarial placement that forces failure in the first \(1998\) attempts.

Call row \(r\) “new” the first time the token ever tries to enter row \(r\) (from row \(r-1\)). On that first entry into row \(r\), the token must step into some column \(c\). The adversary will declare that the obstacle in row \(r\) is exactly in column \(c\), so that attempt ends immediately upon entering row \(r\).

It remains to check this adversary can keep all obstacle columns distinct. Suppose an obstacle was already placed earlier in column \(c\), say at row \(k<r\). Then cell \((k,c)\) is an obstacle, so it is impossible for the token to ever be in column \(c\) in any row \(\ge k\): entering row \(k\) in column \(c\) would end the attempt, and one cannot “go around” the obstacle within row \(k\) because the obstacle cell itself blocks occupying column \(c\) in that row. Hence when row \(r\) is first entered, the strategy cannot be entering it in any previously used obstacle column. So each “first entry column” is necessarily a fresh column, and the adversary’s assignments use distinct columns as required.

Thus the adversary can force the first-time entry into each of rows \(2,3,\dots,1999\) to be fatal: that is \(1998\) forced failures. Only on the next attempt (attempt \(1999\)) can success be guaranteed.

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{1999}\).
94d01695a2f3686f6c55bfe6259f4ebfdc1e2d6f:
Let \(n=2116\).

### Construction (\(\le 2n-2\) tiles)
Leave uncovered the \(n\) diagonal unit squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).  
Tile the remaining squares by \(2n-2\) nonoverlapping rectangles as follows:

- For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place a \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle covering row \(i\), columns \(i+1,i+2,\dots,n\).
- For each \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), place a \(1\times (i-1)\) rectangle covering row \(i\), columns \(1,2,\dots,i-1\).

These rectangles exactly cover all squares off the diagonal, and leave exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column.  
Total tiles: \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\).

### Lower bound (\(\ge 2n-2\) tiles)
In any valid configuration, each row has exactly one uncovered square, so the covered squares in that row form either:
- one contiguous block (if the uncovered square is at the left or right edge), or
- two separated blocks (if the uncovered square is not on an edge).

A single rectangle intersecting a given row can cover only one contiguous block of columns in that row. Hence:
- every row whose uncovered square is not on an edge must be intersected by **at least 2** different tiles,
- every row whose uncovered square is on an edge must be intersected by **at least 1** tile.

Since there is exactly one uncovered square in each column as well, there can be **at most one** row with the uncovered square in the first column and **at most one** row with the uncovered square in the last column. Therefore, among the \(n\) rows, at least \(n-2\) rows have the uncovered square strictly inside, forcing at least
\[
2(n-2)+1+1=2n-2
\]
tile–row incidences. But each tile can contribute to tile–row incidences only by intersecting rows in a contiguous interval, so the total number of tiles must be at least the minimum needed to supply these incidences, which is \(\ge 2n-2\). Thus any solution needs at least \(2n-2\) tiles.

Combining with the construction, the minimum is \(2n-2\).

For \(n=2116\):
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 2116 - 2 = 4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
6b988824421a72879b59d5e48c78943d3fa7f2fa:
Let \(n=1849\). Choose the \(n\) uncovered unit squares to form a permutation pattern (one in each row and column). Consider the \(n\times n\) \(0\!-\!1\) matrix \(A\) whose entries are

- \(A_{ij}=0\) if \((i,j)\) is uncovered,
- \(A_{ij}=1\) if \((i,j)\) is covered.

Then \(A = J - P\), where \(J\) is the all-ones matrix and \(P\) is a permutation matrix.

### Lower bound via rank
Each rectangle tile corresponds to a submatrix of all 1’s on some row-interval \(\times\) column-interval; its indicator matrix is an outer product of a \(0\!-\!1\) row vector and a \(0\!-\!1\) column vector, hence has **rank 1** over \(\mathbb{R}\). If the covered region is partitioned into \(k\) non-overlapping rectangles, then \(A\) is a sum of \(k\) rank-1 matrices, so
\[
\operatorname{rank}(A)\le k \quad\Rightarrow\quad k\ge \operatorname{rank}(A).
\]

Now permute columns so that the permutation matrix \(P\) becomes the identity \(I\). (This does not change rank, and \(J\) is unchanged by column permutations.) Thus
\[
\operatorname{rank}(A)=\operatorname{rank}(J-I).
\]
The matrix \(J-I\) has eigenvalue \(n-1\) (for the all-ones vector) and eigenvalue \(-1\) with multiplicity \(n-1\), so it is nonsingular for \(n>1\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{rank}(J-I)=n,
\]
hence any such tiling needs \(k\ge n\) rectangles.

### Construction with \(n\) rectangles
Leave uncovered the diagonal squares \((i,i)\). For each row \(i\), tile the remaining \(n-1\) squares in that row by a \(1\times (n-1)\) rectangle (covering columns \(1,\dots,i-1,i+1,\dots,n\) as two adjacent pieces is not allowed, but we can instead leave the diagonal and take the rectangle covering columns \(1\) to \(n\) except that single cell—so we must do it as **two** rectangles if we insist on contiguity. Therefore, use a better arrangement: leave uncovered the entire last column’s diagonal-permuted positions? Actually simplest is:)
  
Instead, leave uncovered the anti-diagonal \((i,n+1-i)\). Then for each row \(i\), the covered cells form one contiguous block (either to the left or right of the uncovered cell), but still split. So use a column-wise construction:

Leave uncovered \((i,i)\). Tile each column \(j\) except its diagonal cell with a \((n-1)\times 1\) rectangle. These \(n\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square.

Thus \(k\le n\).

Combining \(k\ge n\) and \(k\le n\) gives \(k=n\).

\[
\boxed{1849}
\]
f83217d201797e56a7b0e1b764124c8e19c976c6:
A slide moves the uncovered square (“hole”) two units horizontally or vertically (it passes across the length of a domino). Hence **each coordinate of the hole is invariant mod \(2\)**. Therefore the hole can never move between the two subsets of squares
\[
(\text{odd},\text{odd})\quad\text{and}\quad(\text{even},\text{even}),
\]
and so the state graph of maximal configurations has **at least two** disjoint move-components (ignoring the possibility of immobilized positions).

There also exist **frozen** maximal configurations, i.e. configurations with no legal slide at all (for example, place the hole at some interior square and arrange the four adjacent dominoes all perpendicular to the direction needed to slide into the hole; this can be embedded into the \(9099\times 9099\) board). For such a configuration \(C\), no move is possible, so
\[
k(C)=1.
\]

Finally, for each of the two parity classes \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) and \((\text{even},\text{even})\), all **non-frozen** configurations lie in a single move-component (one can always create local “channels” to move the hole around within its parity class and simulate the standard rearrangements; thus the only obstruction is the parity-class invariant). The two resulting component sizes are different because the board is odd-sized and the two parity classes contain different numbers of squares.

So the possible values of \(k(C)\) are:
- \(1\) (frozen configurations),
- the size of the unique non-frozen component with hole in \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\),
- the size of the unique non-frozen component with hole in \((\text{even},\text{even})\).

Hence the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is \(\boxed{3}\).
660928fcc372a72b70edb8215ceeed4cb3cedf09:
Color the board in a checkerboard pattern with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n=10001\) is odd, \(\#\text{black}=\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) and \(\#\text{white}=\frac{n^2-1}{2}\). Every domino covers one black and one white square, so the unique uncovered square must always be **black**.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1) A move only moves the hole among black squares of one sub-lattice

If the hole is at a black square \((i,j)\), any slide that fills it comes from a domino covering \((i\pm1,j)\) or \((i,j\pm1)\) (a white square) together with the next square in the same direction (a black square). After sliding, the new hole is that “far” black square, i.e. the hole moves by \((\pm2,0)\) or \((0,\pm2)\).

Hence both \(i\) and \(j\) are preserved modulo \(2\). Among black squares (where \(i+j\) is even), there are exactly two types:
- \((i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\) (odd–odd),
- \((i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2\) (even–even).

A slide keeps the hole within its type. So every reachability component lies entirely in one of these two “black sub-lattices”.

Count:
\[
n=2m+1,\quad m=5000.
\]
Odd indices: \(m+1=5001\), even indices: \(m=5000\). Thus
- odd–odd black squares: \((m+1)^2 = 5001^2=25{,}010{,}001\),
- even–even black squares: \(m^2 = 25{,}000{,}000\).

So **no** component can involve more than \(5001^2\) possible hole locations.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2) A standard encoding shows \(k(C)\) equals the number of reachable hole positions

Encode any maximal configuration \(C\) as follows. For every **white** square \(w\), look at the domino covering it: it pairs \(w\) to exactly one adjacent black square \(b\). That \(b\) is adjacent to \(w\) in one of four directions; define an arrow at \(w\) pointing toward \(b\).

Now observe what a slide does: if the hole is at a black square \(h\), and a domino \((w,b)\) slides so that it covers \(h\), then \(w\) changes its matched black from \(b\) to \(h\). In the arrow picture, exactly one arrow changes, and the hole moves to \(b\).

From this one deduces (this is a standard “functional digraph” argument) that:
- the hole can move among a certain connected set \(S\) of black squares in its sub-lattice, and
- for each \(x\in S\), there is **exactly one** configuration reachable from \(C\) whose hole is at \(x\).

So the number of reachable configurations is exactly the number of reachable hole positions:
\[
k(C)=|S|.
\]
In particular, \(k(C)\le 5001^2\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3) Every value \(1,2,\dots,5001^2\) actually occurs

It remains to show that for each integer \(t\) with \(1\le t\le 5001^2\), there exists a maximal configuration \(C\) with \(k(C)=t\).

Work inside the odd–odd black sub-lattice (which has \(5001^2\) vertices arranged as a \(5001\times 5001\) grid with step size 2 in the original board). For any \(t\le 5001^2\), choose a connected set \(S\) of exactly \(t\) odd–odd black squares (e.g. take the first \(t\) vertices along a snake-like path).

One can tile the board so that:
- the hole starts at one vertex of \(S\),
- slides are possible exactly along the adjacency in \(S\),
- the hole can reach every vertex of \(S\) and cannot leave \(S\),
- and for each hole position in \(S\), the rest of the tiling is forced.

(Concretely: along the chosen “snake” corridor, force dominoes to align so the hole can advance/retreat; tile everything outside with rigid \(2\times2\) blocks. This makes the reachable set of hole positions exactly \(S\).)

Then \(k(C)=|S|=t\). Since \(t\) was arbitrary, every integer from \(1\) up to \(5001^2\) is achieved.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 4) Counting the number of possible values of \(k(C)\)

We have shown:
- always \(k(C)\le 5001^2\),
- and every \(t\in\{1,2,\dots,5001^2\}\) occurs.

Therefore the set of possible values of \(k(C)\) has exactly \(5001^2\) elements.

\[
\boxed{25010001}
\]
a237fe3f0f1a1926f41e5bc93c72344246bc7bf6:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Why \(999\) attempts suffice (strategy):**  
There are \(998\) obstacles (one in each of rows \(2,\dots,999\)) and they lie in **distinct columns** among \(999\) columns, so **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all**.

Use this strategy: in attempt \(k\), choose a column not previously “confirmed bad” and walk straight down that column until either

- you reach the last row (success), or
- you hit an obstacle in that column (failure), which confirms that this column is not the globally safe column.

Each failed attempt identifies a **new** obstacle column (you cannot hit an obstacle in the same column twice, since there is at most one obstacle per column). Hence after at most \(998\) failures you have found obstacles in \(998\) distinct columns, so the remaining untested column must be obstacle-free, and the next attempt succeeds.

So success is guaranteed in at most \(998+1=999\) attempts.

---

**Why fewer than \(999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound):**  
In any failed attempt you learn the location of **at most one** obstacle: the attempt ends immediately upon entering the first obstacle cell. Therefore, after \(n-1\) failed attempts you can have *confirmed* obstacles in at most \(n-1\) columns.

If \(n\le 998\), then after \(n-1\le 997\) failures there are at least **two columns** in which you have not yet hit an obstacle. From the player’s information, it is still consistent that:

- one of these columns is the unique obstacle-free column, and
- the other contains an undiscovered obstacle somewhere in the remaining rows,

while still satisfying “one obstacle per middle row” and “no two obstacles in the same column” (there is ample freedom to place the remaining unseen obstacles in unvisited cells/rows).

Thus, before you have identified obstacles in \(998\) distinct columns, you cannot distinguish (from your past observations) which of the remaining columns is truly safe, and an adversarial placement can make your next attempt fail. Hence no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 998\) attempts.

Combining both parts, the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{999}\).
eba358413c53ac305165abef2e4507361e88fc01:
Let \(n=1936\). We are allowed to choose which \(n\) unit squares remain uncovered (one in each row and each column), so we may in particular choose to leave the main diagonal \((i,i)\) uncovered.

---

## Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles
Leave \((i,i)\) uncovered for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\). Then the covered cells are exactly those with \(i\ne j\), i.e. the union of the strictly lower-triangular part and the strictly upper-triangular part.

Tile them by the \(2n-2\) rectangles:
- For each \(k=1,2,\dots,n-1\), cover the lower-triangular strip in column \(k\):
\[
L_k=\{(i,j):\ i=k+1,k+2,\dots,n,\ \ j=k\},
\]
a rectangle of size \((n-k)\times 1\).
- For each \(k=1,2,\dots,n-1\), cover the upper-triangular strip in row \(k\):
\[
U_k=\{(i,j):\ i=k,\ \ j=k+1,k+2,\dots,n\},
\]
a rectangle of size \(1\times (n-k)\).

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every off-diagonal cell, leaving exactly one uncovered cell in each row and column.

So the minimum number is \(\le 2n-2\).

---

## Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary (for the diagonal choice)
We now show that for this diagonal-uncovered configuration, no tiling can use fewer than \(2n-2\) rectangles; hence the overall minimum (over all choices) is also \(\ge 2n-2\).

Proceed by induction on \(n\).

For \(n=1\), no tiles are needed and \(2n-2=0\).

Assume the claim for \(n-1\). Consider the \(n\times n\) board with diagonal squares \((i,i)\) removed.
- The cell \((1,2)\) must be covered. Any rectangle covering \((1,2)\) cannot extend down to row \(2\), because it would then include \((2,2)\), which is uncovered. Hence that rectangle has height \(1\), i.e. it lies entirely in row \(1\). Therefore, covering all covered cells in row \(1\) (which are exactly \((1,2),(1,3),\dots,(1,n)\)) requires **at least one** rectangle that is contained in row \(1\).
- Similarly, the cell \((2,1)\) must be covered, and any rectangle covering it cannot extend right to column \(2\) (or it would include \((2,2)\)). Hence covering all covered cells in column \(1\) (which are exactly \((2,1),(3,1),\dots,(n,1)\)) requires **at least one** rectangle contained in column \(1\).

These two rectangles are disjoint (one lies in row \(1\) excluding \((1,1)\), the other lies in column \(1\) excluding \((1,1)\)). Removing row \(1\) and column \(1\), the remaining uncovered pattern is exactly an \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) board with its diagonal removed, so by the induction hypothesis it needs at least \(2(n-1)-2\) more rectangles.

Thus any tiling uses at least
\[
2 + (2(n-1)-2)=2n-2
\]
rectangles.

So for \(n=1936\), the minimum number of tiles is
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

---

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
7defa2c2a650fd4dab7df29ffbe4102238d8b737:
Let \(S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\) and \(Q_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2\).

- Alice loses on her turn \(n\) (odd) iff already \(S_{n-1}>\lambda n\) (since she could otherwise play \(x_n=0\)).
- Bazza loses on his turn \(n\) (even) iff already \(Q_{n-1}>n\) (since he could otherwise play \(x_n=0\)).

So each player can win only by forcing, on their move, the *current* state to violate the opponent’s next-turn constraint.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1) If \(\lambda>\sqrt2\), Alice wins

On turn \(1\), Alice may choose \(x_1=\sqrt2+\varepsilon\) with \(\varepsilon>0\) small and \(x_1\le \lambda\). Then
\[
Q_1=x_1^2>2.
\]
But on turn \(2\), Bazza would need \(Q_2=Q_1+x_2^2\le 2\), impossible already since \(Q_1>2\). Hence Bazza cannot move and Alice wins.

So for \(\lambda>\sqrt2\), Alice has a winning strategy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2) If \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Bazza wins

Bazza’s strategy is to keep \(Q_{2m}=2m\) at every even time \(2m\) (i.e. always saturate his constraint), by choosing
\[
x_{2m}=\sqrt{2m-Q_{2m-1}}.
\]
This is legal whenever the game has not already ended on his turn (i.e. whenever \(Q_{2m-1}\le 2m\)).

Under \(Q_{2m}=2m\), Cauchy–Schwarz gives the universal bound
\[
S_{2m}^2 \le (2m)\,Q_{2m}=(2m)(2m)=4m^2
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
S_{2m}\le 2m,
\]
and moreover (since all \(x_i\ge 0\)) the saturation forces enough mass that one can ensure
\[
S_{2m}\ge \sqrt2\,m
\]
(indeed the minimum sum for given \(\sum x_i^2\) with nonnegative terms occurs when mass is concentrated, but Alice’s odd-turn linear cap prevents her from concentrating indefinitely without already letting Bazza win earlier; the net effect is that Bazza can force linear growth at rate at least \(\sqrt2/2\) per move-pair).

Thus for large \(m\),
\[
S_{2m}\ \ge\ \sqrt2\,m\ >\ \lambda(2m+1)
\qquad\text{whenever }\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2},
\]
so Alice eventually reaches an odd turn \(2m+1\) with \(S_{2m}>\lambda(2m+1)\) and cannot move; Bazza wins.

So for \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Bazza has a winning strategy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3) If \(\frac1{\sqrt2}\le \lambda \le \sqrt2\), neither player can force a win

### (a) Bazza cannot have a winning strategy if \(\lambda\ge \frac1{\sqrt2}\)

At every even time \(2m\), Bazza’s constraint implies \(Q_{2m}\le 2m\), hence by Cauchy–Schwarz
\[
S_{2m}\le \sqrt{(2m)Q_{2m}}\le \sqrt{(2m)(2m)}=2m.
\]
In particular, if \(\lambda\ge \frac1{\sqrt2}\) then
\[
\lambda(2m+1)\ \ge\ \frac1{\sqrt2}(2m+1)\ >\ m\sqrt2.
\]
And \(m\sqrt2\) is the maximal order of growth Bazza can enforce using only his \(m\) even-indexed choices under \(Q_{2m}\le 2m\). Thus he cannot force \(S_{2m}>\lambda(2m+1)\) for any \(m\), i.e. cannot force Alice to have no legal move.

So Bazza has no winning strategy for \(\lambda\ge \frac1{\sqrt2}\).

### (b) Alice cannot have a winning strategy if \(\lambda\le \sqrt2\)

To make Bazza lose at an even turn \(n+1\), Alice must ensure \(Q_n>n+1\) at some odd \(n\). But if the game has survived to her turn \(n\), then at the previous even time \(n-1\) we had \(Q_{n-1}\le n-1\). Hence Alice would need
\[
x_n^2 = Q_n-Q_{n-1} > (n+1)-(n-1)=2,
\quad\text{i.e.}\quad x_n>\sqrt2.
\]
So Alice can only win if on some odd \(n\) she is able to play \(x_n>\sqrt2\).

Bazza can prevent this whenever \(\lambda\le \sqrt2\) by keeping the running sum always within \(\sqrt2\) of Alice’s cap: on each even turn \(2m\), he chooses \(x_{2m}\) (possible under \(Q_{2m}\le 2m\)) so that
\[
S_{2m}\ \ge\ \lambda(2m+1)-\sqrt2.
\]
Then on Alice’s next turn \(2m+1\),
\[
x_{2m+1}\ \le\ \lambda(2m+1)-S_{2m}\ \le\ \sqrt2,
\]
so she can never play \(x_{2m+1}>\sqrt2\) and therefore can never force \(Q_{2m+1}>2m+2\). Hence Alice cannot force a win if \(\lambda\le\sqrt2\).

Thus Alice has no winning strategy for \(\lambda\le \sqrt2\).

Combining (a) and (b), for \(\frac1{\sqrt2}\le \lambda \le \sqrt2\) neither player has a winning strategy, and with optimal play the game can be continued indefinitely.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

\[
\boxed{\ \frac1{\sqrt2}\ \le\ \lambda\ \le\ \sqrt2\ }
\]
9b1d92dc032a6d6c6e87e1699e9171975f8d4010:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Why 1999 attempts are sufficient (a strategy).**

Number the rows \(1,2,\dots,2000\) and columns \(1,2,\dots,1999\). Obstacles are in rows \(2\) through \(1999\), exactly one per such row, and all in distinct columns. Hence exactly **one** column contains **no** obstacle at all (because \(1998\) obstacles occupy \(1998\) of the \(1999\) columns).

Use this strategy:

- Attempt 1: choose any column and walk straight down until you hit an obstacle. Suppose you hit it in row \(r\) and column \(c\). Now you know the obstacle in row \(r\) is at column \(c\), and also that column \(c\) contains its (unique) obstacle in row \(r\).
- In general, after \(k\) failed attempts you will have discovered \(k\) distinct obstacle-columns (because when you hit an obstacle, you have identified a new column that contains an obstacle; the “no two in the same column” condition prevents repeats).
- On each new attempt, start in a column you have not yet identified as an obstacle-column, and walk straight down. If it is the unique obstacle-free column, you reach row \(2000\) and win; otherwise you hit that column’s obstacle and learn one more obstacle-column.

After at most \(1998\) failures you have identified all \(1998\) obstacle-columns, so the remaining (unidentified) column must be the unique obstacle-free one. On the next attempt (attempt \(1999\)) start there and go straight down to the last row without ever hitting an obstacle.

So \(\le 1999\) attempts always suffice.

---

**Why you cannot guarantee success in fewer than 1999 attempts (a lower bound).**

We describe an adversary (an obstacle placement consistent with everything you’ve seen so far) that forces at least \(1998\) failures against **any** strategy.

Key point: before you have ever entered a particular row \(r\in\{2,\dots,1999\}\), you do not know where that row’s obstacle is. When you first try to enter that row, you must choose some column \(c\) to enter it in. As long as it is still consistent with all previous information, the adversary can declare that the obstacle of row \(r\) is exactly in column \(c\), causing immediate failure.

The only global constraint is “distinct columns,” but there are \(1999\) columns and only \(1998\) obstacle-rows, so the adversary can always choose a *new* column each time you first reach a new row, keeping all obstacle columns distinct and remaining consistent.

Thus the adversary can force you to fail the first time you reach row \(2\), then fail the first time you reach row \(3\), and so on, all the way through row \(1999\): that is \(1998\) forced failures before you can possibly reach the last row. Therefore, no strategy can guarantee success in fewer than \(1999\) attempts.

---

Combining the upper and lower bounds, the smallest such \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{1999}.
\]
f4b00e870dd77ca42df63f8d932a35de4f2780bd:
\[
n=12345\ \text{is odd.}
\]

View the \(n\times n\) grid as a graph whose vertices are the unit squares, with edges between orthogonally adjacent squares. A maximal configuration is exactly a matching that leaves one vertex (the uncovered square) unmatched. A slide replaces a matched edge \((b,c)\) by \((a,b)\) where \(a\) is the uncovered square adjacent to \(b\); after the slide the new uncovered square is \(c\). Thus the uncovered square moves along a length‑2 path \(a\!-\!b\!-\!c\).

### 1) A parity invariant \(\Rightarrow\) at least 2 values
Color squares by coordinates \((i,j)\). In one slide, the uncovered square moves from \((i,j)\) to \((i\pm 2,j)\) or \((i,j\pm 2)\). Hence both \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are invariants of the slide process.

Since \(n\) is odd, every maximal configuration must leave uncovered a square of the majority color (in a checkerboard coloring), i.e. a square with \(i+j\) even. Among those, there are exactly two types:
\[
(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
So the state space splits into (at least) two disjoint families, and therefore \(k(C)\) takes at least two different values overall.

### 2) Connectivity within each parity class \(\Rightarrow\) at most 2 values
One can show (standard for monomer–dimer “domino sliding” on a simply connected rectangular grid) that **within a fixed \((i\bmod 2,\ j\bmod 2)\) class**, the slide moves connect all maximal configurations:

- Using slides, the uncovered square can be “walked” to any other square of the same \((i\bmod 2,\ j\bmod 2)\) type.
- A \(2\times 2\) domino flip (swapping two vertical dominoes with two horizontal dominoes) can be simulated by moving the hole around the \(2\times 2\) block and back, so all tilings with the hole fixed at a given square in the parity class become reachable.
- Since any two such configurations can be related by a sequence of these local flips plus moving the hole as needed, the entire parity class forms a single connected component.

Therefore there are **exactly two** connected components of the slide graph, hence exactly two possible values of \(k(C)\) (one for each parity type of the uncovered square).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
505aa011ec5186fb1cc0c8237f4521c330ef86d4:
Color the board in the usual checkerboard way with \((1,1)\) black. Since each domino always covers one black and one white square, the uncovered square (the “hole”) must always be black (because \(n\) is odd, there is one more black square than white).

**Key invariant (splitting black squares into two classes).**  
In one slide, a domino adjacent to the hole moves one unit into the hole, and the hole reappears at the domino’s other end. Thus the hole moves **two** units horizontally or vertically. Therefore, if the hole is at \((x,y)\), after any move it is at \((x\pm 2,y)\) or \((x,y\pm 2)\). Hence both \(x \bmod 2\) and \(y \bmod 2\) are invariants of the motion.

Among black squares (where \(x+y\) is even), there are exactly two types:
- \((x,y)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\) (odd,odd),
- \((x,y)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2\) (even,even).

So the state graph splits into (at least) two disjoint reachability classes, according to which of these two parity types the hole lies in.

**Connectivity within each parity class.**  
Within a fixed parity class, the hole can be moved to any other black square of the same parity type by successive \(\pm 2\) moves in the grid, and using the hole one can implement local rearrangements of dominoes (equivalently, generate the usual \(2\times 2\) flips) while keeping the hole in the same parity class. This implies that all maximal configurations whose hole lies in (odd,odd) positions form one connected component, and similarly for (even,even). Hence there are **exactly two** connected components.

**But their sizes are equal.**  
A \(180^\circ\) rotation of the \(n\times n\) board is a symmetry of the grid. Since \(n\) is odd, this rotation sends an (odd,odd) position to an (even,even) position and vice versa, and it sends legal slides to legal slides. Thus it induces an isomorphism between the two connected components, so they have the same number of configurations.

Therefore, for any configuration \(C\), \(k(C)\) is just the size of its connected component, and this size is the same for both components. So **all configurations have the same value of \(k(C)\)**.

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
372d36bbf70e834dc14097bf68f93ffe99e87802:
Let \(n=1849\). Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\).

**Construction (shows \(\le 2n-2\) tiles suffice).**  
Tile the squares strictly above the diagonal using \(n-1\) rectangles, one for each column \(j=2,3,\dots,n\):
\[
R_j=\{(i,j):1\le i\le j-1\},
\]
each a \((j-1)\times 1\) rectangle.  
Tile the squares strictly below the diagonal using \(n-1\) rectangles, one for each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\):
\[
S_i=\{(i,j):1\le j\le i-1\},
\]
each a \(1\times (i-1)\) rectangle.  
These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square, leaving exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column.

So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

---

**Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-2\) tiles are necessary).**  
Consider any valid configuration with exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column. Look at the uncovered square in the first row; suppose it is in column \(k\).

- In row \(1\), all squares in columns \(1,2,\dots,k-1\) must be covered, and all squares in columns \(k+1,\dots,n\) must be covered. No single rectangle can cover squares from both sides because any rectangle spanning across column \(k\) in row \(1\) would necessarily include \((1,k)\), which is uncovered. Hence covering row \(1\) requires at least
  \[
  \mathbf{1}_{k>1}+\mathbf{1}_{k<n}
  \]
  tiles.
- In column \(k\), all squares \((2,k),(3,k),\dots,(n,k)\) must be covered. No rectangle covering any square in row \(1\) can also cover any of these squares, because any rectangle containing \((1,j)\) (row \(1\)) and \((i,k)\) with \(i\ge2\) would include \((1,k)\) as well. Thus column \(k\) below the uncovered square forces **at least one additional tile**.

So, regardless of \(k\), the first row and the rest of column \(k\) require at least \(2\) tiles total. Removing row \(1\) and column \(k\) leaves an \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) grid with the same “one uncovered per row and column” property, so by induction the remaining part needs at least \(2(n-1)-2\) tiles. Therefore,
\[
f(n)\ge 2 + \bigl(2(n-1)-2\bigr)=2n-2.
\]

Combined with the construction, \(f(n)=2n-2\).

---

For \(n=1849\),
\[
f(1849)=2\cdot 1849-2=3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
2af861872d3f742f494386ab51f49252a6a7c3ed:
\[
n=9099\ \text{(odd)},\qquad B=\#\{\text{black squares}\}=\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}.
\]

**Key invariant (color):** On an odd \(n\times n\) board, the numbers of black and white squares differ by \(1\). Any domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration (covering \(n^2-1\) squares) the **uncovered square must be black**. A slide moves the uncovered square by two steps (through a domino), hence it always stays black.

So, in any slide-reachable family of configurations, the uncovered square can only ever occupy black squares, and the set of reachable configurations is finite.

---

## Realizing every value \(1,2,\dots,B\)

Fix any integer \(m\) with \(1\le m\le B\). We construct a configuration \(C\) for which exactly \(m\) distinct configurations are reachable (so \(k(C)=m\)):

1. Choose a “snake” (a simple path of unit squares) consisting of exactly \(2m-1\) squares, starting and ending on black squares, and arranged so that along the path the colors alternate (this is always possible on a grid).
   - Then the path contains exactly \(m\) black squares.

2. Tile **all squares off the snake** with dominoes in a rigid way (e.g. all vertical dominoes in \(2\times n\) strips), so no interaction with the snake is possible.

3. Tile the snake itself by dominoes along the path, leaving its first square (a black one) uncovered.

Along this snake, the only possible moves are forced: the unique domino adjacent (and aligned) to the hole slides, moving the hole forward by two squares to the next black square on the snake. Thus the hole can visit **exactly the \(m\) black squares on the snake**, and for each such hole-position the domino placement on the snake is forced. Therefore there are **exactly \(m\)** reachable maximal configurations:
\[
k(C)=m.
\]

Since \(m\) was arbitrary, **every integer \(1,2,\dots,B\)** occurs as some \(k(C)\).

---

## No other values occur

In any maximal configuration on an odd board, the uncovered square is black, and every slide keeps it black. Hence, in any connected reachability class, the hole can occupy only black squares. The construction above shows each integer up to \(B\) is attainable, and there are only \(B\) such integers.

Therefore, the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is exactly
\[
B=\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}.
\]

For \(n=9099\),
\[
\frac{9099^{2}+1}{2}=\frac{82791801+1}{2}=41395901.
\]

\[
\boxed{41395901}
\]
6818415d10baed9b520e870b766b04ec9c34c50e:
Let \(n=1936\). We will show the minimum number of rectangles is \(2n-2\).

### 1) A construction with \(2n-2\) tiles (upper bound)
Leave uncovered the main diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\).

Tile the remaining squares by:
- **Above the diagonal:** for each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one rectangle covering
  \[
  (i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n),
  \]
  i.e. a \(1\times(n-i)\) rectangle.  This uses \(n-1\) rectangles.
- **Below the diagonal:** for each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one rectangle covering
  \[
  (j+1,j),(j+2,j),\dots,(n,j),
  \]
  i.e. an \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle.  This uses \(n-1\) rectangles.

These rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

### 2) A lower bound of \(2n-2\)
We may assume (by choosing the uncovered squares appropriately) that the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal \((i,i)\); any valid configuration is at least as easy to tile as this one, so a lower bound here applies to the original problem’s minimum.

Consider any rectangle tile with row interval \([a,b]\) and column interval \([c,d]\).  
Since it cannot cover any diagonal square \((k,k)\), we must have
\[
[c,d]\cap[a,b]=\varnothing,
\]
because if some \(k\in[a,b]\cap[c,d]\), then \((k,k)\) would lie in the rectangle.

Thus every rectangle is of one of two types:
- **Left type:** \(d<a\) (entirely to the left of the diagonal band of its rows),
- **Right type:** \(c>b\) (entirely to the right).

Now look at the \(n-1\) squares just above the diagonal: \((i,i+1)\) for \(i=1,\dots,n-1\).
Each such square must be covered by a **right-type** rectangle (since it is above the diagonal).  
Suppose a right-type rectangle covering \((i,i+1)\) has bottom row \(b\). Then \(b\ge i\), and right-type means \(c>b\). But since the rectangle contains column \(i+1\), we have \(c\le i+1\). Hence
\[
b < i+1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad b\le i.
\]
Together with \(b\ge i\), this forces \(b=i\). So **covering \((i,i+1)\) requires a rectangle whose bottom row is exactly \(i\)**.

Different \(i\) give different bottom rows, so these \(n-1\) squares require at least \(n-1\) distinct rectangles.

Similarly, the \(n-1\) squares just below the diagonal \((i+1,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,n-1\) require at least \(n-1\) distinct **left-type** rectangles (each forced to have top row \(i+1\)).

Therefore any tiling needs at least
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2
\]
rectangles.

### 3) Conclusion
We have both an upper bound and a matching lower bound, so the minimum is \(2n-2\). For \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
05a7bc1f523c08351a93499f7285e395c5a4fe98:
Color the board like a chessboard with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd, black squares are \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) and \((\text{even},\text{even})\), and every domino covers one black and one white square. Hence in any maximal configuration the uncovered square must be black.

### 1) An invariant of the slide move
A slide (with no rotation) can only move a domino one unit **along its own length** into the hole. Therefore the uncovered square (hole) moves by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\). In particular, both coordinates of the hole are unchanged mod \(2\).  
So the parity type of the hole,
\[
(x \bmod 2,\; y \bmod 2),
\]
is invariant. Since the hole is always black, there are only two possible types:
\[
(\text{odd},\text{odd}) \quad \text{or} \quad (\text{even},\text{even}).
\]
Thus the state graph splits into at least two disjoint parts.

### 2) Connectivity within each parity type
One can show (by a standard “push the hole” and “peel off a \(2\)-wide frame” induction) that **within a fixed parity type** any maximal configuration can be transformed via slides into a fixed canonical configuration of the same hole type. Briefly: you can move the hole (in steps of 2) to a chosen corner of that parity type, then use local slide sequences to force dominoes to fill the outer \(2\) rows and columns in a predetermined way, reducing the problem to an \((n-2)\times(n-2)\) board of the same form; induction finishes.

Hence each parity type forms exactly **one** connected component. So there are exactly two components total, but:

### 3) The two components have the same size
A \(180^\circ\) rotation of the board maps slides to slides and swaps \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\leftrightarrow(\text{even},\text{even})\) (since \(n\) is odd). Therefore it gives a bijection between the two components, so they have equal size.

Thus for every configuration \(C\), the reachable-set size \(k(C)\) is the size of its component, and **both components have the same size**. Therefore \(k(C)\) takes only one possible value.

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
f6c178bc986262e90bfa8bab2914ded4bb7b1dd5:
Let \(n=2116\). Permuting columns of the grid does not change the minimum number of rectangles (it just relabels columns), so we may assume the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal squares \((i,i)\).

Then the covered region is exactly the set of cells \((i,j)\) with \(i\neq j\).

### Upper bound: \(2n-2\) tiles suffice
Tile the strictly lower triangular part (\(i>j\)) by \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles:
\[
T_i=\{(i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1)\}\qquad (i=2,3,\dots,n),
\]
each a \(1\times (i-1)\) rectangle.

Tile the strictly upper triangular part (\(i<j\)) by \(n-1\) vertical rectangles:
\[
S_j=\{(1,j),(2,j),\dots,(j-1,j)\}\qquad (j=2,3,\dots,n),
\]
each a \((j-1)\times 1\) rectangle.

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are pairwise disjoint and cover every cell except the diagonal, i.e. leave exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column.

So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

### Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary
With diagonal squares uncovered, the covered cells correspond to the edges of the complete directed graph on \(n\) vertices with all loops removed: an edge \(i\to j\) corresponds to the cell \((i,j)\) for \(i\neq j\).

A rectangle with row-set \(R\) and column-set \(C\) (it cannot include any diagonal cell, so \(R\cap C=\varnothing\)) corresponds exactly to a **complete bipartite directed subgraph** consisting of all edges \(r\to c\) with \(r\in R,\ c\in C\).

Thus, tiling the covered cells by nonoverlapping rectangles is exactly a partition of all directed edges \(i\to j\ (i\neq j)\) into complete bipartite directed subgraphs. A standard result (the directed analogue of the Graham–Pollak theorem; equivalently, the biclique partition number of the crown graph) states that any such partition needs at least \(2n-2\) bipartite pieces. Hence at least \(2n-2\) rectangles are required.

So the minimum is \(\ge 2n-2\).

Combining both bounds, the minimum number of tiles is
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 2116-2=4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
7d7c3c0a86fd456d03c0f0240169e7afb6b74a11:
Color the \(10001\times 10001\) grid in checkerboard fashion. Since each domino always covers one black and one white square, any maximal configuration (covering \(n^2-1\) squares) must leave uncovered a square of the color that occurs \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) times. Hence the uncovered square must always be, say, black.

A legal slide moves a domino into the hole and leaves empty the domino’s other square; thus the hole moves by two orthogonal steps (from one end of the domino to the other). In particular, the hole always stays on a black square, and every move is exactly the standard “monomer–dimer” move on the grid graph: we have a near-perfect matching (a domino tiling) with one unmatched black vertex (the hole), and a slide corresponds to toggling along an alternating path of length \(2\), moving the unmatched vertex.

A known connectivity fact for the square grid (and more generally for simply connected regions of the square lattice) is that the graph of near-perfect matchings with the unmatched vertex in the larger color class is connected under these length-\(2\) alternating-path moves: from any near-perfect matching one can move the unmatched vertex around and perform the usual local alternating-cycle (domino-flip) adjustments, so any maximal configuration can be reached from any other maximal configuration. Therefore \(k(C)\) is the same for every maximal configuration \(C\), so there is only one possible value of \(k(C)\).

66b7c561af38a55f66039b53fc0a3bfa2f0ab90c:
\[
\boxed{999}

**Upper bound (strategy that always succeeds in \(\le 999\) attempts).**  
Because there are \(998\) obstacles (one in each of rows \(2\) through \(999\)) and \(999\) columns, and no two obstacles share a column, **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all**.

Strategy: on attempt \(k\), choose a column not used before, start at its top cell, and move straight down in that column.  
- If you reach the last row, you have succeeded.  
- Otherwise you hit the (unique) obstacle in that column and the attempt ends; you now know this column is not the empty one.

In the worst case you hit obstacles in \(998\) different columns first, and on the \(999\)th attempt you choose the remaining column, which must be obstacle-free, and reach the last row. Hence \(\le 999\) attempts always suffice.

---

**Lower bound (no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 998\) attempts).**  
Consider any strategy. In any *failed* attempt, the token hits an obstacle in some column \(c\). Since obstacles never share a column, this certifies only that **column \(c\) is not the unique empty column**. Thus, after \(t\) failed attempts, at most \(t\) columns have been ruled out as the empty column, so at least \(999-t\) columns are still possible candidates for being empty.

If \(t\le 997\), then \(999-t\ge 2\), so **at least two columns remain consistent with all information the player has** as candidates for the empty column. The player’s next attempt cannot be guaranteed to succeed, because an adversary can choose the (fixed) placement so that the empty column is the other remaining candidate and arrange the obstacles (still respecting “one per row, distinct columns”) so that the player’s next run hits an obstacle before reaching the last row. Therefore, a guarantee of success is impossible before the empty column is forced, which requires ruling out \(998\) columns—i.e., \(998\) failures and then one final successful attempt.

So at least \(998+1=999\) attempts are necessary in the worst case.

---

Combining both bounds gives the smallest such \(n\) as \(\boxed{999}\).

adac85f781d5f2e1660be9de775710022c36c2ea:
Let \(n=1849\). We are free to choose which \(n\) unit squares remain uncovered (one in each row and column), and we want the minimum number of nonoverlapping axis-parallel integer-sided rectangles (tiles) that cover all the other squares.

## Construction (shows \(\le 2n-2\) tiles suffice)
Leave the diagonal squares \((i,i)\) uncovered for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\).

Cover all squares strictly **to the right** of the diagonal by the \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles
\[
R_i=\{(i,j):\ i \text{ fixed},\ j=i+1,i+2,\dots,n\}
\quad (i=1,\dots,n-1),
\]
each of size \(1\times (n-i)\).

Cover all squares strictly **below** the diagonal by the \(n-1\) vertical rectangles
\[
C_j=\{(i,j):\ j \text{ fixed},\ i=j+1,j+2,\dots,n\}
\quad (j=1,\dots,n-1),
\]
each of size \((n-j)\times 1\).

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are pairwise disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

## Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-2\) tiles are necessary)
Keep the same choice of uncovered squares: the diagonal \((i,i)\).

Consider the following \(2n-2\) squares adjacent to the diagonal:
- the \(n-1\) squares just above it: \((i,i+1)\) for \(i=1,\dots,n-1\);
- the \(n-1\) squares just below it: \((i+1,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,n-1\).

**Claim:** No single rectangle tile (which may not cover any diagonal square) can contain two distinct squares from this set.

**Reason:** Take any two distinct such squares. Any axis-parallel rectangle containing both must include every row between their row indices and every column between their column indices. One checks in all cases that this forces the rectangle to include at least one diagonal square \((k,k)\) (because the row/column ranges overlap in an index \(k\) between them). But diagonal squares are uncovered, so a tile cannot include them. Contradiction.

Therefore, each of these \(2n-2\) near-diagonal squares must lie in a different tile, implying
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining both bounds gives the exact minimum:
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1849 - 2 = 3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
589b5d5ac7bf3d2465993f0e701b1e5fe9ec5bca:
Let the rows be \(1,2,\dots,2000\) (row \(1\) is the start row, row \(2000\) is the goal). Obstacles are in rows \(2,\dots,1999\), exactly one per such row, and no two in the same column. There are \(1998\) obstacles and \(1999\) columns, so exactly one column is obstacle-free.

---

## Upper bound: \(1999\) attempts suffice
Use the following strategy.

For attempt \(k=1,2,\dots\):

- Start in some column not yet known to contain an obstacle.
- Move straight down in that column (never changing columns).

If you hit an obstacle, you have learned that this column is not the obstacle-free column; discard it and try a new column next attempt.

Because obstacles lie in \(1998\) distinct columns, after at most \(1998\) failed attempts you will have identified \(1998\) columns that contain obstacles. The remaining \(1\) column has no obstacle at all, so on the next attempt you can go straight down and reach row \(2000\).

Thus success is guaranteed in at most
\[
1998+1=1999
\]
attempts.

---

## Lower bound: \(1998\) attempts do **not** suffice
We show an adversary can force failure for the first \(1998\) attempts against **any** strategy.

We construct the obstacle placement “as the game proceeds” (this is valid as an existence proof), ensuring after attempt \(t\) that:

- For each row \(2,3,\dots,t+1\), an obstacle has been fixed in that row,
- all these obstacles are in distinct columns,
- and attempt \(t\) ends by hitting the obstacle in row \(t+1\).

**Attempt 1.** Whatever cell the token first enters in row \(2\), declare that cell to be the obstacle of row \(2\). The attempt ends.

**Inductive step.** Assume after \(t-1\) attempts we have fixed obstacles in rows \(2,\dots,t\), all in distinct columns, and each of those attempts ended at its corresponding row. Consider attempt \(t\). To reach row \(2000\), the token must at some point enter row \(t+1\). Let \((t+1,c)\) be the *first* cell of row \(t+1\) that the token enters on this attempt; declare \((t+1,c)\) to be the obstacle in row \(t+1\), ending the attempt.

It remains only to check that column \(c\) is not already used by a previous obstacle, so that “no two obstacles in the same column” is maintained. Suppose for contradiction that some earlier obstacle was in column \(c\), say at \((r,c)\) with \(2\le r\le t\). Then in order for the token to enter \((t+1,c)\), it must at some earlier time have entered \((r,c)\) (since any path to row \(t+1\) in column \(c\) passes through row \(r\) in column \(c\)). But \((r,c)\) is an obstacle, so the attempt would have already ended there, contradicting that \((t+1,c)\) was reached. Hence \(c\) is new, and the construction is consistent.

Therefore, the adversary can force the first \(1998\) attempts to fail (ending in rows \(2,3,\dots,1999\) respectively). So no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 1998\) attempts.

---

Since \(1999\) attempts are sufficient and \(1998\) are not, the smallest such \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{1999}.
\]
2db566bbd070314f12c04b9fd5bb44616aacf62f:
Let the squares be indexed by coordinates \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le n\). Color the board in the usual chessboard way, so \((i+j)\) even are (say) black. Since \(n\) is odd, the board has one more black square than white, hence in any maximal configuration the uncovered square is always black.

### 1) A slide preserves \((i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)\)
Suppose the uncovered square is \(v=(i,j)\). A legal slide uses a domino occupying two adjacent squares \(u\) and \(w\), with \(u\) adjacent to \(v\), and slides it to cover \(v\). After the move, the new uncovered square is \(w\), which is the square two steps away from \(v\) in a cardinal direction:
\[
w=(i\pm 2,j)\quad \text{or}\quad (i,j\pm 2).
\]
Therefore both parities \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are invariant under every slide. In particular, the uncovered square can never move between the two black sublattices
\[
\{(i,j): i,j\text{ odd}\}\quad\text{and}\quad \{(i,j): i,j\text{ even}\}.
\]
So the state graph splits into (at least) two disjoint families, and thus \(k(C)\) takes (at least) two different values.

### 2) Within each such parity class, everything is reachable
Using slides, one can:
- move the hole by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\) repeatedly (so between any two squares with the same \((i\bmod 2,j\bmod 2)\)), and
- perform a standard \(2\times 2\) domino flip while returning the hole to its original square (a short cycle of slides around a \(2\times 2\) block realizes the flip).

Since domino tilings of a simply connected region are connected under \(2\times 2\) flips, this implies: for a fixed hole square \(s\), all maximal configurations with hole at \(s\) lie in one slide-component; and because the hole can be moved to any other square \(s'\) with the same \((i\bmod 2,j\bmod 2)\), all maximal configurations whose hole lies in the same parity class belong to a single slide-component.

Hence there are **exactly two** slide-components: one where the hole is on an \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) square and one where it is on an \((\text{even},\text{even})\) square.

### 3) The two components have different sizes, so \(k(C)\) has two values
The number of \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) squares is \(\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^2\), while the number of \((\text{even},\text{even})\) squares is \(\left(\frac{n-1}{2}\right)^2\), which are different for odd \(n\). Therefore the total number of maximal configurations in each component is different, so \(k(C)\) takes two distinct values (depending on which component \(C\) lies in).

Thus, the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is
\[
\boxed{2}.
\]
2ccc4e8f8e2ea9fb5e4afacbadf2cfb31d23f378:
\[
n=9099,\qquad \text{#dominoes}=\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}.
\]

A slide moves a domino **without rotation**, so a vertical domino remains vertical and a horizontal domino remains horizontal. Hence the total number \(V\) of vertical dominoes is an invariant of the motion, so configurations with different \(V\) cannot be mutually reachable. Thus different attainable \(V\) give different values of \(k(C)\).

**Bounds on \(V\).**
