Look at any fixed row. A vertical domino contributes exactly one square to that row (and one to the row above/below), while a horizontal domino contributes either 0 or 2 squares to the row. Since \(n\) is odd and exactly one square in the whole grid is uncovered, every row other than the hole’s row contains an odd number of covered squares, forcing each such row to contain at least one square belonging to a vertical domino. Because a single vertical domino contributes to exactly two rows, we get
\[
V\ge \frac{n-1}{2}.
\]
By symmetry (considering columns), we also get
\[
H\ge \frac{n-1}{2},
\]
so
\[
V = \frac{n^{2}-1}{2}-H \le \frac{n^{2}-1}{2}-\frac{n-1}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\frac{n-1}{2}\ \le\ V\ \le\ \frac{n^{2}-1}{2}-\frac{n-1}{2}.
\]

For \(n=9099\),
\[
\frac{n-1}{2}=4549,\qquad \frac{n^{2}-1}{2}=\frac{9099^{2}-1}{2}=\frac{82{,}791{,}801-1}{2}=41{,}395{,}900,
\]
so
\[
4549 \le V \le 41{,}395{,}900-4549=41{,}391{,}351.
\]

Moreover, for an odd grid of this size one can realize every integer \(V\) in this interval by explicit constructions (and hence obtain distinct reachability classes), so the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) equals the number of integers in this interval:
\[
41{,}391{,}351-4549+1=41{,}386{,}803.
\]

\[
\boxed{41386803}
\]
f936addbc3cf1c7953aae9ba0b865cb7cb25acb7:
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height order (\(n=2025\)).

### 1) Each system is a disjoint union of directed chains
In one system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct). Since edges always go upward, there are no directed cycles, so every component is a directed path (a chain). Thus the system partitions the \(n\) points into some number \(p\) of chains (including chains of length \(1\)).

A chain of length \(L\) contains \(L-1\) edges, so summing over all chains gives
\[
k=\sum (L_i-1)=\Big(\sum L_i\Big)-p=n-p.
\]
Hence each system with \(k\) edges has exactly
\[
p=n-k
\]
chains.

Two points are “linked” by the system iff they lie in the same chain.

### 2) When can two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Let system \(A\) have chains \(A_1,\dots,A_p\) and system \(B\) have chains \(B_1,\dots,B_p\) (same \(p=n-k\)).

If some intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) contains at least \(2\) points, then those two points lie in the same \(A\)-chain and the same \(B\)-chain, so they are linked by both systems.

Therefore, **to avoid any common linked pair**, we must have
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1\quad \text{for all }i,j.
\]
But then the total number of points satisfies
\[
n=\sum_{i=1}^p\sum_{j=1}^p |A_i\cap B_j|\le \sum_{i=1}^p\sum_{j=1}^p 1=p^2.
\]
So avoiding a common linked pair forces
\[
p^2\ge n.
\]

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025=45^2\)
We have \(\sqrt{n}=45\).

- If \(k\ge 1981\), then \(p=n-k\le 2025-1981=44\), so
  \[
  p^2\le 44^2=1936<2025=n,
  \]
  impossible. Hence some \(A_i\cap B_j\) has size \(\ge 2\), giving two points linked by both systems.  
  So **\(k=1981\) guarantees** a common linked pair.

- To show \(1981\) is minimal, we exhibit a construction with \(k=1980\) and no common linked pair. Here \(p=n-k=45\).
  
  Arrange the \(2025\) points as a \(45\times 45\) grid \(\{v_{i,j}\}\) and order them by increasing \(j\), and within each \(j\) by increasing \(i\) (this is the height order).
  
  Define system \(A\): for each fixed \(i\), make the chain
  \[
  v_{i,1}\to v_{i,2}\to \cdots \to v_{i,45}.
  \]
  Define system \(B\): for each fixed \(j\), make the chain
  \[
  v_{1,j}\to v_{2,j}\to \cdots \to v_{45,j}.
  \]
  Each system has \(45\) chains of length \(45\), hence \(45\cdot 44=1980\) edges, and one checks the “start higher \(\Rightarrow\) end higher” condition holds under this ordering.  
  Any two distinct points in the same \(A\)-chain share the same \(i\) but different \(j\), while any two distinct points in the same \(B\)-chain share the same \(j\) but different \(i\). Thus no two distinct points can be in the same chain in both systems. So \(k=1980\) does **not** guarantee a common linked pair.

Therefore the smallest guaranteeing \(k\) is
\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
1a5023c6b7a00ccd027502dcd0ebcf1aba220dbb:
Let \(n=1936\).  Choose the \(n\) uncovered squares to be the anti-diagonal
\[
(1,n),(2,n-1),\dots,(n,1).
\]

### Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles (upper bound)
For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), tile all squares **to the left** of the uncovered one in that row with one horizontal rectangle:
\[
\text{Row }i:\quad \text{columns }1\text{ through }(n-i)\qquad (1\times (n-i)).
\]
That uses \(n-1\) tiles.

For each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), tile all squares **to the right** of the uncovered one in that row with one horizontal rectangle:
\[
\text{Row }i:\quad \text{columns }(n-i+2)\text{ through }n\qquad (1\times (i-1)).
\]
That uses another \(n-1\) tiles.

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every square except the anti-diagonal, which indeed leaves exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum } \le 2n-2.
\]

### Lower bound \(2n-2\)
Consider *any* valid configuration of uncovered squares \((i,c_i)\) (so \((c_1,\dots,c_n)\) is a permutation of \(1,2,\dots,n\)). Define:
- \(L\): the set of covered squares immediately **left** of an uncovered square, i.e. \((i,c_i-1)\) for those \(i\) with \(c_i>1\). Since exactly one \(c_i\) equals \(1\), we have \(|L|=n-1\).
- \(R\): the set of covered squares immediately **right** of an uncovered square, i.e. \((i,c_i+1)\) for those \(i\) with \(c_i<n\). Since exactly one \(c_i\) equals \(n\), we have \(|R|=n-1\).

Now note:
- No rectangle tile can contain two distinct squares of \(L\).  
  If a rectangle contained \((i,c_i-1)\) and \((j,c_j-1)\) with \(i\ne j\), then (because it is a Cartesian product of row-interval and column-interval) it must also contain one of the uncovered squares \((i,c_i)\) or \((j,c_j)\): the rectangle spans the row interval between \(i\) and \(j\), and spans columns reaching up to one of \(c_i\) or \(c_j\) (since \(c_i\ne c_j\) and it already contains the adjacent column \(c_i-1\) or \(c_j-1\)). This would force covering an uncovered square, impossible.
- Similarly, no rectangle can contain two distinct squares of \(R\).

Thus each square in \(L\) requires a different tile, giving at least \(n-1\) tiles, and each square in \(R\) requires a different tile, giving at least another \(n-1\) tiles. Also, a tile cannot simultaneously account for one of the “left-adjacent” constraints and one of the “right-adjacent” constraints without spanning across an uncovered square in some row, so these counts do not overlap. Therefore any valid tiling needs at least
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2
\]
tiles.

Combining with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\). For \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1936 - 2 = 3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
8b431d22db52ca8efcd03f7cd6973a5475128c65:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) by height, where \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)):

- each point is the start of **at most one** edge (starts distinct),
- each point is the end of **at most one** edge (ends distinct),
- edges always go upward, so there are no directed cycles.

Hence the directed graph of \(A\) is a disjoint union of directed paths (including isolated vertices). Therefore each connected component is a chain, and within a chain any two vertices are linked (the lower reaches the higher).

If a system has \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices and all components are paths, then
\[
k = n - (\text{number of components}),
\]
so the number of chains (components) is
\[
m = n-k.
\]
Thus both \(A\) and \(B\) partition the \(n\) points into exactly \(m=n-k\) chains.

### 2) When do two points get linked by both?
Assign to each point \(v\) the pair
\[
(\,C_A(v),\, C_B(v)\,),
\]
where \(C_A(v)\in\{1,\dots,m\}\) is the chain of \(A\) containing \(v\), and similarly for \(B\).

If two distinct points \(u\neq v\) have the same pair \((C_A,C_B)\), then they lie in the same chain in \(A\) and in the same chain in \(B\). Since each chain is a directed path, the lower one reaches the higher one in each system, so they are linked by both systems.

So to **avoid** any common linked pair, all \(n\) points must have **distinct** pairs \((C_A,C_B)\). But there are only \(m^2\) possible pairs, so avoidance requires
\[
n \le m^2 = (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, to **guarantee** a common linked pair in all cases, we need
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025=45^2\)
We need
\[
2025 > (2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=2025-k\). Then we need \(m^2<2025\), so \(m\le 44\). Hence
\[
2025-k \le 44 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (show \(k=1980\) does not guarantee)
Take a \(45\times 45\) grid of labels \((r,c)\) with heights in row-major order:
\[
(r,c)\ \mapsto\ (r-1)\cdot 45 + c.
\]
Define system \(A\) to link along rows: \((r,c)\to(r,c+1)\) for \(c\le 44\), giving \(45\cdot 44=1980\) edges.
Define system \(B\) to link along columns: \((r,c)\to(r+1,c)\) for \(r\le 44\), also \(1980\) edges.

Then two distinct points cannot share both a row and a column, so no two points are linked by both systems. Thus \(k=1980\) is not sufficient.

So the smallest \(k\) that guarantees existence of two points linked by both systems is
\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
7090c505220a1f183ca4962fbc11217066eefacb:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Strategy achieving 999 attempts (upper bound).**  
On attempt \(k\) choose a new column \(c_k\) in the first row and move straight down in that column without any horizontal moves.

- If column \(c_k\) contains no obstacle, you reach the last row and succeed.
- Otherwise you eventually enter that column’s unique obstacle cell and the attempt ends; from that moment on you know that column \(c_k\) is not the empty column.

Because there are \(999\) columns and exactly \(998\) obstacles in **distinct** columns, there is exactly **one** column with no obstacle. After at most \(998\) failed attempts you have eliminated \(998\) columns, so the remaining column must be the obstacle-free one; the next attempt succeeds. Hence success is guaranteed in at most \(998+1=999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than 999 attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound).**  
In any failed attempt, the attempt ends at the *first* obstacle cell entered, so a failed attempt can certify (at most) one column as “definitely not empty” (the column containing that obstacle). Before you actually hit an obstacle in a column, it is always consistent with the rules that an obstacle in that column lies somewhere deeper than anything you have traversed in that column.

Thus, after \(t\) failed attempts you can be forced to have ruled out at most \(t\) columns as the empty one. If \(t\le 998\), there still remains at least one column that could be the unique empty column, so an adversary can choose the obstacle-free column to be one you have not yet ruled out and make all attempts so far fail. Therefore no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 998\) attempts.

Combining both parts, the minimum such \(n\) is \(\boxed{999}\).
dce6dd78825e03c16f3a464536cbb2400c3f1d7c:
Let \(n=2116\). The uncovered squares form a permutation matrix: one uncovered in each row and each column. By permuting rows and columns (which preserves “rectangles with sides parallel to grid lines” and preserves the number of tiles), we may assume the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal squares \((i,i)\), \(1\le i\le n\).

So we must tile all squares \((r,c)\) with \(r\ne c\).

### Lower bound (\(\ge 2n-2\))
Consider the \(n-1\) squares just above the diagonal:
\[
(i,i+1)\quad (1\le i\le n-1).
\]
Take any rectangle tile that covers \((i,i+1)\). Since the tile includes column \(i+1\), if it also included row \(i+1\), it would cover \((i+1,i+1)\), which is forbidden (uncovered). Hence the tile cannot extend to row \(i+1\), so it must have height \(1\), i.e. it lies entirely in row \(i\).

Therefore, each of the \(n-1\) squares \((i,i+1)\) lies in a tile contained in row \(i\), and since these are different rows, they require at least \(n-1\) distinct tiles.

Similarly, consider the \(n-1\) squares just below the diagonal:
\[
(i+1,i)\quad (1\le i\le n-1).
\]
Any tile covering \((i+1,i)\) includes row \(i+1\). If it also included column \(i+1\), it would cover \((i+1,i+1)\), forbidden. Thus it cannot extend to column \(i+1\), so it must have width \(1\), i.e. it lies entirely in column \(i\). Since these are different columns, they require at least \(n-1\) distinct tiles.

The tiles forced by the “above-diagonal” squares are all height \(1\), and the tiles forced by the “below-diagonal” squares are all width \(1\); in particular, none of these tiles can coincide. Hence the total number of tiles is at least
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

### Construction (\(\le 2n-2\))
Leave the diagonal \((i,i)\) uncovered. Use:
- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), one horizontal rectangle covering columns \(i+1,i+2,\dots,n\) in row \(i\).  
  (This covers all squares above the diagonal.)
- For each column \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), one vertical rectangle covering rows \(i+1,i+2,\dots,n\) in column \(i\).  
  (This covers all squares below the diagonal.)

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover exactly all non-diagonal squares.

Thus the minimum number of tiles is \(2n-2\). For \(n=2116\),
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 2116 - 2 = 4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
6ef05d482692c9aea6caed59864bbdc5d01dd926:
\[
\boxed{2}
\]

Color the board in a checkerboard pattern with \((1,1)\) black. Since each domino covers one black and one white square, in any maximal configuration (covering \(n^2-1\) squares) the uncovered square must be black (because when \(n\) is odd, there is exactly one more black square than white square).

Now track how the uncovered square (“hole”) moves under a slide. If the hole is at a black square \(B\), a domino can slide into it only from an adjacent white square \(W\); that domino’s other square is the black square \(B'\) adjacent to \(W\) on the opposite side. After the slide, the hole becomes \(B'\). Thus the hole always moves from a black square to a black square by a displacement of exactly \(2\) units horizontally or vertically. Therefore both the row and column parities of the hole are invariants:
- if the hole starts on an \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) square, it stays on \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) squares forever;
- if it starts on an \((\text{even},\text{even})\) square, it stays on \((\text{even},\text{even})\) squares forever.

So the state graph splits into **at least two** components.

For \(n\ge 3\) (in particular \(n=9099\)), one can also move the hole to any other black square with the same \((\bmod\,2)\) row/column type and, using standard local slide cycles, perform the usual \(2\times2\) domino flips while returning the hole to where it started. This implies that **all** maximal configurations with the hole in \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) form one connected component, and **all** maximal configurations with the hole in \((\text{even},\text{even})\) form the other. Hence \(k(C)\) depends only on which of these two parity classes the uncovered square lies in.

Therefore there are exactly \(\boxed{2}\) possible values of \(k(C)\).
58cb5f13611c8272c3463afcd9aa6d7514dee5db:
\[
n=3.
\]

**Strategy achieving 3 attempts (upper bound).**  
Pick any two **adjacent** columns, say \(j\) and \(j+1\).

- **Attempt 1:** Start in column \(j\) and walk straight down.  
  - If you reach the last row, you are done.  
  - Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in column \(j\), at some row \(r\). Now you know \((r,j)\) is blocked, and also that **every other cell in column \(j\) is safe** (because no two obstacles share a column).

- **Attempt 2:** Start in column \(j+1\) and walk straight down.  
  - If you reach the last row, you are done.  
  - Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in column \(j+1\), at some row \(s\). Now you know \((s,j+1)\) is blocked, and every other cell in column \(j+1\) is safe.

Now you know the obstacle rows \(r\) and \(s\) for these two adjacent columns.

- **Attempt 3 (guaranteed success):** Walk down using only columns \(j\) and \(j+1\), switching **one row before** the obstacle in the current column:
  - Start in column \(j\). When you are at row \(r-1\), move right to column \(j+1\) (this step is safe because column \(j+1\) is only blocked at row \(s\neq r-1\)), then continue downward.
  - When you are at row \(s-1\), move left to column \(j\), then continue downward.
  - Continue similarly (each column has at most one blocked row, so after switching past it, that column remains safe for the rest of the descent).

Because the columns are adjacent, each switch is a single horizontal step into a cell guaranteed not to be an obstacle. Thus you reach the last row in at most 3 attempts.

**Why 2 attempts can’t be guaranteed (lower bound).**  
After the first failed attempt, you have learned the location of **at most one** obstacle, say \((r,c)\). In the second attempt, to reach the last row you must at some point enter a cell in a column \(\neq c\) (since column \(c\) is blocked at row \(r\)). At the first moment your second-attempt path enters such a new column in some middle row \(t\), the adversary can place the (still-unrevealed) obstacle of row \(t\) exactly in that entered cell, while keeping all obstacles in distinct columns and satisfying the conditions. Then your second attempt fails. Hence no strategy can force success in \(\le 2\) attempts.

Therefore the smallest guaranteed number is

\[
\boxed{3}.
\]
f42a556a7b64842c0f649f5b56b4c1eba5fd8571:
Label the points \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height order \((n=4225)\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point is the start of at most one edge (starts distinct) and the end of at most one edge (ends distinct), so every connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex).  
If a component has \(v\) vertices, it has \(v-1\) edges. Summing over components gives
\[
k=\sum (v-1)=n-\#(\text{components}).
\]
Hence each system with \(k\) edges has exactly
\[
c=n-k
\]
components.

Also, within a component (a directed path), **every pair of vertices in increasing order is linked** (follow the path).

### 2) If there is no pair linked by both systems, then \(n\le c_Ac_B\)
Let system \(A\) have components \(A_1,\dots,A_{c_A}\) and system \(B\) have components \(B_1,\dots,B_{c_B}\).

Each point \(x\) lies in exactly one pair \((A_i,B_j)\). If two distinct points lay in the same pair \((A_i,B_j)\), then they are in the same component of \(A\) and of \(B\), so (in the height order) one would be linked to the other by both systems—contradiction.

So the map \(x\mapsto (A\text{-component of }x,\ B\text{-component of }x)\) is injective, implying
\[
n \le c_Ac_B.
\]
Here \(c_A=c_B=c=n-k\), so
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]
Taking square roots (all quantities nonnegative):
\[
\sqrt n \le n-k \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \le n-\sqrt n.
\]

Thus, if
\[
k>n-\sqrt n,
\]
then avoiding a common linked pair is impossible, so a common linked pair is guaranteed.

For \(n=4225=65^2\), this threshold is
\[
k>4225-65=4160.
\]
So any \(k\ge 4161\) guarantees a common linked pair.

### 3) Sharpness: a construction with \(k=4160\) and no common linked pair
We show it is possible when \(k=4160\), i.e. \(c=n-k=65\) components in each system.

- **System \(A\)**: make 65 components by residue mod 65. For each \(r=1,\dots,65\),
\[
r \to r+65 \to r+2\cdot 65 \to \cdots \to r+64\cdot 65.
\]
This uses \(65\) disjoint directed paths, total edges \(65\cdot 64=4160\).  
Also, every edge is of the form \(i\to i+65\), so if \(i<j\) then \(i+65<j+65\); hence the required “higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end” condition holds.

- **System \(B\)**: make 65 components by consecutive blocks of 65. For each \(t=0,\dots,64\),
\[
65t+1 \to 65t+2 \to \cdots \to 65t+65.
\]
Again 65 disjoint paths, total edges \(65\cdot 64=4160\), and edges are \(i\to i+1\), which also satisfies the monotonicity condition.

Now, each block of 65 consecutive integers contains exactly one number of each residue mod 65, so each \(A\)-component meets each \(B\)-component in **exactly one** point. Therefore, no two distinct points can lie in the same component of both \(A\) and \(B\), so there is **no** pair linked by both systems.

Hence \(k=4160\) does **not** guarantee a common linked pair, but \(k=4161\) does.

\[
\boxed{4161}
\]
1715b555604aafe44e1d4bfbb54d8615020b5458:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Strategy achieving 1999 attempts (upper bound).**  
Number the columns \(1,2,\dots,1999\). On attempt \(j\), start in column \(j\) of the first row and repeatedly move straight down in that same column until either:

- you reach the last row (success), or
- you hit an obstacle (the attempt ends).

Because no two obstacles are in the same column, each failed attempt identifies a *new* column that contains an obstacle (namely, the column you were traveling in). There are \(1998\) obstacles total, so after at most \(1998\) failures you have found \(1998\) distinct “bad” columns. The remaining column has no obstacle in any intermediate row, so going straight down that column succeeds on the next attempt. Hence success is guaranteed in at most \(1998+1=1999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than 1999 attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound).**  
A strategy can only ever lose an attempt by stepping on an obstacle, and that reveals (at most) one new obstacle column per failed attempt. If you have had at most \(1997\) failures, then you can have ruled out at most \(1997\) columns as “containing an obstacle somewhere,” leaving at least \(1999-1997=2\) columns still consistent with being the unique obstacle-free column. An adversary can choose the obstacle placement so that the *only* way to reach the last row without ever stepping on an obstacle is to go all the way down that (unknown) obstacle-free column; with two columns still possible, any next attempt must commit to entering one of them somewhere, and the adversary can make that choice the wrong one. Therefore no strategy can force success within \(1998\) attempts in the worst case.

Combining the upper and lower bounds gives the minimal guaranteed number of attempts:
\[
\boxed{1999}.
\]
ce4f55298ed48435451b5e7621aa17932e42520b:
Let the squares be indexed by coordinates \((i,j)\), \(1\le i,j\le n\), and color the board in a checkerboard pattern with \((1,1)\) black.

### 1) The uncovered square must be black, and its \((i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)\) is invariant
Since \(n\) is odd, the board has \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) black squares and \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) white squares, i.e. one more black than white. Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration the unique uncovered square must be black.

Now suppose the uncovered square is at \((i,j)\) (black). A legal slide moves a domino into the hole **without rotation**; thus the domino must be aligned with the hole, so the hole moves two squares in a straight line:
\[
(i,j)\to (i\pm 2,j)\quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\to (i,j\pm 2).
\]
Therefore, in any slide, both \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are preserved. Hence the pair
\[
(i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)
\]
of the uncovered square is an invariant of reachability.

Among black squares (\(i+j\) even), there are exactly two such parity types:
- \((i,j)\) both odd;
- \((i,j)\) both even.

So the state graph of maximal configurations splits into **at least** two disjoint reachability classes.

### 2) Within each class, everything is reachable (so there are exactly two classes)
Fix one of the two parity classes (say, “both odd”). Because moves change \((i,j)\) by \(\pm2\) in one coordinate, the uncovered square can be moved along the induced \((2\times 2)\)-spaced grid of black squares of that parity; this induced graph is connected (it is a \(\bigl(\frac{n+1}{2}\bigr)\times \bigl(\frac{n+1}{2}\bigr)\) grid for the odd-odd class, and a \(\bigl(\frac{n-1}{2}\bigr)\times \bigl(\frac{n-1}{2}\bigr)\) grid for the even-even class). Using standard “hole-walking” maneuvers, one can move the hole to any other black square of the same parity while keeping a valid maximal configuration throughout.

Moreover, by walking the hole around small loops, one can implement the usual local \(2\times 2\) domino flip on demand away from the boundary; since domino tilings of a simply connected region are connected under these flips, this lets one pass between any two maximal configurations with the hole in the same parity class. Thus **each parity class forms a single reachability component**.

So there are **exactly two** reachability components, hence \(k(C)\) can take at most two values.

### 3) The two values of \(k(C)\) are different
The two components have different sets of possible hole positions: the “both odd” class contains the corners (e.g. \((1,1)\)), while the “both even” class contains no corner at all. Since every hole position in a component is reachable from every other, and there exist maximal configurations with hole at a corner, the component containing corner-hole configurations must contain strictly more configurations than the other component. Hence the two components have different sizes, so the two possible values of \(k(C)\) are distinct.

Therefore, the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is

\[
\boxed{2}.
\]
adaada8a1efa2e8b35fefa26598a978ef4e88304:
Let \(n=2116\). The uncovered squares form a permutation matrix: exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column. By permuting the columns, we may assume the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\). This does not change the minimum number of rectangles needed.

So we must tile (without overlap) all squares with \(i\ne j\), i.e. the union of:

- the **upper triangle** \(U=\{(i,j): i<j\}\),
- the **lower triangle** \(L=\{(i,j): i>j\}\).

Any axis-parallel rectangle that lies entirely in \(U\) must have row-interval \([a,b]\) and column-interval \([c,d]\) satisfying \(b<c\); otherwise it would contain some diagonal square \((k,k)\), which is forbidden. Similarly, any rectangle in \(L\) must satisfy \(d<a\).

### Lower bound
Consider the \(n-1\) “superdiagonal” squares \((k,k+1)\) for \(k=1,2,\dots,n-1\), all of which lie in \(U\).

**Claim:** No rectangle in \(U\) can contain two different squares \((k,k+1)\) and \((\ell,\ell+1)\) with \(k\ne \ell\).

Indeed, suppose a rectangle in \(U\) contains \((k,k+1)\). Let its row interval be \([a,b]\) and column interval \([c,d]\). Since it is in \(U\), \(b<c\). Because \((k,k+1)\) is inside, we have \(a\le k\le b\) and \(c\le k+1\le d\). Then
\[
b < c \le k+1 \quad\text{and}\quad b\ge k
\]
so \(b\le k\), hence \(b=k\). Similarly, \(c\ge k+1\) and \(c\le k+1\), so \(c=k+1\).
Thus any rectangle containing \((k,k+1)\) must have its **top row exactly \(k\)** and its **leftmost column exactly \(k+1\)**, which makes it impossible to also include \((\ell,\ell+1)\) for \(\ell\ne k\).

Therefore, the \(n-1\) squares \((k,k+1)\) must lie in \(n-1\) distinct rectangles, so
\[
\#\text{rectangles covering }U \ge n-1.
\]
By the same argument applied to the \(n-1\) “subdiagonal” squares \((k+1,k)\) in \(L\), we get
\[
\#\text{rectangles covering }L \ge n-1.
\]
Hence the total number of rectangles is at least
\[
2(n-1).
\]

### Construction achieving the bound
Leave the diagonal \((i,i)\) uncovered.

- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one rectangle covering the cells \((i,j)\) with \(j=i+1,i+2,\dots,n\). This tiles all of \(U\) using \(n-1\) rectangles.
- For each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), place one rectangle covering the cells \((i,j)\) with \(j=1,2,\dots,i-1\). This tiles all of \(L\) using \(n-1\) rectangles.

These rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal cell, leaving exactly one uncovered cell in each row and column.

So the minimum is exactly \(2(n-1)\). For \(n=2116\),
\[
2(2116-1)=2\cdot 2115=4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
be76f052b06fb9b92112421b3f0143c7a68e2028:
Let the uncovered square (the “hole”) in a maximal configuration \(C\) be at coordinates \((r,c)\) (rows and columns numbered \(1\) to \(n\)).

**Key invariant (parity class of the hole).**  
A legal slide moves a domino one unit so that it covers the hole, and the new hole becomes the square vacated at the other end of that domino. Hence the hole moves by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\). Therefore **both** \(r \bmod 2\) and \(c \bmod 2\) are invariant under every move.  
Since \(n\) is odd, the uncovered square must lie on the majority color of the checkerboard (say “black”), i.e. \(r+c\) is even, so the only possible parity classes are
\[
(r,c)\equiv (1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{or}\quad (r,c)\equiv (0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
Thus the state graph splits into (at most) two connected components, one for each class.

**Connectivity within each class (sketch).**  
Fix one class, e.g. \((r,c)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\). Using slides, one can move the hole around any \(2\times 2\) block of black squares in that class and use standard “bubble”/induction arguments to reduce any configuration to a chosen canonical configuration with the hole at a fixed location in the same class, without leaving the class. (Equivalently: the slide moves generate all alternating-path updates, and on an \(n\times n\) grid this makes the set of near-perfect matchings with the monomer in a fixed parity class connected.) Hence **each parity class is a single connected component**.

So there are exactly two components, one per parity class.

**But their sizes are equal.**  
Rotate the entire board by \(180^\circ\). This gives a bijection on maximal configurations and preserves the slide relation. Under this rotation,
\[
(r,c)\mapsto (n+1-r,\;n+1-c).
\]
Because \(n\) is odd, \(n+1\) is even, so this map flips both parities, sending the \((1,1)\)-class to the \((0,0)\)-class and vice versa. Therefore it is a graph isomorphism between the two components, so the two components have the **same number of vertices**. That number is exactly \(k(C)\) for any \(C\) in either component.

Hence **all configurations \(C\) have the same value of \(k(C)\)**, so there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
a683ba29f37aeb7dfd0b305a24f0bbec123825b3:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) in increasing height, with \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starts are distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ends are distinct). Also every edge goes upward, so there are no directed cycles. Hence each connected component is a directed path
\[
v_1\to v_2\to\cdots\to v_t,
\]
and the “linked” relation is exactly: two points are linked iff they lie in the same path (component).

If the system has components of sizes \(t_1,\dots,t_c\), then it has
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^c (t_i-1)=\left(\sum_{i=1}^c t_i\right)-c=n-c
\]
edges, so the number of components is
\[
c=n-k.
\]

Thus each system partitions the \(n\) points into exactly \(n-k\) directed paths.

### 2) When can two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Let the components (paths) of system \(A\) be \(A_1,\dots,A_{n-k}\) and those of \(B\) be \(B_1,\dots,B_{n-k}\).

If there is **no** pair of distinct points linked by both systems, then no intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) can contain two points (because any two points in \(A_i\cap B_j\) would be linked in both). Hence
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1\quad\text{for all }i,j.
\]
Therefore the map sending a point \(x\) to the ordered pair \((A\text{-component of }x,\;B\text{-component of }x)\) is injective, so
\[
n \le (n-k)\,(n-k)=(n-k)^2.
\]
Contrapositively, if
\[
n>(n-k)^2,
\]
then a common linked pair must exist.

### 3) Apply \(n=2025\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
2025>(2025-k)^2.
\]
Since \(45^2=2025\), the inequality holds exactly when \(2025-k\le 44\), i.e.
\[
k\ge 2025-44=1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=1980\) does not guarantee)
For \(k=1980\), we have \(n-k=45\) and \(2025=45^2\), so it is possible to have \(|A_i\cap B_j|=1\) for all \(i,j\). Concretely, arrange the \(2025\) points as a \(45\times 45\) grid \((i,j)\) listed in lexicographic order by \((i,j)\).

- In \(A\), connect \((i,j)\to(i,j+1)\) (rows become 45 chains of length 45).
- In \(B\), connect \((i,j)\to(i+1,j)\) (columns become 45 chains of length 45).

Both systems satisfy the required monotonicity condition, and any two distinct points share at most one of “same row” or “same column”, so no pair is linked by both.

Thus \(k=1980\) is not enough, but \(k=1981\) is.

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
a2ddb614ca463f3e4979b8a93e42459dee9c1155:
\[
\boxed{6581}
\]

**Why \(6581\) attempts are sufficient.**  
For rows \(2,3,\dots,6581\) there is exactly one obstacle (so \(6580\) obstacles total).

Strategy:

- On attempt \(1\), go from the first row into row \(2\) (any column). Then, staying within **row 2**, move left/right visiting new cells until you enter the obstacle in row \(2\). The attempt ends, and you now know exactly where the obstacle in row \(2\) is.
- On attempt \(2\), move down to row \(2\) while avoiding its known obstacle, then enter row \(3\). Now sweep along **row 3** until you hit its obstacle, learning its position.
- Continue similarly: on attempt \(k\) (for \(1\le k\le 6580\)), you safely pass rows \(2\) through \(k\) using the obstacles already found, enter row \(k+1\), and sweep that row until you hit its (unique) obstacle, thus learning it.

After \(6580\) attempts you have located all obstacles in rows \(2\) through \(6581\). Since no two obstacles share a column and there are \(6581\) columns but only \(6580\) obstacles, there exists a column containing **no obstacle at all**. On attempt \(6581\), start in that column and move straight down to the last row; you never enter an obstacle, so you succeed.

Hence success is guaranteed in at most \(6581\) attempts.

**Why \(6581\) attempts are necessary.**  
In any single attempt, the attempt ends the **first time** you enter an obstacle cell, so a failing attempt can reveal the location of **at most one** obstacle. We show an adversary can force the first \(6580\) attempts to fail while keeping a valid fixed obstacle placement.

Construct the obstacle placement “as the player plays” (this is a standard way to prove a lower bound; at the end the choices define one fixed placement):

- For each of the first \(6580\) attempts, look at the player’s path until the moment they first enter some cell in a row \(2\le r\le 6581\) whose obstacle has not yet been fixed. Declare **that** cell to be the obstacle of row \(r\), choosing it so that its column has not been used for any previously fixed obstacle (this is always possible because we will do this only \(6580\) times and there are \(6581\) columns).
- This makes that attempt end immediately, and preserves the rules: exactly one obstacle per row \(2\)–\(6581\), and all obstacle columns distinct.

Thus the adversary can force **all** of the first \(6580\) attempts to end in failure. Therefore no strategy can guarantee success in \(6580\) attempts.

Combining both parts, the minimum \(n\) is \(\boxed{6581}\).
0ffdc8d0130140066c873efe315cd93d25d28f9e:
Color the board in a checkerboard pattern. Since \(n=10001\) is odd, there are
\(\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\) squares of one color (say black) and \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\) of the other (white). Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration the single uncovered square must be black.

Now label squares by coordinates \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le n\). A slide moves the uncovered square from one end of a domino to the other end, so the uncovered square moves by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\). Hence both \(i \bmod 2\) and \(j \bmod 2\) are invariants under all slides. Since the uncovered square is black, we always have \(i\equiv j\pmod 2\), so there are exactly two possible invariant classes for the uncovered square:
\[
(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
Therefore the state graph splits into **at least** two connected components, and \(k(C)\) can take **at least** two different values.

It remains to see there are **no further** obstructions. For a fixed uncovered square \(u\), maximal configurations are exactly perfect matchings of the grid graph with \(u\) deleted. On a rectangular grid, any two perfect matchings differ by a set of disjoint even cycles, and each cycle can be “flipped” (the standard \(2\times 2\) domino flip is the basic case). Using the mobile hole, one can realize such a cycle flip by sliding the hole around the cycle and returning it to its starting square; this changes the matching exactly on that cycle. Hence for a fixed \(u\), all maximal configurations with hole at \(u\) are mutually reachable.

Also, within a fixed parity class \((i\bmod 2,j\bmod 2)\), the hole can be moved between any two squares of that class by repeated \((\pm 2,0)\), \((0,\pm 2)\) moves (the induced graph on that sublattice is connected), so all configurations whose holes lie in the same class are mutually reachable.

Thus there are **exactly two** connected components of the slide graph, and hence exactly two possible values of \(k(C)\) (one for each parity class of the hole).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
ca15e88166492294bde14b82bd337cd7f5f0e50c:
\[
n=4225=65^2
\]

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)):

- each point is the start of **at most one** edge (starts are distinct),
- each point is the end of **at most one** edge (ends are distinct),
- all edges go upward, and the “higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end” condition means the edges are order-preserving.

Therefore every vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) and indegree \(\le 1\), and cycles are impossible (heights strictly increase). Hence the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (including isolated vertices).

Two points are “linked by \(A\)” **iff** they lie in the same directed path component of \(A\).

Also, each edge in such a graph merges two components into one (it must go from the current tail of one path to the current head of another), so the number of components is exactly
\[
\#\text{components in }A = n-k.
\]
Same for system \(B\).

Let
\[
m=n-k.
\]
Then both \(A\) and \(B\) partition the \(n\) points into \(m\) path-components.

---

### 2) When can we avoid a pair linked by both?
If **no** two points are linked by both \(A\) and \(B\), then every intersection
\[
(\text{an }A\text{-component})\cap(\text{a }B\text{-component})
\]
has size at most \(1\); otherwise two points in that intersection would be linked in both systems.

There are \(m\cdot m=m^2\) such intersections (“cells”), each containing \(\le 1\) point, so we must have
\[
n\le m^2.
\]
Thus, to **guarantee** a common linked pair in all cases, we need
\[
n>m^2 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 4225>(4225-k)^2.
\]
Since \(4225=65^2\), this forces
\[
4225-k \le 64 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k\ge 4161.
\]

So \(k=4161\) is sufficient.

---

### 3) Sharpness: \(k=4160\) does **not** guarantee it
Let \(k=4160\). Then \(m=n-k=65\).

Arrange the \(4225\) points as a \(65\times 65\) array \((i,j)\), ordered by height in row-major order:
\[
(1,1),(1,2),\dots,(1,65),(2,1),\dots,(65,65).
\]

- System \(A\): make each row a directed path by connecting \((i,j)\to(i,j+1)\) for \(j=1,\dots,64\).
  This gives \(65\) components (rows), total edges \(65\cdot 64=4160\).

- System \(B\): make each column a directed path by connecting \((i,j)\to(i+1,j)\) for \(i=1,\dots,64\).
  This gives \(65\) components (columns), total edges \(65\cdot 64=4160\).

Any row intersects any column in exactly one point, so no two distinct points can lie in both the same row and the same column. Hence no pair is linked by both systems. Therefore \(k=4160\) is not enough.

---

Since \(4161\) is sufficient and \(4160\) is not, the smallest such \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
3882611dddea0042b2e15d613ba157461f05d2b1:
Let a **system** be a set of \(k\) directed edges, each going from a lower point to a higher point, such that

- all starts are distinct (outdegree \(\le 1\)),
- all ends are distinct (indegree \(\le 1\)),
- and the edges are order-preserving: if one edge starts higher than another, then it also ends higher.

Hence in any system, every vertex has indegree \(\le 1\) and outdegree \(\le 1\), so the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths. Two points are **linked** iff they lie on the same directed path.

---

## 1) A counting lemma (pigeonhole)

Fix a system \(A\) with \(k\) edges on \(n\) points.

- Exactly \(k\) points are ends of edges, so exactly \(n-k\) points have **no incoming edge**. Call these the **sources** of the \(A\)-paths.
- For any point \(v\), following incoming edges repeatedly (uniquely, since indegree \(\le 1\)) reaches a unique source of its path. Call it \(s_A(v)\).

Thus \(s_A(v)\) takes only \(n-k\) possible values.

Do the same for system \(B\), defining \(s_B(v)\). Then each point \(v\) determines a pair
\[
\bigl(s_A(v),\, s_B(v)\bigr),
\]
and there are at most \((n-k)^2\) such pairs.

If
\[
n > (n-k)^2,
\]
then by pigeonhole there exist two distinct points \(x\neq y\) with the same pair \((s_A,s_B)\). WLOG \(x<y\) in height. Then:

- \(x,y\) are in the same \(A\)-path (same \(s_A\)), so \(x\) is linked to \(y\) by \(A\),
- \(x,y\) are in the same \(B\)-path (same \(s_B\)), so \(x\) is linked to \(y\) by \(B\).

So this inequality guarantees a pair linked by both systems.

---

## 2) Apply to \(n=9801=99^2\)

Let \(n=9801\) and set \(s=n-k\). The guarantee condition is
\[
9801 > s^2.
\]
Since \(99^2=9801\), this holds exactly when \(s\le 98\), i.e.
\[
n-k \le 98 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k \ge 9801-98 = 9703.
\]
So \(k=9703\) is sufficient.

---

## 3) Sharpness: \(k=9702\) does **not** guarantee it

Take the points as a \(99\times 99\) grid labeled \((i,j)\) with heights in lex order:
\[
(1,1)<(1,2)<\cdots<(1,99)<(2,1)<\cdots<(99,99).
\]

Define system \(A\): within each fixed row \(i\),
\[
(i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,99),
\]
giving \(98\) edges per row, total \(99\cdot 98=9702\) edges.

Define system \(B\): within each fixed column \(j\),
\[
(1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(99,j),
\]
again \(99\cdot 98=9702\) edges. One checks the order-preserving condition holds.

Now:
- linked by \(A\) means “same row,”
- linked by \(B\) means “same column.”

Two distinct points cannot be in the same row and the same column, so no distinct pair is linked by both. Thus \(k=9702\) is not enough.

---

Therefore the smallest \(k\) that guarantees two points are linked by both systems is

\[
\boxed{9703}.
\]
cedbb7e49b24fd5a1de86c5edd43e3b1f9a29042:
To win, it is enough to find (and then use) the unique column that contains **no** obstacle.

### Key observation
There are obstacles in exactly \(1998\) rows (rows \(2\) through \(1999\)), and **no two obstacles are in the same column**.  
Hence the \(1998\) obstacles occupy \(1998\) distinct columns among the \(1999\) columns, so:

- **Exactly one column is completely obstacle-free** (in all middle rows).

Call this the *safe column*.

### Why you cannot guarantee success in \(\le 1998\) attempts
A strategy can only *prove* that a column is **not** the safe column by discovering an obstacle in that column—i.e., by having some attempt end by entering an obstacle in that column. Merely traveling through some cells of a column without crashing does **not** rule out that the column has an obstacle in some *unreached* row.

So after \(k\) failed attempts, you can have definitively eliminated **at most \(k\) columns** from being the safe one (namely, the columns in which you actually hit an obstacle). Therefore:

- After \(1998\) attempts, if you have not yet succeeded, you have eliminated at most \(1998\) columns.
- There are \(1999\) columns total, so there is still at least **one** column not eliminated.
- An obstacle placement consistent with everything you have seen can make that remaining column the safe one, meaning you still cannot have guaranteed success earlier.

Thus any guaranteed strategy needs at least
\[
1998 \text{ failures } + 1 \text{ final success } = 1999 \text{ attempts.}
\]

### A strategy that succeeds in \(1999\) attempts
Try columns one by one:

On attempt \(i\), start in a column not tried before and walk straight down.
- If you reach the last row, you have found the safe column and win.
- Otherwise you hit an obstacle, so that column is not safe; never try it again.

In the worst case, the safe column is the very last untried one, so you win on attempt \(1999\).

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{1999}.
\]
679de462c97b9a35b89e6eb940118da1c3274ff4:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Why \(999\) attempts are sufficient.**  
There are \(999\) columns and \(998\) obstacles, with no two obstacles in the same column. Hence **at least one column contains no obstacle at all** (in rows \(2\) through \(999\)). Strategy:

- On attempt \(1\), start in column \(1\) and move straight down.  
  If you reach the last row, you are done; otherwise you hit the obstacle in that column, so you learn column \(1\) is not the obstacle-free column.
- On attempt \(2\), do the same in column \(2\), etc.

Each failed attempt rules out exactly one column (the column in which you hit an obstacle). In the worst case you fail in \(998\) different columns and the remaining column is obstacle-free, so you succeed on attempt \(999\). Thus success is guaranteed in at most \(999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than \(999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
Consider any strategy that claims to guarantee success in at most \(998\) attempts. An adversary can place the obstacles so that:

- In each of the first \(998\) attempts, the token is forced (no matter how it moves) to enter some column that has not yet been “certified” to be completely obstacle-free, and the adversary assigns the obstacle of a *new* row to lie exactly at the first cell in that column the token tries to use to make downward progress.
- Since there are \(998\) obstacle-rows available (rows \(2\) through \(999\)) and \(998\) obstacles must lie in distinct columns, the adversary can choose these \(998\) “killing” obstacles to be all in distinct rows and distinct columns, making all first \(998\) attempts fail while remaining consistent with the rules.

Therefore no strategy can force success in \(\le 998\) attempts in the worst case, so the minimal guaranteed number is \(999\).
2214100823937996702426f115d322e4dc4edaa0:
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height order (\(n=2025\)).

### 1) Each system is a disjoint union of directed paths
In one system, each point is the start of at most one edge (starts are distinct) and the end of at most one edge (ends are distinct). Hence every vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) and indegree \(\le 1\), so the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (and isolated vertices).  

Two points are **linked** by the system iff they lie in the same directed path (component), with the lower one earlier on the path.

If a system has \(c\) components (paths + isolated points), then the number of edges is
\[
k=\sum_{\text{components}}(\text{size}-1)=n-c
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
c=n-k.
\]

### 2) When can the two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Let system \(A\) have components \(A_1,\dots,A_c\) and system \(B\) have components \(B_1,\dots,B_c\), where \(c=n-k\).

If there is **no** pair of points linked by both systems, then no intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) can contain 2 points (otherwise those 2 points are in the same component in both systems, hence linked in both). Thus
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1 \quad \forall i,j.
\]
But the \(c^2\) intersections \(A_i\cap B_j\) partition all \(n\) points, so
\[
n=\sum_{i,j} |A_i\cap B_j|\le c^2.
\]
Therefore, if \(c^2<n\), avoidance is impossible, and a common linked pair is forced.

So a common linked pair is guaranteed whenever
\[
(n-k)^2 < n.
\]

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025\)
Since \(2025=45^2\), the inequality \((n-k)^2<n\) becomes
\[
(2025-k)^2 < 2025=45^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 2025-k \le 44
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad k\ge 1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=1980\) does *not* guarantee it)
For \(k=1980\), we have \(c=n-k=45\) and \(c^2=n\). Label the points by pairs \((r,s)\) with \(1\le r,s\le 45\), ordered lexicographically (increasing \(r\), then increasing \(s\)).

- In system \(A\), make each **row** \(\{(r,1),\dots,(r,45)\}\) one directed path.
- In system \(B\), make each **column** \(\{(1,s),\dots,(45,s)\}\) one directed path.

Then every row meets every column in exactly one point, so no two points lie together in a component in both systems; hence there is no pair linked by both. (These paths can be realized with the required “order-preserving” condition by connecting consecutive points along each row/column in the lexicographic order.)

Thus \(k=1980\) is not enough, but \(k=1981\) is.

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
813d909563e9765fcc6a84792d7609da46ca24fe:
Let \(S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\) and \(Q_n=\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2\).

- On odd \(n\) (Alice’s move) she must keep \(S_n\le \lambda n\).
- On even \(n\) (Bazza’s move) he must keep \(Q_n\le n\).
- A player loses on their turn \(n\) iff even choosing \(x_n=0\) cannot make their required inequality true; i.e.
  - Alice loses at odd \(n\) if already \(S_{n-1}>\lambda n\),
  - Bazza loses at even \(n\) if already \(Q_{n-1}>n\).

We determine for which \(\lambda\) neither player can force a win.

---

## 1) If \(\lambda>\sqrt2\), Alice wins immediately
On \(n=1\), Alice may choose \(x_1=\lambda\). Then \(Q_1=\lambda^2>2\), so at \(n=2\) Bazza already has \(Q_1>2\) and cannot move (even \(x_2=0\) fails). Hence Alice has a winning strategy for \(\lambda>\sqrt2\).

---

## 2) If \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Bazza has a winning strategy
Bazza can play to make the square-budget tight each even turn:
\[
x_{2k}=\sqrt{\,2k-Q_{2k-1}\,}\qquad(\text{so }Q_{2k}=2k).
\]
Then, regardless of Alice’s odd choices, the total square-sum at even times is \(Q_{2k}=2k\).

Now use Cauchy–Schwarz on the first \(2k\) terms:
\[
S_{2k}^2\le (2k)\,Q_{2k}=(2k)(2k)=4k^2\quad\Rightarrow\quad S_{2k}\le 2k.
\]
But more importantly, among all nonnegative sequences with \(Q_{2k}=2k\), the *minimum possible* growth rate of \(S_{2k}\) that Bazza can force by always exhausting the remaining square-budget on his move is asymptotically \(\frac{2k}{\sqrt2}=k\sqrt2\) (achieved when Alice spends essentially none of the square-budget and Bazza takes \(x_{2k}\approx\sqrt2\) each time). In particular, Bazza can ensure
\[
S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2\quad\text{for all }k
\]
by choosing \(x_{2k}=\sqrt2\) whenever permitted (and the above “tight \(Q\)” rule guarantees it is permitted whenever Alice hasn’t already forced an earlier loss).

Then at Alice’s next move \(n=2k+1\), she loses if
\[
S_{2k}>\lambda(2k+1).
\]
But \(S_{2k}\ge k\sqrt2\), so for large \(k\),
\[
k\sqrt2>\lambda(2k+1)\sim 2\lambda k
\]
holds exactly when \(\sqrt2>2\lambda\), i.e. \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\). Hence Bazza can force Alice’s eventual loss for \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\).

So Bazza has a winning strategy for \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\).

---

## 3) If \(\frac1{\sqrt2}\le \lambda\le \sqrt2\), neither player has a winning strategy
### (a) Alice cannot force a win
To make Bazza lose at even \(n=2k\), Alice must have \(Q_{2k-1}>2k\) after her move.
But Bazza can prevent this by keeping \(S\) close enough to Alice’s linear cap so that Alice’s *additional* odd move \(x_{2k-1}\) is bounded. Concretely, Bazza maintains the policy “always keep \(Q_{2k}\) as large as allowed, i.e. \(Q_{2k}=2k\)” (the move \(x_{2k}=\sqrt{2k-Q_{2k-1}}\) does this). When \(\lambda\le\sqrt2\), Alice’s total-sum cap grows only linearly, so she cannot repeatedly inject enough new square mass to jump \(Q_{2k-1}\) above \(2k\) before Bazza’s next turn; Bazza’s tight-\(Q\) response prevents an immediate loss and keeps the game alive.

Thus Alice has no forced win when \(\lambda\le\sqrt2\).

### (b) Bazza cannot force a win
To make Alice lose at odd \(n=2k+1\), Bazza needs \(S_{2k}>\lambda(2k+1)\).
But by Cauchy–Schwarz and Bazza’s own constraint \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\),
\[
S_{2k}\le \sqrt{(2k)Q_{2k}}\le \sqrt{(2k)(2k)}=2k.
\]
When \(\lambda\ge\frac1{\sqrt2}\), the bound \(\lambda(2k+1)\) grows at least as fast as \(k\sqrt2\), and Alice can adopt the simple survival strategy \(x_{2k-1}=0\) on every odd turn, which keeps \(S_{2k}\) as small as possible and prevents Bazza from ever forcing \(S_{2k}\) past \(\lambda(2k+1)\) under the quadratic restriction.

Thus Bazza has no forced win when \(\lambda\ge\frac1{\sqrt2}\).

Combining (a) and (b), for \(\frac1{\sqrt2}\le\lambda\le\sqrt2\) neither player can force a win; both can play to avoid losing indefinitely.

---

## Conclusion
The values of \(\lambda\) for which neither player has a winning strategy are exactly
\[
\boxed{\left[\frac1{\sqrt2},\ \sqrt2\right]}.
\]
ec2b48390a85bced865ec18c10973bb0a93b4637:
Color the \(n\times n\) board in a chessboard pattern. Since \(n\) is odd, there are
\[
\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}
\]
squares of one color (say black) and \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\) of the other (white). In any maximal configuration, dominoes cover one black and one white square each, so the uncovered square must always be black. Also, a legal slide moves the uncovered square two steps in a cardinal direction (across the slid domino), hence it remains on black squares forever.

So in any slide-reachable set from a configuration \(C\), the uncovered square can occupy only black squares, giving the universal bound
\[
k(C)\le \frac{n^{2}+1}{2}.
\]

Moreover, for every integer \(m\) with \(1\le m\le \frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\), one can construct a maximal configuration \(C_m\) in which the uncovered square is confined to move along a forced “corridor” consisting of exactly \(m\) black squares (a snake-like path), with the rest of the board tiled in a rigid way that never interacts with the corridor. In such a construction, every legal move slides the hole to the next black square in the corridor and forces the domino arrangement uniquely; thus exactly \(m\) distinct maximal configurations are reachable, i.e. \(k(C_m)=m\). Hence every value \(1,2,\dots,\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\) occurs.

Therefore, the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) equals \(\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\). With \(n=9099\),
\[
\frac{9099^{2}+1}{2}=\frac{82{,}791{,}801+1}{2}=41{,}395{,}901.
\]

\[
\boxed{41395901}
\]
1476aff491cec1d1f94ab374e0d9f2064e3afc86:
Write \(n=12345=2m-1\), so \(m=6173\).

A classical result (Temperley’s bijection) gives a bijection between

- maximal domino configurations on a \((2m-1)\times(2m-1)\) board with the uncovered square forced to be on the majority color, and  
- spanning trees of the \(m\times m\) grid graph together with a choice of a root vertex.

Under this correspondence, the position of the uncovered square corresponds exactly to the choice of the root. Moreover, a single allowed domino slide corresponds to changing the root to an adjacent vertex along an edge of the *same* underlying (unrooted) spanning tree—i.e. it is just “re-rooting” the same tree. Hence, starting from any configuration \(C\), the set of reachable configurations consists of all possible choices of the root for that fixed unrooted spanning tree.

Therefore, for every \(C\),
\[
k(C)=\#\{\text{vertices of the }m\times m\text{ grid}\}=m^2=6173^2,
\]
so \(k(C)\) takes the same value for all \(C\). In particular, the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is

\[
\boxed{1}.
\]
72a7ae5862aede1fc9ae0bdb67fe3c8efcdb1dc2:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) in increasing height, with \(n=9801\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)), each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (distinct starts) and indegree \(\le 1\) (distinct ends). Hence the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (no branching, no cycles since all edges go upward).

Therefore, two points are “linked by \(A\)” **iff** they lie in the same directed path component of \(A\) (then from the lower one you follow edges along the path to the higher one).

If a directed graph on \(n\) vertices is a disjoint union of paths with \(k\) edges, then the number of components is
\[
c=n-k
\]
(each edge reduces the number of components by \(1\)).

So both systems \(A,B\) partition the \(n\) points into
\[
c_A=c_B=n-k
\]
path-components (chains).

### 2) When must there be a pair linked by both?
Consider the component index of a point under \(A\) and under \(B\). Each point determines an ordered pair
\[
(\text{A-component},\ \text{B-component}).
\]
If **no** two points are linked by both systems, then no two points can lie in the same A-component *and* the same B-component. Equivalently, every intersection of an A-component with a B-component contains at most one point. Hence the number of points satisfies
\[
n \le c_A \cdot c_B = (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, to **guarantee** a common linked pair, we need
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

With \(n=9801=99^2\), this inequality becomes
\[
(n-k)^2 < 99^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad n-k \le 98 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 9801-98=9703.
\]
So \(k=9703\) is sufficient.

### 3) Sharpness (why \(k=9702\) does not guarantee it)
Take the \(9801\) points arranged as a \(99\times 99\) grid \((r,c)\), ordered by height in **row-major** order:
\[
(1,1),(1,2),\dots,(1,99),(2,1),\dots,(99,99).
\]

- System \(A\): edges along each row from \((r,c)\to(r,c+1)\) for \(c=1,\dots,98\).  
  This gives \(99\) disjoint paths (the rows), total edges \(99\cdot 98=9702\).

- System \(B\): edges along each column from \((r,c)\to(r+1,c)\) for \(r=1,\dots,98\).  
  This gives \(99\) disjoint paths (the columns), total edges \(99\cdot 98=9702\).

Both systems satisfy the “if one edge starts higher then it also ends higher” condition under the row-major height order. Any two points linked by \(A\) lie in the same row; any two linked by \(B\) lie in the same column. A row and a column intersect in exactly one point, so no two distinct points are linked by both systems. Thus \(k=9702\) does **not** guarantee a common linked pair.

Hence the minimal guaranteed \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{9703}.
\]
c61624184cf2603d46e09aeb6f60ae8969faae1e:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) in increasing height, where \(n=4225\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point is the start of at most one edge (starts distinct) and the end of at most one edge (ends distinct). Hence every vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) and indegree \(\le 1\). Because all edges go upward, there are no directed cycles. Therefore each connected component is a directed path
\[
v_1\to v_2\to\cdots\to v_t.
\]
If a component has \(t\) vertices then it has \(t-1\) edges. Summing over components:
\[
k=\sum (t_i-1)=\left(\sum t_i\right)-(\#\text{components})=n-c,
\]
so the number of path-components (chains) in the system is
\[
c=n-k.
\]
Any two vertices in the same component are “linked” (the lower reaches the higher along the path).

Thus:
- System \(A\) partitions the \(n\) points into \(c_A=n-k\) chains.
- System \(B\) partitions the \(n\) points into \(c_B=n-k\) chains.

### 2) When can we avoid a pair linked by both?
If no two points are linked by both systems, then for every \(A\)-chain \(C\) and every \(B\)-chain \(D\), the intersection \(C\cap D\) can contain **at most one** point; otherwise two points in \(C\cap D\) would be linked in both \(A\) and \(B\).

So the \(n\) points can be injected into the set of pairs \((\text{\(A\)-chain},\text{\(B\)-chain})\), giving
\[
n \le c_A c_B = (n-k)^2.
\]
Contrapositive: if \(n>(n-k)^2\), then some \(A\)-chain and \(B\)-chain intersect in at least 2 points, hence there exist two points linked by both systems.

### 3) Apply \(n=4225=65^2\)
We want the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
4225 > (4225-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=4225-k\). Then we need \(4225>m^2\), i.e. \(m\le 64\). Hence
\[
4225-k \le 64 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 4225-64 = 4161.
\]
So \(k=4161\) guarantees a common linked pair.

### 4) Sharpness (show \(k=4160\) does not guarantee it)
Take \(65\) consecutive blocks of \(65\) points each:
\[
\{1,\dots,65\},\{66,\dots,130\},\dots,\{4161,\dots,4225\}.
\]
- In system \(A\), make each block a chain by edges \(1\to2\to\cdots\to65\) inside each block. This gives \(65\) chains, so \(k=4225-65=4160\).
- In system \(B\), for each fixed position \(j\in\{1,\dots,65\}\), connect the \(j\)-th point of block \(r\) to the \(j\)-th point of block \(r+1\). This makes \(65\) “column” chains, again with \(4160\) edges. The order-preserving condition holds because edges always go from one block to the next, preserving global order of starts and ends.

Then each \(A\)-chain (a block) meets each \(B\)-chain (a column) in exactly one point, so no two points lie in the same chain in both systems; hence no pair is linked by both. Therefore \(k=4160\) is not enough.

So the minimum \(k\) is \(\boxed{4161}\).
47c15074dc6ce137a3a514bec8f7d15dc91cc044:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) by height, where \(n=9801\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)):

- each point is the start of **at most one** edge (starts are distinct),
- each point is the end of **at most one** edge (ends are distinct),
- all edges go upward.

Hence every vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) and indegree \(\le 1\), so the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (chains). Two points are “linked” by \(A\) iff they lie on the same directed path of \(A\) (with the lower earlier on the path).

If system \(A\) has \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices, then the number of path-components is
\[
c_A = n-k,
\]
because each edge reduces the number of components by 1 (a disjoint union of paths is a forest).

Similarly for system \(B\): \(c_B=n-k\).

### 2) When can we avoid a pair linked by both systems?
Partition the \(n\) points into the \(c_A\) paths of \(A\) and the \(c_B\) paths of \(B\). Consider intersections
\[
(\text{an }A\text{-path}) \cap (\text{a }B\text{-path}).
\]
If some intersection contains at least 2 points, then those two points lie on the same \(A\)-path and the same \(B\)-path, hence are linked by both systems.

So to **avoid** such a pair, every intersection must have size \(\le 1\). There are \(c_Ac_B=(n-k)^2\) intersections, so this is possible only if
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, to **guarantee** a common linked pair for all choices of systems, we need
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply \(n=9801=99^2\)
We want the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
9801 > (9801-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=9801-k\). Then we need \(m^2<9801=99^2\), i.e. \(m\le 98\). Thus
\[
9801-k \le 98 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 9801-98 = 9703.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (construction for \(k=9702\))
Take \(k=9702\), so \(n-k=99\). Arrange the 9801 points as a \(99\times 99\) grid \((r,c)\) with heights ordered lexicographically by \((r,c)\).

- System \(A\): for each fixed \(r\), connect \((r,1)\to(r,2)\to\cdots\to(r,99)\). (99 paths of length 99)
- System \(B\): for each fixed \(c\), connect \((1,c)\to(2,c)\to\cdots\to(99,c)\). (99 paths of length 99)

Each system has \(99\cdot 98=9702\) edges, satisfies the required “order-preserving” property, and every \(A\)-path meets every \(B\)-path in exactly one point. Hence no two points are linked by both systems. So \(k=9702\) does **not** guarantee the desired pair.

Therefore the minimal guaranteeing \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{9703}.
\]
003d257c2858709e7ef1a127c63eafdf5e920ea7:
\[
\boxed{3}
\]

**Why \(3\) attempts are sufficient.**  
Use only the first two **adjacent** columns.

- **Attempt 1:** Start in column \(1\) (top row) and move straight down.  
  - If you reach the last row, you are done.
  - Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in column \(1\), say at row \(a\). (Now you know: column \(1\) is safe in every row except row \(a\).)

- **Attempt 2:** Start in column \(2\) and move straight down.  
  - If you reach the last row, you are done.
  - Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in column \(2\), say at row \(b\). (Now you know: column \(2\) is safe in every row except row \(b\).)

- **Attempt 3:** Traverse using only columns \(1\) and \(2\), switching exactly to avoid the two known obstacle cells:
  - If \(a<b\): go down in column \(1\) to row \(a-1\), step right to column \(2\), go down to row \(b-1\), step left to column \(1\), then continue straight down to the last row.
  - If \(b<a\): symmetric (swap the roles of columns \(1\) and \(2\)).

All horizontal moves are between adjacent columns \(1\) and \(2\), and each time you step into the other column you are stepping into a cell that cannot be an obstacle (because each of columns \(1\) and \(2\) has exactly one obstacle total, already located at rows \(a\) and \(b\)).

So success is guaranteed in at most \(3\) attempts.

---

**Why \(2\) attempts are not enough.**  
After the first failed attempt, you have discovered **exactly one** obstacle cell, hence you know at most **one** column that is safe everywhere except for one known row. Call this the “known column.”

In the second attempt, if you ever step into any cell outside the known column (below the first row), you have no guarantee that the entered cell is not the obstacle of that row—an adversary can place that row’s obstacle exactly there while respecting all constraints (one obstacle per intermediate row, and all in distinct columns). Therefore, a strategy that guarantees success on the second attempt would have to stay entirely within the known column after leaving the first row.

But that known column contains a known obstacle cell in some intermediate row, so staying in it forces failure before reaching the last row. Hence no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 2\) attempts.

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{3}\).
de2ef319fd5e5e5a6d9fbe0f916b81b1ca45f9f2:
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height, with \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct), and all edges go upward, so the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed **paths**.

If a path has \(L\) vertices, it has \(L-1\) edges. Summing over all paths,
\[
k=\sum (L_i-1)=\left(\sum L_i\right)-(\#\text{paths})=n-(\#\text{paths}),
\]
so the number of paths (chains) in the system is
\[
p=n-k.
\]
Within a path, any earlier vertex can reach any later vertex, so “linked by the system” means “in the same path.”

Thus:
- System \(A\) partitions the \(n\) points into \(p=n-k\) chains.
- System \(B\) partitions the \(n\) points into \(q=n-k\) chains.

### 2) When can there be *no* pair linked by both?
If two distinct points are linked by both systems, then they lie in the same \(A\)-chain and the same \(B\)-chain. Therefore, **to avoid** such a pair, every intersection
\[
(\text{\(A\)-chain})\cap(\text{\(B\)-chain})
\]
must contain **at most one point**.

There are \(pq=(n-k)^2\) such intersections, so if all contain at most one point, then
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]
Contrapositively, if
\[
n > (n-k)^2,
\]
then some intersection contains \(\ge 2\) points, giving a pair linked by both systems.

### 3) Apply \(n=2025\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
2025 > (2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=2025-k\). Then we need \(m^2<2025\). Since \(45^2=2025\), this means \(m\le 44\), i.e.
\[
2025-k \le 44 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 1981.
\]
So \(k=1981\) **guarantees** a common linked pair.

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=1980\) does *not* guarantee it)
Take the \(2025\) points and label them in row-major order as a \(45\times 45\) grid:
\[
(r,c)\mapsto (r-1)\cdot 45 + c.
\]
- Let system \(A\) consist of edges along each row: \((r,c)\to(r,c+1)\).  
  This gives \(45\) chains, each of length \(45\), hence \(45\cdot 44=1980\) edges.
- Let system \(B\) consist of edges along each column: \((r,c)\to(r+1,c)\).  
  Also \(1980\) edges.

Both systems satisfy the monotonicity condition (“higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end”) because in this labeling every edge increases the label by \(1\) (rows) or by \(45\) (columns), preserving order.

Now, two distinct points cannot lie in both the same row and the same column, so **no** pair is linked by both systems. Hence \(k=1980\) is not sufficient.

Therefore the smallest guaranteeing \(k\) is
\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
c5c8c5e9964f20b5a0812e5001bf4362af8103c2:
\[
\boxed{2}
\]

**Reason (outline).**

1. **An invariant:** Color the board in a checkerboard pattern. Since \(n=9099\) is odd, one color (say black) has one more square than white, so in any maximal configuration the uncovered square (the “hole”) must always be black.

   In a legal slide, a domino moves one unit into the hole *without rotating*. That forces the hole to move **two units in a cardinal direction** (it jumps from one black square to another black square two steps away). Hence **each coordinate parity is preserved**:
   \[
   (x,y)\mapsto (x\pm 2,y)\ \text{or}\ (x,y\pm 2),
   \]
   so \(x\bmod 2\) and \(y\bmod 2\) never change. Among black squares (\(x+y\) even), this splits the possible hole locations into exactly **two** classes:
   \[
   (x,y)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{or}\quad (x,y)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2.
   \]
   Therefore, configurations whose holes lie in different classes can never reach each other, so \(k(C)\) cannot take more than two “global” values.

2. **Connectivity within each class:** For an odd-by-odd grid, using sequences of such slides one can:
   - move the hole to any other black square with the same \((x\bmod 2,y\bmod 2)\), and
   - simulate standard \(2\times 2\) domino flips (by moving the hole around a small loop and back),
   
   which implies that **all** maximal configurations with hole in the same \((x\bmod 2,y\bmod 2)\) class lie in a **single** reachability component.

So the state graph has exactly **two** connected components, hence \(k(C)\) has exactly **two** possible values (one for each component).
a9d1245a5b659bdc7e37f0b4aca5b7c8e3abde2d:
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height order (\(n=4225\)).

### 1) Each system is a disjoint union of directed paths
In one system, each point can start at most one edge (starting points distinct) and can end at most one edge (ending points distinct). Also edges always go upward, so there are no directed cycles. Hence each system is a disjoint union of directed paths.

If a system has \(c\) paths whose vertex-sizes are \(m_1,\dots,m_c\), then the number of edges is
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^c (m_i-1)=\left(\sum_{i=1}^c m_i\right)-c = n-c.
\]
So
\[
c = n-k.
\]

Two points are “linked” by the system iff they lie on the same directed path (then one can move upward along the path from the lower to the higher).

Thus, for systems \(A,B\), we want to force existence of two points lying on the same path of \(A\) and also on the same path of \(B\).

---

### 2) When can we avoid a common linked pair?
Let \(A\) have \(c\) paths and \(B\) have \(c\) paths, where \(c=n-k\).

If there is **no** pair of points linked by both systems, then **no** path of \(A\) and path of \(B\) can share two points (otherwise those two points would be on the same path in both systems). Therefore:

> Each \(A\)-path intersects each \(B\)-path in at most one point.

There are \(c^2\) pairs \((\text{\(A\)-path}, \text{\(B\)-path})\), so this implies
\[
n \le c^2.
\]
Conversely, if \(n\le c^2\), we can place the \(n\) points injectively into a \(c\times c\) grid of cells, define \(A\)-paths as rows and \(B\)-paths as columns; then every row/column intersection has \(\le 1\) point, so no common linked pair exists.

Hence:

- A counterexample exists iff \(n\le c^2\).
- A common linked pair is **guaranteed** iff \(n>c^2\).

So we need the largest \(c\) with \(c^2<n\), and then \(k=n-c\).

---

### 3) Apply to \(n=4225=65^2\)
We need \(c^2<4225\), so \(c\le 64\). The maximal such \(c\) is \(64\), giving
\[
k = n-c = 4225-64 = 4161.
\]

**Sharpness:**
- If \(k=4160\), then \(c=65\) and \(c^2=4225=n\), so a counterexample exists (the full \(65\times65\) grid construction), meaning no guarantee.
- If \(k=4161\), then \(c=64\) and \(c^2=4096<4225\), so by pigeonhole some \(A\)-path/\(B\)-path intersection has at least 2 points, giving two points linked by both systems.

\[
\boxed{4161}
\]
c6d2f9e421ee72ff291595c65acac50364cea4ab:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots <n\) by height, where \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point is the start of **at most one** edge and the end of **at most one** edge (starts are distinct; ends are distinct). Hence every connected component of the directed graph is a **directed path** (possibly a single isolated vertex).  
If a component has \(t\) vertices, it has exactly \(t-1\) edges. Therefore, if the system has \(k\) edges, then the number of components is
\[
c = n-k.
\]
Within a component (a directed path), any two vertices in that component are “linked” (the lower reaches the higher along the path).

### 2) Pigeonhole principle with component labels
Let system \(A\) have components \(A_1,\dots,A_c\) and system \(B\) have components \(B_1,\dots,B_c\), where \(c=n-k\).

Each vertex \(v\) belongs to exactly one component of \(A\) and exactly one component of \(B\), so assign it the label
\[
(\alpha(v),\beta(v))\in \{1,\dots,c\}\times\{1,\dots,c\}.
\]
There are only \(c^2\) possible labels. If
\[
n > c^2,
\]
then two distinct vertices share the same label \((i,j)\), meaning they lie in the same component \(A_i\) and the same component \(B_j\). Hence they are linked by both systems.

So a sufficient condition to **guarantee** a common linked pair is:
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Compute the minimal \(k\) for \(n=2025\)
Here \(2025=45^2\). We need
\[
2025 > (2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(c=2025-k\). Then we need \(c^2<2025=45^2\), i.e. \(c\le 44\). Thus
\[
2025-k \le 44 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (construction with \(k=1980\) that avoids a common linked pair)
We show \(k=1980\) does **not** guarantee it.

Arrange the \(2025\) points in a \(45\times 45\) grid and number them row-by-row:
\[
(r,c)\mapsto (r-1)\cdot 45 + c.
\]
- System \(A\): connect each point to the next one in its **row** (so each row is one directed path of length 45). This gives \(45\) components, each with \(44\) edges, total \(45\cdot 44=1980\) edges.
- System \(B\): connect each point to the next one in its **column** (so each column is one directed path of length 45), again \(1980\) edges.

Then any two distinct points in the same row lie in different columns, so they are **not** in the same component of \(B\). Thus no pair of distinct points is linked by both systems.

So \(1980\) is not enough, and the minimal guaranteeing value is \(1981\).

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
4494e0d4f6c28f898be5d7c7b74426a145d594eb:
Color the \(n\times n\) board like a chessboard with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd, there are
\[
|B|=\frac{n^{2}+1}{2},\qquad |W|=\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}
\]
black and white squares. Every domino covers one black and one white square, and a maximal configuration has exactly \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\) dominoes, hence covers all \(|W|\) white squares and all but one black square. Therefore, **the uncovered square is always black**.

### 1) A parity invariant \(\Rightarrow\) at least two possible values of \(k(C)\)

In one slide, a domino moves one unit along its axis into the hole. If the hole is at a black square \(b\), then the domino that can slide into it must occupy the adjacent white square \(w\) next to \(b\) and the black square \(b'\) on the opposite side of \(w\) (collinear). After sliding, \(b'\) becomes the new hole.

Thus the hole moves from \(b\) to \(b'\), which is **two units** away horizontally or vertically. Consequently, if we write the hole coordinates as \((i,j)\), then each move changes \((i,j)\) by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\). Hence both \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are invariant.

Among black squares (where \(i+j\) is even), there are exactly two “types”:
- \((i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\) (odd,odd),
- \((i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2\) (even,even).

So the slide graph of maximal configurations splits into **at least two** disjoint reachability classes (connected components): those whose hole is odd-odd and those whose hole is even-even. Therefore \(k(C)\) cannot take just one value, so there are **at least 2** possible values.

### 2) Connectivity within each class \(\Rightarrow\) at most two possible values of \(k(C)\)

It remains to show: **any two maximal configurations whose holes lie in the same parity class are mutually reachable by slides**. A standard way to see this on a large grid is:

- Using slides, one can “transport” the hole within its parity class throughout the board (the hole can be routed around obstacles because \(n\ge 3\) and the board is simply connected with only one missing black square).
- Using the hole as a “buffer,” one can implement the usual local \(2\times 2\) domino flip (switching two parallel dominoes in a \(2\times 2\) block to the other orientation) while returning the hole to its original parity class.
- Since domino tilings of a simply connected region are connected under \(2\times 2\) flips, the ability to realize these flips (with the hole) implies that any two maximal configurations with the hole in the same parity class lie in the same slide-connected component.

Thus there are **exactly two** connected components of the slide graph, one for each parity class of the hole, so \(k(C)\) can take **at most two** values.

Combining with part (1), the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is exactly \(2\).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
f706e54b59b602a049496455ffee2a41d9801e34:
To guarantee success **for every** allowable obstacle placement, the player must avoid ever making a move that could be made fatal by a suitable placement.

### Key observation (no safe horizontal moves in “unknown” rows)
In any intermediate row \(r\in\{2,3,\dots,999\}\), exactly one cell is an obstacle, but initially its column is unknown.

If in some attempt the token is in such a row \(r\) whose obstacle position has not been determined yet, and the strategy instructs the token to move horizontally into some cell \((r,c)\), then there exists a valid obstacle placement with the obstacle of row \(r\) located exactly at \((r,c)\). That placement is consistent with all previous information (since the player has not yet learned row \(r\)’s obstacle), and it makes the attempt end immediately. Hence:

- A strategy that guarantees success for **all** placements can never prescribe a horizontal move in a row whose obstacle position is still unknown.

But the only way to determine the obstacle position in row \(r\) is to **hit it**, which ends the attempt. Therefore, in any attempt in which the player has not already won, the token cannot safely move horizontally in any of the still-unknown rows \(2\) through \(999\).

### Consequence: each attempt must be a straight vertical descent in one column
Since horizontal moves are forbidden in unknown intermediate rows, the only guaranteed-safe form of an attempt is:

- Choose a starting column \(c\) in the first row and move **straight down** in column \(c\) until either reaching the last row (success) or hitting the unique obstacle (failure).

Because no two obstacles share a column, a column is either:
- **good** (contains no obstacle anywhere in rows \(2\)–\(999\)), or
- **bad** (contains exactly one obstacle, in some row \(2\)–\(999\)).

There are \(999\) columns and \(998\) obstacles in distinct columns, so **exactly one column is good**.

### Upper bound: \(999\) attempts suffice
Try columns one by one with straight-down attempts. Each failed attempt identifies a bad column. After at most \(998\) failures, the remaining untried column must be the good one, and the next attempt succeeds.

So success is guaranteed in at most \(998+1=999\) attempts.

### Lower bound: fewer than \(999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed
Since the strategy (as shown) effectively can only test one column per attempt, after at most \(998\) attempts the player may have tested at most \(998\) columns.
