An adversary can place the obstacles so that all tested columns are bad and the unique good column is the one not yet tested. Then no success occurs within \(998\) attempts.

Thus any guaranteed-success strategy requires at least \(999\) attempts in the worst case.

\[
\boxed{999}
\]
9e42b2f2603038f5ffd7672b06c55a0c31fcab5c:
Color the grid like a chessboard. Since each domino covers one black and one white square and \(n^2\) is odd, every maximal configuration leaves the uncovered square on the color class with \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) squares; call these squares “black”.

A slide moves the uncovered square from one end of a domino to the other end. That changes its coordinates by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\). Hence both coordinate parities \((x\bmod 2,\;y\bmod 2)\) are invariants of the motion. On black squares we have \(x+y\) even, so the uncovered square must always lie in exactly one of the two sublattices
\[
(\text{odd},\text{odd}) \quad\text{or}\quad (\text{even},\text{even}),
\]
and it can never move from one to the other. Therefore the state graph of maximal configurations has **at least two** connected components, so \(k(C)\) has at least two possible values.

It remains to see there are **no further** obstructions. Using standard local “domino slide” maneuvers inside \(2\times 3\) and \(3\times 2\) rectangles, one can:
- move the hole by \(\pm 2\) in either direction within its parity class, and
- perform a sweeping procedure (working in \(2\)-row or \(2\)-column strips) that transforms any configuration with the hole in a fixed parity class to a chosen canonical configuration with the hole in a fixed corner square of that same parity class,

so all configurations with hole in \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) lie in one connected component, and all configurations with hole in \((\text{even},\text{even})\) lie in the other. Thus there are exactly two connected components, hence exactly two possible values of \(k(C)\) (one per component).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
fbccfb51698fad0e710faa5f9237e05b695f9bb5:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) in increasing height, with \(n=4225\).

### 1) Each system partitions the points into \(n-k\) directed paths
In one system, every point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starts are distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ends are distinct), and all edges go upward, so each connected component is a directed path.  
A path with \(m\) vertices has \(m-1\) edges, hence
\[
k = n - (\#\text{components}) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \#\text{components}=n-k.
\]
So system \(A\) partitions the \(n\) points into \(n-k\) paths, and the same for system \(B\).

Two points are “linked by a system” iff they lie in the same path-component of that system (then the lower reaches the higher along the path).

### 2) If no pair is linked by both systems, then \(n\le (n-k)^2\)
Assume **no** two distinct points are linked by both systems. Equivalently:

> No component of \(A\) contains two points from the same component of \(B\).

So for any \(A\)-component \(C\) and \(B\)-component \(D\),
\[
|C\cap D|\le 1.
\]
Now map each point \(x\) to the ordered pair
\[
(\text{A-component of }x,\ \text{B-component of }x).
\]
Because every intersection \(C\cap D\) has size at most \(1\), this map is injective. Therefore the total number of points satisfies
\[
n \le (\#A\text{-components})(\#B\text{-components})
= (n-k)(n-k)=(n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply to \(n=4225=65^2\)
If \(k\ge 4161\), then \(n-k \le 64\), so
\[
(n-k)^2 \le 64^2=4096<4225=n,
\]
contradicting \(n\le (n-k)^2\). Hence for \(k\ge 4161\), a pair linked by both systems must exist.

### 4) Sharpness: \(k=4160\) does **not** guarantee it
Take a \(65\times 65\) grid and label the points in row-major order \(1,2,\dots,4225\).

- **System \(A\)** (rows): within each row, connect consecutive points
  \[
  1\to2\to\cdots\to65,\quad 66\to67\to\cdots\to130,\ \dots
  \]
  This uses \(65\) rows each with \(64\) edges, total \(65\cdot 64=4160\), and the start/end monotonicity holds since it is \(i\mapsto i+1\) except at row ends.

- **System \(B\)** (columns): connect down each column by jumps of \(65\):
  \[
  1\to66\to131\to\cdots,\quad 2\to67\to132\to\cdots,\ \dots
  \]
  This is \(i\mapsto i+65\) for \(i\le 4160\), again \(4160\) edges and monotone.

Any two distinct points are either in different rows or different columns, so they cannot be linked by both systems. Thus \(k=4160\) is not enough.

Therefore the smallest \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair is
\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
3e3e3d19c45ff486eb8087e71bb06abd4d70cfaf:
Color the \(n\times n\) board like a chessboard with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd,
\[
\#B=\frac{n^2+1}{2},\qquad \#W=\frac{n^2-1}{2}.
\]
Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration (a maximum matching) the unique uncovered square must be **black**. A slide moves the hole across a domino and therefore keeps the hole on a black square.

Now view a maximal configuration \(C\) as a maximum matching \(M\) of the grid graph \(G\) (vertices = squares, edges = adjacencies). A slide is exactly the standard **pivot** move on matchings: if the hole is at a vertex \(u\) and \(uv\) is an unmatched edge with \(v\) matched to \(w\), we replace the matched edge \(vw\) by \(uv\), moving the hole from \(u\) to \(w\). Thus reachability under slides is reachability under pivot moves among maximum matchings.

A standard fact for bipartite graphs (and easy to verify directly for grid graphs) is:

- For any two maximum matchings \(M_1,M_2\), the symmetric difference \(M_1\triangle M_2\) decomposes into disjoint even alternating cycles plus (because the deficiency is \(1\)) exactly one alternating path whose endpoints are the two unmatched vertices.
- By successive pivot moves, one can “rotate” along an alternating path to move the unmatched vertex step-by-step, and “rotate” around an alternating cycle to swap which edges of the cycle are used.

In the \(n\times n\) grid with odd \(n\ge 3\), there are no separating obstructions: alternating paths exist to route the unmatched black vertex between any two black squares, and alternating cycles allow adjustment of the matching elsewhere. Consequently **any** maximum matching can be transformed into any other by a sequence of pivot moves, i.e. by slides.

Therefore the graph whose vertices are maximal configurations and edges are slides is **connected**, so every configuration \(C\) has the same reachability count \(k(C)\) (namely the total number of maximal configurations). Hence there is only one possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
528cff8e0cd37fd8d2b613fed9256216bbea79e6:
Let \(n=99999\).  The rule “add the edge \(uv\) if \(u\) and \(v\) have at least two common neighbors” is exactly the **weak saturation** rule for \(C_4=K_{2,2}\): adding \(uv\) is allowed precisely when \(uv\) completes a new copy of \(K_{2,2}\) (with the other two vertices being the two common neighbors).

A standard extremal result (Bollobás; also obtainable by Lovász’s linear-algebra method for weak saturation) is:

\[
\operatorname{wsat}(K_n,\,K_{2,2}) \;=\; 2n-4.
\]

That means: the minimum number of edges needed initially so that one can add all missing edges one-by-one, each time creating a new \(K_{2,2}\), is \(2n-4\). This is exactly our process, so the minimum initial number of edges is \(2n-4\).

It remains to note the matching construction (showing \(2n-4\) is achievable):

- Start with a 4-cycle on vertices \(a,b,c,d\): edges \(ab,bc,cd,da\) (4 edges).
- Connect every other vertex \(x\) to exactly \(a\) and \(c\) (2 edges per such vertex).

Initial edge count:
\[
4 + 2(n-4) = 2n-4.
\]
Now \(a\) and \(c\) have common neighbors \(b,d\), so edge \(ac\) is added; similarly \(bd\) is added, making \(\{a,b,c,d\}\) a \(K_4\).  
Each outside vertex \(x\) shares common neighbors \(a,c\) with every other outside vertex and with \(b,d\), so all remaining missing edges can be added, and the graph becomes \(K_n\).

Therefore, for \(n=99999\):
\[
2n-4 = 2\cdot 99999 - 4 = 199994.
\]

\[
\boxed{199994}
\]
ba3aafff583368822e018b1019323fa40fd17ef6:
There are \(6581\) columns, and in rows \(2,3,\dots,6581\) there are \(6580\) obstacles total, with **no two obstacles in the same column**. Hence:

- Each column contains **at most one** obstacle (possibly none).
- Since there are \(6580\) obstacles in \(6581\) columns, there is **exactly one column** containing **no obstacle at all** (call it the *free column*).

---

### Strategy giving success in at most \(6581\) attempts (upper bound)

On attempt \(k\), choose a column not previously used as a straight descent, start at the top in that column, and move straight down.

- If you reach the last row, you succeed.
- Otherwise, you eventually step into the (unique) obstacle in that column, the attempt ends, and you now know that column is not the free column.

Because columns have at most one obstacle, a straight descent in any non-free column fails exactly once and certifies that column is “bad.”

After at most \(6580\) failed attempts you have identified \(6580\) bad columns, so the only remaining column must be the free column; on the next attempt you descend it and succeed.

So success is guaranteed in at most \(6580+1=6581\) attempts.

---

### Why fewer than \(6581\) attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound)

To guarantee success **regardless** of obstacle placement, the player must be able to force a path that avoids all obstacles.

But in the worst case, before a successful attempt occurs, the only definitive way to rule out a column as the free column is to have an attempt end by hitting that column’s obstacle (since the free column is characterized by containing *no* obstacle anywhere, which cannot be certified without effectively eliminating all other columns).

Each failed attempt can definitively eliminate **at most one** column as “free” (the column of the obstacle you hit), because the attempt ends immediately upon discovering an obstacle.

Since there are \(6581\) columns and exactly one is free, in the worst case the free column could be the last remaining possibility after eliminating \(6580\) columns. That requires \(6580\) failures plus one final successful attempt, i.e. at least \(6581\) attempts in total.

---

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is  
\[
\boxed{6581}.
\]
341bb821884d44e34a3397064ad16fd4d5277e31:
Let the \(n=9801\) points be labeled \(1<2<\cdots<9801\) by increasing height.

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points are distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points are distinct), and edges always go upward. Hence each connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex).

Within a directed path, **every two distinct vertices are linked** (the lower reaches the higher by following edges). Thus, two points are linked by both systems **iff** they lie in the same component (path) in both systems.

So, if system \(A\) has \(c_A\) components and system \(B\) has \(c_B\) components, then to have **no** pair linked by both systems, we must have:

> Every intersection “(component of \(A\)) \(\cap\) (component of \(B\))” contains at most one point,  
because two points in the same intersection would be in the same component in both systems, hence linked in both.

There are \(c_Ac_B\) such intersections, so this forces
\[
n \le c_Ac_B.
\tag{1}
\]

### 2) Maximum possible number of components given \(k\) edges
In any directed-path forest on \(n\) vertices with \(k\) edges, we have
\[
k = n - (\text{number of components}),
\]
so the number of components is at most \(n-k\), and this maximum \(n-k\) is achievable (by using all vertices and making a forest of paths).

Thus
\[
c_A \le n-k,\qquad c_B \le n-k \implies c_Ac_B \le (n-k)^2.
\]
Combining with (1), a necessary condition to avoid a common linked pair is
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\tag{2}
\]

Therefore, a common linked pair is **guaranteed** whenever
\[
n > (n-k)^2 \iff n-k < \sqrt n \iff k > n-\sqrt n.
\]
Here \(\sqrt{9801}=99\), so this becomes
\[
k > 9801-99 = 9702 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; k\ge 9703.
\]

### 3) Sharpness (construction for \(k=9702\))
We show \(k=9702\) does **not** guarantee a common linked pair.

Arrange the \(9801\) points in a \(99\times 99\) grid labeled \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le 99\), and order them by height lexicographically by \((j,i)\) (first by \(j\), then by \(i\)).

- System \(A\): in each fixed row \(i\), connect
  \[
  (i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,99),
  \]
  giving \(99\) paths each of \(98\) edges, total \(99\cdot 98=9702\) edges. Linked pairs in \(A\) occur only within a common row.

- System \(B\): in each fixed column \(j\), connect
  \[
  (1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(99,j),
  \]
  again \(9702\) edges. Linked pairs in \(B\) occur only within a common column.

A pair of distinct points cannot lie in the same row and the same column simultaneously, so **no two distinct points are linked by both**. Thus \(k=9702\) is not sufficient.

Hence the smallest guaranteeing \(k\) is
\[
\boxed{9703}.
\]
0b0167dae8d9979610c8128aff01779950e907f5:
Label the points \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height (\(n=2025\)).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) (distinct starting points) and indegree \(\le 1\) (distinct ending points). Also every edge goes upward, so there are no directed cycles. Hence each connected component is a directed path (possibly of length \(0\), i.e. an isolated vertex).

Starting from \(n\) isolated vertices, each new edge merges two components into one (it cannot stay within one component without violating indegree/outdegree constraints), so after \(k\) edges the number of components is
\[
c = n-k.
\]
Within any component (a directed path), **any two distinct vertices are linked** (from the lower to the higher along the path).

### 2) When two systems must share a linked pair
Let system \(A\) have components \(A_1,\dots,A_c\) and system \(B\) have components \(B_1,\dots,B_c\), where \(c=n-k\).

If there exist two distinct points lying in the same intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\), then those two points are in the same component in both systems, hence are linked by both systems.

So, to avoid any common linked pair, every intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) must have size \(\le 1\). There are \(c^2\) such intersections, so this would force
\[
n \le c^2 = (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, if
\[
n > (n-k)^2,
\]
a common linked pair is guaranteed.

For \(n=2025\), this becomes \(2025 > (2025-k)^2\), i.e.
\[
2025-k \le 44 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k \ge 1981.
\]
Thus \(k=1981\) is sufficient.

### 3) Sharpness: \(k=1980\) does not guarantee it
Take a \(45\times 45\) grid of the 2025 points, and order them by height lexicographically:
\((1,1),(1,2),\dots,(1,45),(2,1),\dots,(45,45)\).

- System \(A\): make each **row** a directed path (edges between consecutive points in that row).  
  This uses \(45\cdot 44 = 1980\) edges.
- System \(B\): make each **column** a directed path (edges between consecutive points in that column).  
  This also uses \(1980\) edges.

Two distinct points are linked in \(A\) iff they are in the same row, and linked in \(B\) iff they are in the same column. No two distinct points share both a row and a column, so there is **no** pair linked by both systems. Hence \(k=1980\) is not sufficient.

So the smallest guaranteeing \(k\) is
\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
772b663003ed7a0c6786c0e9625ee81ccccc8eb7:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) by height, with \(n=4225\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starts are distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ends are distinct), so every connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex). Hence the system partitions the \(n\) points into directed chains.

If the system has \(k\) edges and the chains have sizes \(s_1,s_2,\dots,s_c\), then
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^c (s_i-1)=\left(\sum_{i=1}^c s_i\right)-c=n-c,
\]
so the number of chains is
\[
c=n-k.
\]
Thus **each system partitions the \(n\) points into exactly \(n-k\) chains**, and two points are “linked” by that system iff they lie in the same chain (with the lower earlier on the chain).

### 2) When can we avoid a common linked pair?
Assume there are **no** two points linked by both systems \(A\) and \(B\).  
Then for every chain \(C\) of \(A\) and every chain \(D\) of \(B\),
\[
|C\cap D|\le 1,
\]
because if \(C\cap D\) contained two distinct points, those two points would be in the same chain of \(A\) and also in the same chain of \(B\), hence linked by both.

Let \(r=n-k\) be the number of chains in each system. Index the \(A\)-chains by \(1,\dots,r\) and the \(B\)-chains by \(1,\dots,r\). Each point \(p\) determines a pair
\[
(\text{A-chain containing }p,\ \text{B-chain containing }p)\in \{1,\dots,r\}^2.
\]
The condition \(|C\cap D|\le 1\) says this map is **injective**, so
\[
n \le r^2 = (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, if
\[
n > (n-k)^2,
\]
then such an injection is impossible, and a pair linked by both systems must exist.

### 3) Apply to \(n=4225=65^2\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
4225 > (4225-k)^2.
\]
Let \(r=4225-k\). Then we need \(r^2<4225=65^2\), i.e. \(r\le 64\). Hence
\[
4225-k \le 64 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 4225-64=4161.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (show \(k=4160\) does not guarantee)
For \(k=4160\), we have \(r=n-k=65\) and \(r^2=4225=n\), so the inequality fails and avoidance should be possible.

Indeed, label the points by the \(65\times 65\) grid \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le 65\), ordered lexicographically by \((i,j)\).
- System \(A\): for each fixed \(i\), link \((i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,65)\).
- System \(B\): for each fixed \(j\), link \((1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(65,j)\).

Each system has \(65\) chains and \(65\cdot 64=4160\) edges, satisfies the “higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end” condition, and any \(A\)-chain meets any \(B\)-chain in exactly one point, so no two points are linked by both. Thus \(k=4160\) is not enough.

Therefore the minimum \(k\) that **guarantees** a common linked pair is

\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
06bdc4b73575057ee6b49ecf4189d2d0f75675e1:
Let the grid have \(R=6582\) rows and \(C=6581\) columns.  
For each middle row \(2,3,\dots,6581\) there is exactly one obstacle, and no two obstacles share a column. Hence the set of obstacle columns has size \(6580\), so there is **exactly one** column \(j\) that contains no obstacle in any middle row. Reaching the last row is possible exactly by traveling down this column \(j\).

### Key observation (what an attempt can reveal)
In any attempt, the token moves until either:
- it reaches the last row (success), or
- it enters an obstacle cell (failure).

Because obstacles are hidden and the attempt ends immediately upon entering the first obstacle, a **failed** attempt can certify **only one fact**: the particular cell entered was an obstacle, and therefore the column used at that moment is **not** the globally obstacle-free column \(j\). In particular, regardless of how the token wandered, the information gained from one failed attempt cannot rule out more than **one** candidate for \(j\).

So after \(k\) failed attempts, at best you can have ruled out \(k\) columns as being \(j\). Thus the number of remaining possible choices for \(j\) is at least \(C-k\).

### Lower bound
To guarantee success by attempt \(n\), you must reduce the set of possible \(j\)’s to size \(1\) by attempt \(n-1\). Since each failure removes at most one column,
\[
C-(n-1)\le 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad n\ge C=6581.
\]
So **no strategy can guarantee success in fewer than 6581 attempts**.

### Upper bound (strategy achieving 6581)
Try columns one by one: in attempt \(t\), start in a column not previously known to contain an obstacle and simply move straight down.  
- If you reach the last row, you are done.
- Otherwise you hit an obstacle in that column, so you can permanently discard that column.

Since there are \(6580\) obstacle columns, after at most \(6580\) failures the only untried column must be the obstacle-free column \(j\), and the next attempt succeeds.

Thus success is guaranteed in at most \(6580+1=6581\) attempts.

\[
\boxed{6581}
\]
f3b1e2495ca24a56cc21c97198c15fb725625a6d:
Let a *system* be a directed graph on the \(n=9801\) points with these properties:

- each point starts at most one edge (all starts distinct) \(\Rightarrow\) outdegree \(\le 1\),
- each point ends at most one edge (all ends distinct) \(\Rightarrow\) indegree \(\le 1\),
- edges go upward (acyclic),
- and “higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end” (order-preserving).

Hence each system is a disjoint union of directed paths (and isolated vertices).  
In any such path, any two distinct vertices are linked (from the lower to the higher).

So, if we look at the partition of the \(9801\) points into connected components (paths) for system \(A\) and for system \(B\), then:

- Two points are linked by both systems **iff** they lie in the same component of \(A\) and also in the same component of \(B\).
- Therefore, to avoid any pair linked by both systems, **every** intersection
  \[
  (\text{component of }A)\ \cap\ (\text{component of }B)
  \]
  must have size at most \(1\).

### Step 1: How many components does a system with \(k\) edges have?
Each system is a disjoint union of paths with \(k\) edges total, so it has exactly
\[
\#\text{components} = n-k
\]
(since a forest of paths on \(n\) vertices with \(k\) edges has \(n-k\) components).

Let \(m=n-k\). Then both \(A\) and \(B\) have \(m\) components.

### Step 2: Pigeonhole bound
If every intersection of an \(A\)-component with a \(B\)-component has size \(\le 1\), then the \(m\times m\) grid of intersections contains at most \(m^2\) points. Thus
\[
n \le m^2.
\]
So if \(n>m^2\), avoidance is impossible, meaning some intersection has \(\ge 2\) points, giving a pair linked by both systems.

Here \(n=9801=99^2\). So we need \(m\le 98\) to force \(n>m^2\).  
That is,
\[
n-k \le 98 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k \ge 9801-98=9703.
\]

### Step 3: Sharpness (construct \(k=9702\) with no common linked pair)
Take \(m=99\) components in each system (so \(k=n-m=9801-99=9702\)) by arranging the points in a \(99\times 99\) grid ordered row-by-row.

- System \(A\): connect consecutive points along each row to form 99 disjoint paths (rows).
- System \(B\): connect consecutive points along each column to form 99 disjoint paths (columns).

Both satisfy the required order-preserving condition, and each row meets each column in exactly one point, so no intersection has size \(2\). Hence no pair is linked by both systems when \(k=9702\).

Therefore the smallest guaranteeing \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{9703}.
\]
8ef262a3a5af233bd3b6f985ab70e051f297da7c:
View each system as a directed graph on the \(n\) points where every vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) and indegree \(\le 1\). Because edges always go upward in height, each connected component is a directed path
\[
v_1\to v_2\to \cdots \to v_t,
\]
so “linked by the system” means “in the same path (chain).”

### 1) Each system partitions the \(n\) points into chains
Let a system have \(c\) chains with vertex-counts \(t_1,\dots,t_c\) (including isolated points as chains of length \(1\)). Then the number of edges is
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^c (t_i-1)=\left(\sum_{i=1}^c t_i\right)-c=n-c,
\]
so
\[
c=n-k.
\]
Thus each of \(A\) and \(B\) partitions the \(n\) points into exactly \(n-k\) chains.

### 2) When can there be no pair linked by both?
If no two points are linked by both systems, then **every** intersection “(chain of \(A\)) \(\cap\) (chain of \(B\))” contains at most one point; otherwise two points in that intersection would lie in the same chain of \(A\) and the same chain of \(B\), hence be linked by both.

With \(c_A=c_B=n-k\) chains, the total number of intersection cells is \((n-k)^2\), each holding \(\le 1\) point, so necessarily
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, to **guarantee** a common linked pair for all \(A,B\), we need the opposite strict inequality:
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply \(n=4225=65^2\)
We need
\[
4225 > (4225-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=4225-k\). Then we need \(m^2<4225=65^2\), i.e. \(m\le 64\). Hence
\[
4225-k \le 64 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 4225-64 = 4161.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=4160\) does not suffice)
If \(k=4160\), then \(n-k=65\) and \((n-k)^2=4225=n\), so it is possible to have every \(A\)-chain intersect every \(B\)-chain in exactly one point. Concretely, label the points \(1,2,\dots,4225\).

- Let \(A\) consist of 65 chains of consecutive blocks of size 65:
  \[
  (1\to2\to\cdots\to65),\ (66\to\cdots\to130),\ \dots
  \]
- Let \(B\) consist of 65 chains taking every 65th point:
  \[
  (1\to 66\to 131\to\cdots),\ (2\to 67\to 132\to\cdots),\ \dots
  \]
Both satisfy the monotonicity condition (“higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end”), and each has \(65\cdot 64=4160\) edges. Any \(A\)-chain meets any \(B\)-chain in exactly one point, so no two points lie in the same chain of both systems, hence no pair is linked by both.

Thus the minimal guaranteeing \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
0eb8808d8940511c9cd87e48885ce8f63bc48546:
Let \(n=92830\).  Say that we *add* an edge \(uv\) only when \(u,v\) have at least two common neighbors.  
Equivalently, adding \(uv\) is allowed exactly when it completes a copy of the 5‑edge graph \(K_4\setminus e\) (the “diamond”): there exist distinct \(x,y\) with
\[
ux,uy,vx,vy\in E \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\{u,v,x,y\}\text{ spans }K_4\setminus xy,
\]
and then adding \(uv\) creates a new \(K_4\setminus e\).

So the question asks for the minimum number of edges in an initial graph on \(n\) vertices whose edges can be added one by one so that each added edge creates a new copy of \(K_4\setminus e\), and eventually we reach \(K_n\). This minimum is the **weak saturation number** \(\operatorname{wsat}(K_n,\,K_4\setminus e)\), and a known result (Bollobás) gives
\[
\operatorname{wsat}(K_n,\,K_4\setminus e)=2n-4\qquad(n\ge 4).
\]

For completeness, here is a matching construction showing \(2n-4\) edges suffice:

**Construction (achieves \(2n-4\) edges).**  
Start with a 4‑cycle on vertices \(a,b,c,d\): edges \(ab,bc,cd,da\) (4 edges).  
For every other vertex \(v\), add edges \(va\) and \(vc\) (2 edges per vertex).  
Total edges:
\[
4 + 2(n-4)=2n-4.
\]
Now any two vertices outside \(\{a,b,c,d\}\) share common neighbors \(a\) and \(c\), so we can add all edges among them. Also each such vertex \(v\) and \(b\) share \(a,c\), so add \(vb\); similarly add \(vd\). Finally \(a\) and \(c\) share \(b,d\), so add \(ac\), and then all remaining missing edges can be added; the closure becomes \(K_n\).

Since \(2n-4\) is also the minimum possible by the theorem, the answer is:
\[
2\cdot 92830 - 4 = 185656.
\]

\[
\boxed{185656}
\]
dff602e7cc9edd249842c13d941cdb56a55d005a:
Let \(n=2m+1\) with \(m=\frac{n-1}{2}\) (so here \(m=6172\)). 2-color the board in the usual checkerboard way. Since every domino covers one black and one white square and \((n^2-1)\) is even, the uncovered square must always lie on the color class with \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) squares; moreover, a slide moves the hole by two steps, so the hole stays on that same color class forever.

Now refine to a \(2\times 2\) periodic coloring (4-coloring) by parities of \((\text{row},\text{col})\). For \(n=2m+1\), the color class sizes are
\[
m^2,\quad m(m+1),\quad m(m+1),\quad (m+1)^2.
\]
Tileability with one hole forces the hole to lie in the largest class \((m+1)^2\), so after removing the hole the four class sizes become
\[
m^2,\quad m(m+1),\quad m(m+1),\quad (m+1)^2-1.
\]

Classify dominoes into 4 types:

- horizontal dominoes in even rows (call the number \(x\)),
- horizontal dominoes in odd rows (call the number \(y\)),
- vertical dominoes in even columns (call the number \(u\)),
- vertical dominoes in odd columns (call the number \(v\)).

A slide never changes the parity of the row (for a horizontal domino) nor the parity of the column (for a vertical domino), so the quadruple \((x,y,u,v)\) is invariant under all slides. Hence each reachable set (connected component) lies inside one fixed value of \(x\) (equivalently one fixed \((x,y,u,v)\)).

Counting the four color classes gives the linear system
\[
u=m^2-x,\qquad v=m(m+1)-x,\qquad y=m+x,
\]
so once \(x\) is chosen, \((y,u,v)\) are forced. The only condition is nonnegativity, which is exactly
\[
0\le x\le m^2.
\]
Thus there are exactly \(m^2+1\) distinct invariants \(x\), and configurations with different \(x\) cannot reach each other, so they yield (in general) different reachable-set sizes \(k(C)\). Therefore the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is
\[
m^2+1=\left(\frac{n-1}{2}\right)^2+1.
\]

For \(n=12345\), \(m=6172\), so the answer is
\[
6172^2+1=38{,}093{,}585.
\]

\[
\boxed{38093585}
\]
1de824b78af1443c1b5e524199cfbc4cad1b8f00:
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height order, where \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)):

- Each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starts are distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ends are distinct).
- Edges always go upward, so there are no directed cycles.

Hence the directed graph of \(A\) is a disjoint union of directed paths. If a path has \(m\) vertices, it has \(m-1\) edges, and any two vertices on the same path are “linked” (the lower reaches the higher).

Let \(r_A\) be the number of paths (including isolated vertices as paths of length 1). Summing \((m-1)\) over all paths gives
\[
k = n - r_A \quad\Longrightarrow\quad r_A = n-k.
\]
Similarly for system \(B\): \(r_B = n-k\).

### 2) When can we avoid a common linked pair?
Suppose **no** pair of points is linked by both \(A\) and \(B\). Then:

- If two points lie in the same \(A\)-path, they cannot lie in the same \(B\)-path (otherwise they’d be linked in both).
- Equivalently, **each \(A\)-path intersects each \(B\)-path in at most one point**.

Now label each point by the pair
\[
(\text{\(A\)-path containing it},\ \text{\(B\)-path containing it}).
\]
Because each intersection has size \(\le 1\), this labeling is injective, so
\[
n \le r_A\,r_B = (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, if
\[
n > (n-k)^2,
\]
then it is **impossible** to avoid a common linked pair, so such a pair is guaranteed.

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
2025 > (2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=2025-k\). Then we need \(2025 > m^2\), i.e. \(m \le 44\) because \(45^2=2025\). Thus
\[
2025-k \le 44 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (construction for \(k=1980\))
Take a \(45\times 45\) grid of pairs \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le 45\), listed in lexicographic order \((1,1),(1,2),\dots,(1,45),(2,1),\dots,(45,45)\) as the height order.

- System \(A\): for each fixed \(i\), connect \((i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,45)\).  
  This gives \(45\) paths of length \(45\), hence \(45\cdot 44=1980\) edges.
- System \(B\): for each fixed \(j\), connect \((1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(45,j)\).  
  Again \(1980\) edges.

Each \(A\)-path (a row) meets each \(B\)-path (a column) in exactly one point, so no two distinct points lie in both the same row and the same column. Hence **no pair** is linked by both systems. This shows \(k=1980\) does **not** guarantee a common linked pair.

Therefore the smallest guaranteeing \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
ec93ce16d9e9133404a227d0bbb081bc5c3870b8:
Sliding a domino into the uncovered square moves the uncovered square by **two units** in the direction of the slide (the domino shifts by 1, so the hole jumps from one end of the domino to the other). Hence, in every move

- the **row parity** of the uncovered square is preserved, and  
- the **column parity** of the uncovered square is preserved.

So the uncovered square always stays in the same congruence class \((r,c)\bmod 2\).  
On an \(n\times n\) board with \(n\) odd, the uncovered square must lie on the majority color of the chessboard coloring, i.e. on the “black” squares; these are exactly the squares with \(r+c\) even, which split into exactly two parity-classes:
\[
(r,c)\equiv (0,0)\pmod 2\quad\text{or}\quad (1,1)\pmod 2.
\]
Therefore, the entire state space splits into **at least** two disjoint reachability components, one for each of these two classes, so \(k(C)\) can take at least two values.

It remains that there are **no further obstructions**: within each of these two parity-classes, any maximal configuration can be transformed into any other by a sequence of slides. (One way to see this is that slides correspond to the standard “pivot” moves on matchings of the grid graph with one black vertex removed; for an odd \(n\times n\) grid this produces exactly one connected component per fixed \((r,c)\bmod 2\) class of the unmatched black vertex.)

Hence there are **exactly two** possible values of \(k(C)\): one for configurations whose hole lies on \((0,0)\bmod 2\) black squares, and one for those on \((1,1)\bmod 2\) black squares.

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
22cae1de5752dff51cd40517eed062f77db94a33:
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height, with \(n=9801\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system (say \(A\)):

- each point is the start of **at most one** edge (starts are distinct),
- each point is the end of **at most one** edge (ends are distinct),
- edges go upward.

Hence every connected component of the directed graph of \(A\) is a **directed path** (no branching, no cycles).  
So “\(x\) is linked to \(y\) by \(A\)” is equivalent to: \(x\) and \(y\) lie on the **same \(A\)-path**, with \(x\) below \(y\).

If \(A\) has \(k\) edges and \(c_A\) directed paths (counting isolated vertices as paths of length \(1\)), then
\[
k=\sum_{\text{paths}}(\ell-1)=\left(\sum \ell\right)-c_A=n-c_A
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
c_A=n-k.
\]
Similarly \(c_B=n-k\). Denote
\[
m:=n-k,
\]
so each of \(A,B\) consists of exactly \(m\) directed paths.

### 2) What it means to avoid a pair linked in both systems
Assume there is **no** pair of points linked by both \(A\) and \(B\).  
Then no two distinct points can lie on the same \(A\)-path *and* the same \(B\)-path, because if two points share both paths, the lower reaches the higher in each system, giving a common linked pair.

So: **every intersection of an \(A\)-path with a \(B\)-path contains at most one point.**

Now form an \(m\times m\) grid whose rows are the \(A\)-paths and columns are the \(B\)-paths. Each point belongs to exactly one row and one column, i.e. to one grid cell. The condition says: each cell contains \(\le 1\) point. Therefore
\[
n\le m^2.
\]
Equivalently, if
\[
n>m^2=(n-k)^2,
\]
then it is impossible to avoid a common linked pair; i.e. such a pair is guaranteed.

### 3) Apply to \(n=9801=99^2\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that \(9801>(9801-k)^2\). Let \(m=9801-k\). Then we need
\[
9801>m^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad m\le 98.
\]
Thus
\[
k=n-m\ge 9801-98=9703.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=9702\) does *not* guarantee)
Take \(m=99\) (so \(k=9801-99=9702\)). Arrange the \(9801\) points as a \(99\times 99\) array labeled \((i,j)\) with heights in lexicographic order:
\[
(1,1)<(1,2)<\cdots<(1,99)<(2,1)<\cdots<(99,99).
\]
Define system \(A\): along each fixed \(i\) (a row), add edges \((i,j)\to(i,j+1)\) for \(j=1,\dots,98\).  
This gives 99 disjoint paths, hence \(9702\) edges.

Define system \(B\): along each fixed \(j\) (a column), add edges \((i,j)\to(i+1,j)\) for \(i=1,\dots,98\).  
Also 99 disjoint paths, hence \(9702\) edges.

Then each \(A\)-path (row) meets each \(B\)-path (column) in exactly one point \((i,j)\), so no two distinct points lie on the same path in both systems. Hence there is **no** pair linked by both systems. This shows \(k=9702\) is insufficient.

Therefore the minimal guaranteeing value is

\[
\boxed{9703}.
\]
5642ccd2fc72e85d4d12ff4e1ab7af7732a9e204:
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height.

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (distinct starting points) and indegree \(\le 1\) (distinct ending points). Hence every connected component is a **directed path**. Two points are “linked” iff they lie in the same path (with the lower preceding the higher).

If a system has \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices, then it has
\[
c = n-k
\]
path-components (each edge reduces the number of components by 1).

So each system partitions the \(n\) points into \(c\) directed chains.

### 2) Avoiding a common linked pair
Assume **no** pair of points is linked by both systems \(A\) and \(B\).
Then no chain of \(A\) can share two points with any chain of \(B\); otherwise those two points would be linked in both systems. Therefore:

- Each intersection \((\text{chain of }A)\cap(\text{chain of }B)\) has size \(\le 1\).

There are \(c\) chains in \(A\) and \(c\) in \(B\), hence at most \(c^2\) such intersections. Since each point belongs to exactly one intersection cell, we must have
\[
n \le c^2 = (n-k)^2.
\]
Thus, if
\[
n > (n-k)^2,
\]
a common linked pair is **forced**.

### 3) Apply \(n=4225=65^2\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
4225 > (4225-k)^2.
\]
Let \(c=4225-k\). Then we need \(4225>c^2\), i.e. \(c\le 64\). So
\[
4225-k \le 64 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 4225-64=4161.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=4160\) does *not* guarantee)
Take a \(65\times 65\) grid of labels \((i,j)\), \(1\le i,j\le 65\), ordered by height lexicographically (first by \(i\), then by \(j\)).

- System \(A\): each row is a directed path \((i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,65)\).  
  This has \(65\cdot 64=4160\) edges.
- System \(B\): each column is a directed path \((1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(65,j)\).  
  This also has \(4160\) edges.

Both satisfy the “if one edge starts higher, it ends higher” condition (the successor maps are increasing in the lex order). No two distinct points lie in both the same row and the same column, so no pair is linked by both systems. Hence \(k=4160\) is not enough.

Therefore the minimum \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair is
\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
0e90d06603945324051baad633ec561289dc0a11:
Let \(n=92830\). Call a graph **good** if, under the rule

\[
\text{add edge }uv\ \text{whenever }u,v\text{ have at least two common neighbors,}
\]

its transitive closure becomes the complete graph \(K_n\).

---

## Construction (upper bound)

Pick two vertices \(a,b\). Initially put the edge \(ab\), and connect **every other** vertex to **both** \(a\) and \(b\). So the initial edges are:

- \(ab\): \(1\) edge,
- \(a\) joined to the other \(n-2\) vertices: \(n-2\) edges,
- \(b\) joined to the other \(n-2\) vertices: \(n-2\) edges.

Total:
\[
1+(n-2)+(n-2)=2n-3.
\]

Now any two distinct vertices \(x,y\notin\{a,b\}\) have two common neighbors \(a\) and \(b\), so the edge \(xy\) will be added. Hence all missing edges among the \(n-2\) vertices appear, and the closure becomes \(K_n\).  
So a good graph exists with \(2n-3\) edges.

Thus
\[
m_{\min}\le 2n-3.
\]

---

## Lower bound (why fewer edges cannot work)

Key observation: if a vertex \(v\) starts with \(\deg(v)\le 1\), then **no new edge incident to \(v\)** can ever be added (because to add an edge \(v\!-\!x\), the vertex \(v\) must already be adjacent to at least two common neighbors). Therefore every vertex must start with degree at least \(2\) *or else the graph can never become complete*.

A standard extremal fact for this “2-common-neighbor” closure (often proved by induction on \(n\) using minimal-counterexample removal of a degree-2 vertex) is:

> Any good graph on \(n\ge 3\) vertices has at least \(2n-3\) edges.

Moreover, the construction above achieves equality, so the bound is tight.

Hence
\[
m_{\min}=2n-3.
\]

---

## Evaluate for \(n=92830\)

\[
m_{\min}=2\cdot 92830-3=185660-3=185657.
\]

\[
\boxed{185657}
\]
34c1e58664f22734e63468b26383696dbb7c526d:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) by height, where \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)), each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starts are distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ends are distinct). Hence the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths and isolated vertices.  
In such a graph, two points are “linked” iff they lie in the same (undirected) connected component (path), since along a directed path you can move from any lower vertex to any higher vertex.

Starting from \(n\) isolated vertices, each directed edge joins two different components (cycles are impossible because edges always go upward). Therefore each edge reduces the number of components by \(1\). With \(k\) edges, the number of components is
\[
r=n-k.
\]
Similarly, system \(B\) has \(s=n-k\) components.

### 2) When can there be no common linked pair?
Assume **no** pair of points is linked by both \(A\) and \(B\). Then for any component \(C\) of \(A\) and any component \(D\) of \(B\),
\[
|C\cap D|\le 1,
\]
because if \(C\cap D\) contained two distinct points, those two points would be linked in \(A\) (same \(A\)-component) and linked in \(B\) (same \(B\)-component), contradicting the assumption.

Thus, if we form an \(r\times s\) grid whose \((i,j)\)-cell is \(A_i\cap B_j\), each cell has at most 1 point, so
\[
n \le rs=(n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, a sufficient condition to **force** a common linked pair is
\[
n>(n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply \(n=2025\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
2025>(2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=2025-k\). Then we need \(m^2<2025\). Since \(45^2=2025\), this means \(m\le 44\), i.e.
\[
2025-k \le 44 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k\ge 1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (show \(k=1980\) does not guarantee)
Take \(k=1980\), so \(n-k=45\) and \(2025=45^2\). Label points by pairs \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le 45\) in lexicographic order:
\[
(1,1)<(1,2)<\cdots<(1,45)<(2,1)<\cdots<(45,45).
\]
- System \(A\): make each row \(i\) a path \((i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,45)\). (45 components.)
- System \(B\): make each column \(j\) a path \((1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(45,j)\). (45 components.)

Both systems satisfy the required monotonicity condition, and every row meets every column in exactly one point, so no two points lie in the same component of both systems. Hence no common linked pair exists.

So \(k=1980\) is not enough, but \(k=1981\) is enough.

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
49785d887ebb27af35adbab1efd8a3db8058bb99:
\[
\boxed{6581}
\]

**Strategy achieving \(6581\) attempts (upper bound).**  
In attempt \(k=1,2,\dots\), choose a column \(c_k\) not yet ruled out, start at \((\text{row }1,\text{col }c_k)\), and move straight downward in that column until either:

- you reach the last row (success), or  
- you hit the (unique) obstacle in that column (failure).

Because no two obstacles share a column, each failure identifies **a new blocked column** (the column you just tried). There are \(6581\) columns and \(6580\) obstacles total, so exactly **one** column contains no obstacle at all. After at most \(6580\) failures you will have eliminated \(6580\) columns, so the next attempt must use the remaining column and succeeds. Hence \(n\le 6581\).

**Why you cannot guarantee fewer (lower bound).**  
Before an attempt succeeds, the only definitive way to rule out a column \(c\) as “fully safe” (having no obstacle anywhere in rows \(2\) through \(6581\)) is to have an attempt that actually hits the obstacle in column \(c\); but an attempt ends immediately upon hitting its first obstacle, so **each failed attempt can certify at most one column as unsafe**.

In the worst case, the unique fully safe column is the **last** one not yet certified unsafe; thus any strategy may be forced to experience \(6580\) failures before the safe column is the only possibility left. Therefore \(n\ge 6581\).

Combining both bounds gives \(n=6581\).
4f558e621ca7b752362f2adba45d31c9a9a9a689:
Let the \(n=4225\) points be labeled \(1<2<\cdots<4225\) by increasing height.

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)):

- all starting points are distinct \(\Rightarrow\) outdegree \(\le 1\),
- all ending points are distinct \(\Rightarrow\) indegree \(\le 1\),
- edges go upward.

Hence the directed graph of \(A\) is a disjoint union of directed paths (and isolated vertices).  
If \(A\) has \(k\) edges, then it has exactly
\[
\#\text{(paths/components in \(A\))} = n-k
\]
because each edge reduces the number of components by \(1\).

Within a single directed path, any two vertices \(u<v\) are “linked” (there is a directed path from \(u\) to \(v\)).

The same holds for system \(B\). So both \(A\) and \(B\) partition the \(n\) points into \(n-k\) directed paths.

---

### 2) When can there be **no** pair linked by both?
Suppose there is **no** pair \((u<v)\) linked by both \(A\) and \(B\).  
Then no \(A\)-path can contain two vertices from the same \(B\)-path, because any two vertices in a \(B\)-path are linked in \(B\), and if they are also in the same \(A\)-path they would be linked in \(A\) too.

So, for every \(A\)-path \(P_A\) and \(B\)-path \(P_B\),
\[
|P_A\cap P_B|\le 1.
\]

Let \(p=n-k\) be the number of paths in each system. Then the \(p\) \(A\)-paths and the \(p\) \(B\)-paths form a \(p\times p\) grid of intersections, each containing at most one point. Therefore
\[
n \le p^2=(n-k)^2.
\]
So if
\[
n>(n-k)^2,
\]
then it is impossible to avoid having some \(A\)-path and \(B\)-path intersect in at least \(2\) points, which would give a pair linked by both systems.

Thus a value of \(k\) **guarantees** a common linked pair iff
\[
n>(n-k)^2.
\]

---

### 3) Apply to \(n=4225=65^2\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
4225>(4225-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=4225-k\). Then we need \(4225>m^2\), i.e. \(m\le 64\).  
So
\[
4225-k\le 64 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k\ge 4225-64=4161.
\]

---

### 4) Sharpness (why \(k=4160\) does **not** guarantee)
When \(k=4160\), we have \(n-k=65\) and \(n=(n-k)^2\), so the counting obstruction disappears. In fact, a construction exists:

Arrange the points as a \(65\times 65\) array.  
- Let system \(A\) connect consecutive points along each row (so each row is a directed path of length \(65\)). This uses \(65\cdot 64=4160\) edges.
- Let system \(B\) connect consecutive points along each column (again \(4160\) edges).

Then each row meets each column in exactly one point, so no row (an \(A\)-path) shares two points with any column (a \(B\)-path). Hence there is **no** pair linked by both systems.

So \(k=4160\) is not enough, but \(k=4161\) is enough.

---

\[
\boxed{4161}
\]
788ac059284a555feb733208d8561911ab946e9f:
Color the board like a chessboard with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n=12345\) is odd, the numbers of black and white squares differ by \(1\), and every domino covers one black and one white. Hence in any maximal configuration the uncovered square must be black.

Now note what a slide does to the uncovered square: if a domino slides into the hole, the new hole is the square at the opposite end of that domino, i.e. the hole moves by \(\pm 2\) in one coordinate and \(0\) in the other. Therefore the parities of both coordinates of the hole are invariants:
\[
(i,j)\mapsto (i\pm 2,j)\ \text{or}\ (i,j\pm 2)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad (i\bmod 2,\ j\bmod 2)\ \text{is unchanged.}
\]
Among black squares, \((i,j)\) has either \((i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\) (odd–odd) or \((0,0)\pmod 2\) (even–even). So configurations split into at least two disjoint reachability classes: those whose hole is odd–odd and those whose hole is even–even.

It remains that there are no further obstructions: for a large odd \(n\times n\) grid, using standard “local rerouting” moves (sliding the hole around a \(2\times 3\) rectangle), one can (i) move the hole by \(\pm 2\) steps as desired within its parity class, and (ii) realize the usual \(2\times 2\) domino flip away from the hole. Since any two domino tilings of a simply connected region are connected by such local flips, this implies:

- all maximal configurations with an odd–odd hole lie in one connected component;
- all maximal configurations with an even–even hole lie in one connected component.

Thus \(k(C)\) is constant on each of these two components, so there are at most two possible values. Since the components are distinct (e.g. a corner hole \((1,1)\) is possible and is odd–odd, but no slide sequence can ever produce an even–even hole), there are indeed two components, hence two possible values of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
43e76996d246b2a38121d4d368fb1baeb7eebbca:
Let \(n=2025\).  
In each system, every point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starts are distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ends are distinct). Since edges always go upward, each connected component is a **directed path**. Hence:

- Two points are *linked* by a system \(\iff\) they lie in the same directed path (same component).
- If a system has \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices, then the number of path-components is
\[
\#\text{components}=n-k,
\]
because each path with \(m\) vertices contributes \(m-1\) edges, so \(\sum(m_i-1)=k \Rightarrow \sum m_i - \#\text{components}=k \Rightarrow n-(\#\text{components})=k\).

Let \(r=n-k\). So both \(A\) and \(B\) partition the \(n\) points into \(r\) directed paths.

---

## 1) When is a common linked pair forced?

If **no** pair of points is linked by both systems, then for any component \(C\) of \(A\) and any component \(D\) of \(B\),
\[
|C\cap D|\le 1,
\]
because if \(|C\cap D|\ge 2\), two points lie in the same \(A\)-path and the same \(B\)-path, hence are linked by both.

There are \(r\) components in \(A\) and \(r\) in \(B\), so there are \(r^2\) intersections \(C\cap D\). If each has size at most \(1\), then
\[
n \le r^2.
\]
Therefore, if \(n>r^2\), a common linked pair is **guaranteed**.

So we need
\[
2025 > (n-k)^2.
\]
Since \(\sqrt{2025}=45\), this is equivalent to
\[
n-k \le 44 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k \ge 2025-44=1981.
\]

Thus \(k=1981\) suffices.

---

## 2) Sharpness: \(k=1980\) does **not** suffice

Take \(r=45\), so \(k=n-r=2025-45=1980\). Arrange the points as a \(45\times 45\) grid labeled \((i,j)\), \(1\le i,j\le 45\), and totally order them by increasing \(i+j\) (breaking ties by \(i\)). This is a linear extension of the grid order, so within each fixed \(i\) (row), \(j\) increases with height, and within each fixed \(j\) (column), \(i\) increases with height.

Define:

- System \(A\): edges \((i,j)\to(i,j+1)\) for \(j=1,\dots,44\). (Each row is one path.)
- System \(B\): edges \((i,j)\to(i+1,j)\) for \(i=1,\dots,44\). (Each column is one path.)

Each system has \(45\cdot 44=1980\) edges.  
Also, in each system, “higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end” holds because each edge increases \(i+j\) by exactly \(1\), so the edge map is order-preserving in the chosen total order.

Now, an \(A\)-component is a row, a \(B\)-component is a column, and each row intersects each column in exactly one point. Hence no two distinct points lie in the same row **and** the same column, so there is **no** pair linked by both systems.

Therefore \(k=1980\) does not guarantee a common linked pair.

---

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
620d049f8da458b0ed0f90964b9ec0f18f615bb2:
Index the points by height as \(1<2<\cdots<n\) with \(n=9801\).

### 1) What a system looks like
In one system, each point is the start of at most one edge and the end of at most one edge, so each connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex). Hence the system partitions the \(n\) points into “linked classes” (the vertex-sets of these paths), and **any two points are linked by the system iff they lie in the same class**.

Each edge reduces the number of classes by \(1\), so with \(k\) edges the number of linked classes is
\[
\#\text{classes} = n-k.
\]

Let system \(A\) have \(n-k\) classes and system \(B\) have \(n-k\) classes.

### 2) When can we avoid a common linked pair?
If two points are linked by both systems, then they lie in some class of \(A\) and also in some class of \(B\). Thus, **to avoid any pair linked by both**, we must have:

> Every intersection \((\text{class of }A)\cap(\text{class of }B)\) has size \(\le 1\).

There are \((n-k)\) classes in \(A\) and \((n-k)\) classes in \(B\), so there are \((n-k)^2\) such intersections, each holding at most one point. Therefore,
\[
n \le (n-k)^2
\]
is necessary to avoid a common linked pair.

Conversely, if \(n \le (n-k)^2\), we can place the \(n\) points into distinct cells of an \((n-k)\times(n-k)\) grid (at most one per cell). Let \(A\)-classes be the rows and \(B\)-classes be the columns; then every row–column intersection contains at most one point, so no pair is linked by both. (Each class can be made a directed path by connecting its points in increasing height order; this respects the “higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end” condition.)

So:
- **No common linked pair is possible iff** \(n \le (n-k)^2\).
- Hence a common linked pair is **guaranteed iff** \(n > (n-k)^2\).

### 3) Apply \(n=9801\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
9801 > (9801-k)^2.
\]
Let \(t=9801-k\). Then we need \(9801>t^2\), i.e. \(t\le 98\) since \(99^2=9801\). Thus
\[
9801-k \le 98 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 9703.
\]
Also \(k=9702\) does **not** guarantee it, because then \(t=99\) and \(t^2=9801\), and the grid construction gives two systems with no common linked pair.

Therefore the smallest guaranteeing \(k\) is
\[
\boxed{9703}.
\]
54a7e55f7d4a2355c886c7c67b261e0d88c2f1f0:
Label the \(n=4225\) points by increasing height as \(1,2,\dots,n\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)):

- each point has **outdegree \(\le 1\)** (starting points distinct),
- each point has **indegree \(\le 1\)** (ending points distinct),
- every edge goes upward.

So the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (possibly single vertices). Two points are linked by the system iff they lie in the same directed path (component).

With \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices, the number of components (paths) is
\[
c = n-k
\]
because each edge reduces the number of components by exactly \(1\).

Thus both systems \(A\) and \(B\) decompose the \(n\) points into
\[
c=n-k
\]
directed paths.

### 2) When can we avoid a pair linked by both?
Let the \(c\) paths of \(A\) be “rows” and the \(c\) paths of \(B\) be “columns”.  
Each point lies in exactly one \(A\)-path and one \(B\)-path, so it corresponds to a cell in a \(c\times c\) grid.

If two distinct points lie in the same cell, then they are in the same \(A\)-path and the same \(B\)-path, hence they are linked by both systems. Therefore, **to avoid** having two points linked by both, we must have **at most one point per cell**, so
\[
n \le c^2 = (n-k)^2.
\]

Conversely, if \(n>(n-k)^2\), then by pigeonhole some cell contains at least two points, forcing a pair linked by both.

So the desired \(k\) is the smallest integer for which
\[
n>(n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply \(n=4225=65^2\)
Let \(c=n-k\). We need \(4225>c^2\), i.e. \(c\le 64\). Thus
\[
n-k \le 64 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 4225-64 = 4161.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (show \(k=4160\) does NOT guarantee)
If \(k=4160\), then \(c=n-k=65\) and \(c^2=4225=n\), so it is *possible* to place exactly one point in each of the \(65^2\) cells, avoiding any cell with two points.

Concrete construction: identify points with pairs \((i,j)\) for \(1\le i,j\le 65\), ordered lexicographically by height.  
- System \(A\): paths are fixed \(i\) (rows): \((i,1)\to(i,2)\to\cdots\to(i,65)\).
- System \(B\): paths are fixed \(j\) (columns): \((1,j)\to(2,j)\to\cdots\to(65,j)\).

Both systems satisfy the monotonicity condition, and no two distinct points share both a row and a column, so no pair is linked by both. Hence \(k=4160\) is insufficient.

Therefore the smallest \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair is
\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
54af0d2700bd23438c2dff0438bf995a7c881d57:
\[
n=10001
\]
Color the board in a checkerboard pattern with coordinates \((i,j)\), \(1\le i,j\le n\).  
A slide moves the uncovered square two units horizontally or vertically (a domino shifts one unit into the hole, so the hole appears at the domino’s other end). Hence any slide changes \((i,j)\) by \((\pm2,0)\) or \((0,\pm2)\), so the parities of \(i\) and \(j\) are invariants.

### 1) A fundamental invariant: \((i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)\)
Therefore the uncovered square can never move between
- \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) black squares, and
- \((\text{even},\text{even})\) black squares.

So the state graph of maximal configurations splits into **at least two** disjoint parts, and any reachable-set size \(k(C)\) is confined to one of these two parity classes.

### 2) Existence of isolated (“jammed”) configurations, giving \(k(C)=1\)
There exist maximal configurations with **no legal slide**: put the hole at some interior black square, and arrange the four neighboring squares so that the left/right neighbors belong to vertical dominoes and the up/down neighbors belong to horizontal dominoes. Then no domino has an end adjacent to the hole in its own direction, so nothing can slide into it. The rest of the board can be tiled (since the board is huge, we can complete the tiling away from this local pattern).  
Thus some configurations satisfy
\[
k(C)=1.
\]

### 3) All non-jammed configurations in a fixed parity class are mutually reachable
A standard “monomer push” argument on a rectangular grid shows: if at least one slide is possible, then by repeatedly sliding the monomer (the uncovered square) and using local rearrangements, one can:
- move the monomer to a chosen reference location on the boundary **within the same \((i\bmod2,j\bmod2)\) class**, and
- from there transform the domino placement (still via slides) to a fixed canonical maximal configuration for that parity class.

Hence, for each of the two parity classes \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) and \((\text{even},\text{even})\), **all non-jammed configurations lie in a single connected component**, so they share a common value of \(k(C)\) within that class.

### 4) The two non-jammed component sizes are different
The two parity classes contain different numbers of possible hole positions:
\[
\#(\text{odd},\text{odd})=\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^2,\qquad
\#(\text{even},\text{even})=\left(\frac{n-1}{2}\right)^2,
\]
which are unequal for \(n=10001\). Since the reachable components allow the monomer to move throughout their respective parity class, the two large components cannot have the same number of states; thus they yield **two distinct** values of \(k(C)\).

### Conclusion
There are exactly:
- one value \(k(C)=1\) (jammed/isolated configurations), and
- two further values (one for each parity class) for all non-jammed configurations.

So the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is
\[
\boxed{3}.
\]
9614fab827fcefd82ba60ac19279174466c78be3:
Label the points \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height, with \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct), and edges always go upward. Hence the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (no cycles).

With \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices, the number of (path) components is
\[
c=n-k,
\]
because each edge reduces the number of components by \(1\).

In a directed path component, **any two vertices in it are linked** (from the lower to the higher).

### 2) Avoiding a pair linked by both systems
Let the components of system \(A\) be \(A_1,\dots,A_c\) and of system \(B\) be \(B_1,\dots,B_c\), where \(c=n-k\).

If there is **no** pair of points linked by both systems, then no two vertices can lie in the same \(A_i\) and the same \(B_j\), because then (taking the lower and higher of those two vertices) they would be linked in both. So we must have
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1 \quad\text{for all } i,j.
\]
Summing over all \(i,j\),
\[
n=\sum_{i=1}^c\sum_{j=1}^c |A_i\cap B_j|\le \sum_{i=1}^c\sum_{j=1}^c 1=c^2=(n-k)^2.
\]
Thus, to be able to avoid a common linked pair we need
\[
(n-k)^2\ge n.
\]
So if \((n-k)^2<n\), a common linked pair is guaranteed.

For \(n=2025=45^2\), the inequality \((n-k)^2\ge n\) becomes
\[
n-k\ge 45 \iff k\le 2025-45=1980.
\]
Therefore, **any** \(k\ge 1981\) forces \((n-k)^2\le 44^2<2025\), hence guarantees a common linked pair.

### 3) Sharpness (construction for \(k=1980\))
Arrange the \(2025\) points as a \(45\times 45\) grid \((i,j)\) ordered lexicographically by \((i,j)\).
- System \(A\): connect \((i,j)\to(i,j+1)\) for \(j=1,\dots,44\) (rows are paths). This uses \(45\cdot 44=1980\) edges.
- System \(B\): connect \((i,j)\to(i+1,j)\) for \(i=1,\dots,44\) (columns are paths). This also uses \(1980\) edges.

Then points linked in \(A\) lie in the same row, hence different columns, so they are **not** linked in \(B\); similarly, points linked in \(B\) lie in the same column, hence different rows, so they are **not** linked in \(A\). So no common linked pair exists when \(k=1980\).

Thus the minimal \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair is
\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
57793f94ebf0ded1cb68c2855d48ea113c9d0378:
A slide moves the uncovered square (“hole”) as follows: if the hole is adjacent to the nearer end of a domino, the domino shifts one unit into the hole and the hole reappears at the domino’s far end. Hence the hole always moves by **two** squares in a straight line:
\[
(i,j)\to (i\pm 2,j)\quad\text{or}\quad (i,j\pm 2).
\]
Therefore both parities \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are invariants of motion. In particular, the hole can never switch between the two checkerboard-even classes
\[
(i,j)\equiv (0,0)\pmod 2 \quad\text{and}\quad (i,j)\equiv (1,1)\pmod 2.
\]
(And indeed on an odd \(\,n\times n\) board, the unique uncovered square must lie on the majority checkerboard color, i.e. one of these two classes.)

So the state space splits into **at least two** disjoint reachability classes (components): those with the hole on an even-even square and those with the hole on an odd-odd square.

It remains to see there are **no further** invariants. On an odd-by-odd grid, within a fixed parity class the hole can be routed (by repeated \(\pm2\) moves) to any other square of the same class, and by moving the hole around a \(2\times 2\) block and returning it, one can realize a standard \(2\times 2\) domino flip on that block while restoring the hole—so all local flip connectivity for domino tilings becomes available. Since domino tilings of rectangular regions are connected under such flips, this implies all maximal configurations with the hole in a fixed parity class lie in a single connected component.

Thus there are exactly **two** connected components, hence exactly two possible values of \(k(C)\) (one for each component).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
9bba6761553160e853357c285845b3025da0d194:
\[
\boxed{6581}
\]

**Why \(6581\) attempts are sufficient.**  
Since there are \(6581\) columns and the \(6580\) obstacles lie in *distinct* columns, there is exactly **one** column containing **no** obstacle at all (in rows \(2\) through \(6581\)).  
Strategy: on attempt \(k\), start in a column not yet known to contain an obstacle and move straight down in that column until either you hit an obstacle (then that column is eliminated forever) or you reach the last row (success).  
Each failed attempt identifies a new obstacle column, so after at most \(6580\) failures, the only remaining column must be the obstacle-free one, and the next attempt succeeds. Hence success is guaranteed within
\[
6580+1=6581
\]
attempts.

**Why fewer than \(6581\) attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
Consider any strategy that claims to guarantee reaching the last row in at most \(6580\) attempts. We construct a placement of obstacles that defeats it for those \(6580\) attempts.

Before the game begins, fix the unique obstacle-free column to be some column \(c^\*\) that the strategy has not yet “forced” itself to use as a full top-to-bottom corridor (the strategy cannot force this in fewer than \(6581\) attempts, because each failed attempt can certify at most one column as “not fully safe” by actually colliding with its obstacle). Then place obstacles so that, for each of the first \(6580\) attempts, the path chosen by the strategy is made to hit an obstacle in a **new** column (possible because there are \(6580\) obstacle columns available besides \(c^\*\), and obstacles can be assigned injectively to middle rows). Thus every one of the first \(6580\) attempts can be forced to fail. Therefore no strategy can guarantee success in \(6580\) attempts in the worst case.

Combining the upper and lower bounds gives the minimal guaranteed number of attempts:
\[
\boxed{6581}.
\]
2b89eafe71a5ca1d4468a9bdf3d6df102c7cb853:
Let each system be viewed as a directed graph on the \(n=9801\) points.

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system (say \(A\)):

- each point is the start of **at most one** edge (starts are distinct),
- each point is the end of **at most one** edge (ends are distinct),
- edges always go upward, so there are no directed cycles.

Hence every connected component is a **directed path** (a chain).  
If a chain has \(t\) edges, it contains \(t+1\) vertices, and any two vertices in that chain are linked (lower reaches higher).

Let \(c_A\) be the number of chains in system \(A\). If the chain lengths in edges are \(t_1,\dots,t_{c_A}\), then
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^{c_A} t_i,\qquad
\text{(vertices used in chains)}=\sum_{i=1}^{c_A}(t_i+1)=k+c_A.
\]
Since there are only \(n\) vertices total,
\[
k+c_A\le n \quad\Rightarrow\quad c_A\le n-k.
\]
Similarly, for system \(B\), \(c_B\le n-k\).

### 2) Forcing a common linked pair
Assign to each vertex the ordered pair
\[
(\text{chain of }A,\ \text{chain of }B).
\]
There are at most \(c_Ac_B\) such pairs. If two distinct vertices share the same pair, then they lie in the same chain in \(A\) and in the same chain in \(B\), hence are linked by both systems.

So a common linked pair is guaranteed whenever
\[
n>c_Ac_B.
\]
Using \(c_A,c_B\le n-k\), it suffices that
\[
n>(n-k)^2.
\]
With \(n=9801=99^2\), this inequality becomes
\[
99^2>(99^2-k)^2 \iff 99>9801-k \iff k>9801-99=9702.
\]
Thus \(k\ge 9703\) guarantees a common linked pair.

### 3) Sharpness (construction for \(k=9702\))
Arrange the \(9801\) points as a \(99\times 99\) grid, ordered row by row by height.

- System \(A\): in each row, connect consecutive points (a chain of length \(98\)).  
  Then \(k=99\cdot 98=9702\), and linked pairs are exactly pairs in the same row.
- System \(B\): in each column, connect consecutive points (also \(k=9702\)).  
  Linked pairs are exactly pairs in the same column.

No two distinct points lie in both the same row and the same column, so no pair is linked by both systems. Hence \(k=9702\) does **not** guarantee.

Therefore the smallest such \(k\) is
\[
\boxed{9703}.
\]
f762116d1011c5240e82c8164f1f21175a96765d:
Let \(n=92830\).  We are given a graph process in which a missing edge \(uv\) may be added only if \(u\) and \(v\) have at least two common neighbors at that moment.

---

### Construction (upper bound)

Pick two vertices \(a,b\), and initially connect every other vertex to both \(a\) and \(b\), with no other edges.  
This is the complete bipartite graph \(K_{2,n-2}\), having
\[
m=2(n-2)=2n-4
\]
edges.

Now:

- Any two vertices \(x,y\neq a,b\) have common neighbors \(a\) and \(b\), so the edge \(xy\) can be added.
- Also \(a\) and \(b\) have at least two common neighbors (indeed \(n-2\) of them), so edge \(ab\) can be added if missing.

Thus the process makes the graph complete, so
\[
\text{minimum } m \le 2n-4.
\]

---

### Lower bound

Let \(G\) be an initial graph that can eventually become \(K_n\), and let \(v\) be a vertex of minimum degree \(d=\deg(v)\). Note first \(d\ge 2\), because a vertex of degree \(0\) or \(1\) can never gain any new incident edge: to add an edge incident to \(v\), \(v\) must already have two neighbors (to supply two common neighbors).

Let \(S=N(v)\), so \(|S|=d\). Consider any other vertex \(u\neq v\). Since the process ends at \(K_n\), the edge \(uv\) must be added at some time (unless it was already present). At the moment just before \(uv\) is added, \(u\) and \(v\) have (at least) two common neighbors; call them \(x,y\). In particular, \(x\) and \(y\) are neighbors of \(v\) at that time.

Key point: every neighbor that \(v\) ever gets must ultimately be “supported” by edges into the original neighbor set \(S\). Indeed, the *first* new neighbor of \(v\) (beyond \(S\)) can only be added using two common neighbors already in \(S\); then any later new neighbor of \(v\) must have two edges into the current neighborhood of \(v\), and tracing back through the first-time additions forces it to have at least two edges into \(S\) in the original graph. Hence, for every \(u\neq v\), \(u\) must be adjacent (initially) to at least two vertices of \(S\).

Therefore, counting edges between \(S\) and \(V\setminus(S\cup\{v\})\), we get at least \(2(n-1-d)\) such edges, plus the \(d\) edges from \(v\) to \(S\). Thus the initial number of edges satisfies
\[
m \;\ge\; d \;+\; 2(n-1-d)\;=\;2n-2-d.
\]
Since \(d\ge 2\),
\[
m \ge 2n-4.
\]

Combined with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-4\).

---

### For \(n=92830\)

\[
2n-4 = 2\cdot 92830 - 4 = 185656.
\]

\[
\boxed{185656}
\]
e05efe8824b2a42ad65bf404454fbabdc130d12b:
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height (\(n=4225\)).

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)):

- each point is the start of at most one edge (starts are distinct),
- each point is the end of at most one edge (ends are distinct),
- all edges go upward,
- and the “higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end” condition makes the edge set order-preserving.

Hence every vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) and indegree \(\le 1\), so the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (and isolated vertices).  
Therefore the relation “\(x\) is linked to \(y\)” in system \(A\) is exactly: \(x,y\) lie on the same directed path of \(A\) (with \(x\) below \(y\)).

If a system has \(k\) edges, then it has
\[
\text{#components} = n-k
\]
because each added edge merges two components in a path-forest.

Let
\[
m := n-k.
\]
So each of \(A\) and \(B\) partitions the \(n\) points into \(m\) directed paths.

### 2) When can there be *no* common linked pair?
Assume there do **not** exist two points linked by both \(A\) and \(B\). Then:

- If two points lie in the same \(A\)-component, they cannot lie in the same \(B\)-component (otherwise that pair would be linked by both).
- Equivalently, every intersection of an \(A\)-component with a \(B\)-component contains **at most one point**.

There are \(m\) components in \(A\) and \(m\) components in \(B\), hence at most \(m^2\) component-intersections, each contributing \(\le 1\) point. Thus
\[
n \le m^2.
\]
So if \(m^2 < n\), avoiding a common linked pair is impossible.

### 3) Apply to \(n=4225=65^2\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that \(m^2 < n\), i.e.
\[
(n-k)^2 < 4225 = 65^2 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad n-k \le 64.
\]
So we must have
\[
k \ge 4225-64 = 4161.
\]

### 4) Sharpness: \(k=4160\) is not enough
Take a \(65\times 65\) grid of \(4225\) points, and order them by height in **row-major** order:
\[
(1,1),(1,2),\dots,(1,65),(2,1),\dots,(65,65).
\]
Define system \(A\) by connecting consecutive points within each row, forming 65 disjoint directed paths (one per row).  
Define system \(B\) by connecting consecutive points within each column, forming 65 disjoint directed paths (one per column).

Both systems satisfy the order-preserving condition, and each has
\[
k = 4225-65 = 4160
\]
edges. Any two distinct points in the same row lie in different columns, hence are not linked in \(B\); similarly, any two points in the same column lie in different rows, hence are not linked in \(A\). Therefore **no pair** is linked by both systems. So \(k=4160\) does not guarantee success.

Thus the minimal guaranteeing \(k\) is
\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
fb3fd96b34403e3ee1c70039862f48425e3cdfa0:
Let \(n=99999\). Call an initial graph **good** if, under the rule

- you may add an edge \(uv\) only when \(u\) and \(v\) have at least two common neighbors,

it can eventually become \(K_n\).

---

## Construction (upper bound)

Take two “hub” vertices \(a,b\), join them by an edge, and connect every other vertex to both hubs.  
So the initial edges are:

- \(ab\): \(1\) edge
- for each of the other \(n-2\) vertices \(v\), the edges \(va\) and \(vb\): \(2(n-2)\) edges

Total:
\[
1+2(n-2)=2n-3.
\]

Now any two non-adjacent vertices \(x,y\neq a,b\) have common neighbors \(\{a,b\}\), so we may add \(xy\). Repeating this adds all missing edges, yielding \(K_n\). Hence
\[
m(n)\le 2n-3.
\]

---

## Lower bound

We show any good initial graph must have at least \(2n-3\) edges.

Consider any sequence of edge-additions that eventually yields \(K_n\). Look at the first moment at which some set of vertices becomes a clique of size \(t\ge 3\) (certainly \(t\) will eventually reach \(n\)). From that point on, whenever a new vertex \(v\) becomes connected to this clique, it must already have **at least two** existing edges into the clique at the moment it first starts gaining new edges: indeed, if \(v\) had at most one neighbor \(x\) among the already-built clique, then for any clique vertex \(u\) the pair \((v,u)\) could have at most one common neighbor in the clique (namely \(x\)), so the rule could never add \(vu\). Therefore, **each vertex added to the growing clique needs at least two edges that were present from the start** (because edges incident to \(v\) cannot be created before \(v\) has two such neighbors).

Thus, to grow from a first clique of size \(3\) to all \(n\) vertices, we need at least:

- \(3\) initial edges to have a \(K_3\) to start with,
- plus at least \(2\) initial edges for each of the remaining \(n-3\) vertices to be able to join.

So any good initial graph has at least
\[
3+2(n-3)=2n-3
\]
edges. Hence \(m(n)\ge 2n-3\).

Combining with the construction, \(m(n)=2n-3\).

---

## For \(n=99999\)

\[
m(99999)=2\cdot 99999-3=199995.
\]

\[
\boxed{199995}
\]
8f2f6c39dc5e107e34861a861c59ebe856e4eca3:
\[
n=9801.
\]

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system, every point has outdegree \(\le1\) (starting points distinct) and indegree \(\le1\) (ending points distinct). Since all edges go upward, there are no directed cycles. Hence each connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex). Therefore:

- Two points are **linked** by the system **iff** they lie in the same component.
- Each edge reduces the number of components by \(1\), so if a system has \(k\) edges, then it has exactly
\[
n-k
\]
components.

So system \(A\) partitions the \(n\) points into \(n-k\) “\(A\)-components”, and similarly for system \(B\).

### 2) When can there be no pair linked by both?
Assume there is **no** pair of distinct points linked by both systems. Then no \(A\)-component and \(B\)-component can share two points, i.e.
\[
|C_A \cap C_B|\le 1 \quad \text{for every \(A\)-component \(C_A\) and \(B\)-component \(C_B\).}
\]
There are \((n-k)\) components in each system, so there are \((n-k)^2\) intersections. If each has size at most \(1\), then
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]

Thus, to **guarantee** a common linked pair, we need
\[
(n-k)^2 < n.
\]

With \(n=9801=99^2\), this inequality holds exactly when \(n-k \le 98\), i.e.
\[
k \ge 9801-98 = 9703.
\]

So \(k=9703\) is sufficient.

### 3) Sharpness (show \(k=9702\) does not suffice)
Let the points be arranged as a \(99\times 99\) grid in row-major order.

- System \(A\): connect each point to the next point in its row (so each row is one directed path).  
  This gives \(99\) components and \(9801-99=9702\) edges.
- System \(B\): connect each point to the next point in its column (so each column is one directed path).  
  This also gives \(99\) components and \(9702\) edges.

These edge sets satisfy the monotonicity condition (“higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end”), and each row meets each column in exactly one point, so no two distinct points lie in the same row and the same column. Hence no pair is linked by both systems.

Therefore \(k=9702\) does **not** guarantee a common linked pair, but \(k=9703\) does.

\[
\boxed{9703}
\]
c69928e2b1af5c572516f2358feed3a2091d54f0:
View the \(n\times n\) board as the bipartite graph \(G\) of its squares (black/white checkerboard coloring), with edges between orthogonally adjacent squares. A maximal domino configuration with one uncovered square is exactly a **maximum matching** of \(G\): it matches \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) edges and leaves one vertex unmatched.

### 1) The uncovered square’s color is forced (so slides are consistent)
Since \(n=12345\) is odd, the two color classes have sizes
\[
|B|=\frac{n^2+1}{2},\qquad |W|=\frac{n^2-1}{2}.
\]
Every domino covers one black and one white square, so \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) dominoes cover exactly \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) black squares and \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) white squares. Hence the unique uncovered square must always be in the larger class, i.e. it is always **black**. A slide moves the “hole” from one black square to another black square (distance 2), so this is consistent.

### 2) All maximal configurations lie in one slide-connected component
A slide is exactly the standard “matching reconfiguration” move: if \(u\) is the unmatched (hole) vertex and \(v\) is a neighbor matched to \(w\), then replacing the matching edge \(vw\) by \(uv\) slides the domino and moves the unmatched vertex from \(u\) to \(w\).

For the \(n\times n\) grid with \(n\) odd, the underlying graph is highly connected and (crucially) has no structural separation that would trap the unique unmatched black vertex into different “matching components”; in matching-theoretic terms, the odd grid is an elementary bipartite graph with \(|B|=|W|+1\), and the graph of maximum matchings under these slide moves is connected. Concretely, one can always:
- move the hole (unmatched black square) along the connected graph of black squares (steps of length 2), and
- use local rearrangements (realizable by sequences of slides) to adjust domino placements as needed,

so any maximal configuration can be transformed into any other by a sequence of slides. Therefore every configuration \(C\) can reach **all** maximal configurations, i.e. \(k(C)\) is the same constant for all \(C\).

### 3) Number of possible values of \(k(C)\)
Since \(k(C)\) is constant over all \(C\), it takes exactly one value.

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
d99e84e85cc815445381b853fb76d6fde69c5200:
Color the \(n\times n\) board like a chessboard. Since \(n=10001\) is odd, one color (say black) has
\[
\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}
\]
squares and the other (white) has \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\).

In any maximal configuration \(C\), there are \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\) dominoes, hence exactly \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\) black squares and \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\) white squares are covered. Therefore the uncovered square must always be black. A slide moves the uncovered square from one black square to another black square.

Now we show two facts.

**(1) Upper bound on \(k(C)\).**  
From any configuration, every reachable configuration has its unique uncovered square on a black square. Moreover, from a fixed starting configuration, the sliding process determines the domino positions uniquely once the uncovered square is fixed (because each slide simply shifts one domino into the hole, so the “history” is forced). Hence, within the reachable set, there is at most one configuration for each possible uncovered black square. Therefore
\[
k(C)\le \#\{\text{black squares}\}=\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}.
\]

**(2) All values \(1,2,\dots,\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\) occur.**  
For any integer \(m\) with \(1\le m\le \frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\), construct a configuration in which:
- all dominoes outside a chosen \(1\)-cell-wide “snake corridor” are frozen (dominoes packed so the hole cannot interact with them), and
- inside the corridor, the dominoes are forced so that the hole can move along exactly \(m\) black squares, and each such hole position corresponds to exactly one maximal configuration.
